2. **Pitched Roof** — Slope > 10°; gable, hip, or mansard shapes.
   - *Construction:* Truss + rafter + tile/sheet cladding.
   - *Suitability for Kerala:* **Highly suitable** due to heavy rainfall (≥ 3000 mm/yr in Idukki). Excellent drainage.

3. **Curved / Shell Roof** — Dome or barrel vault.
   - *Construction:* RCC shell, complex formwork.
   - *Suitability for Kerala:* Limited use; expensive for residential but iconic for public buildings.

4. **Steel Truss Roof (PEB)** — Pre-engineered steel frame.
   - *Construction:* Factory fabrication + bolted site assembly.
   - *Suitability for Kerala:* Growing popularity for industrial sheds and warehouses; resistant to cyclonic wind uplift.

**Conclusion:** For Kerala's **tropical monsoon climate with cyclonic exposure**, **pitched tile roofs** and **steel PEB roofs** are the most safety-efficient choices.

**(b)** Calculate the total factored load on a flat RCC roof slab of thickness 120 mm located in coastal Kerala. Use IS 875 recommended values. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Slab thickness, $t = 120\,mm = 0.12\,m$
- Density of RCC, $\rho = 25\,kN/m^{3}$
- Live load (roof not accessible), $L.L. = 0.75\,kN/m^{2}$
- Wind uplift coefficient, $C_p = -0.8$ (flat roof corners, IS 875 Part 3)
- Dynamic wind pressure (Kerala coast, V = 39 m/s):

$$
q_z = 0.6 \cdot V^{2} = 0.6 \cdot (39)^{2} = 0.6 \cdot 1521 = 912.6\,N/m^{2} = 0.913\,kN/m^{2}
$$

**Step 1 — Dead Load**

$$
D.L. = 25 \times 0.12 = 3.0\,kN/m^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Wind Uplift**

$$
W.L. = C_p \cdot q_z = (-0.8) \times 0.913 = -0.73\,kN/m^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Working Load**

$$
\text{Total} = 3.0 + 0.75 + \vert -0.73 \vert = 4.48\,kN/m^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Factored Load (FoS = 1.5)**

$$
\text{Factored Load} = 4.48 \times 1.5 = 6.72\,kN/m^{2}
$$

**Conclusion:** The slab must be designed for **6.72 kN/m²** to ensure safety against gravity load and wind uplift.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Set
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted Style]**

**(a)** Discuss the fall protection systems used during roof construction. Mention the relevant IS codes. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Fall protection is mandated whenever a worker operates at a height of **1.8 m or more** above ground (as per Factories Act 1948, Section 32, and KTU/IS 3696). The three-tier hierarchy of fall protection is:

1. **Elimination / Prevention** — Design the work to be done at ground level (e.g., prefabricated trusses lifted into place). This is the **best** level.

2. **Passive Protection** — Physical barriers that do not require worker action:
   - **Guard rails** (top rail at 1.0 m, mid-rail at 0.5 m, toe board ≥ 150 mm) — IS 3696.
   - **Safety nets** — IS 11057.
   - **Catch platforms** — IS 4014.

3. **Active Protection** — Worker-attached systems:
   - **Full-body harness** + **lanyard** + **anchor point** rated for **22.2 kN** (OSHA/EN 361).
   - **Retractable lifeline** for vertical movement.
   - **Horizontal lifeline** for long roof edges.

**Relevant IS Codes:**
- **IS 3696** — Scaffolds and ladders.
- **IS 11057** — Industrial safety nets.
- **IS 3521** — Industrial safety belts and harnesses.
- **IS 4756** — Safety code for construction work.

**(b)** A roof truss has a span of 10 m and a rise of 2.5 m. Determine (i) the pitch, (ii) the roof angle, and (iii) the rafter length. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Span, $L = 10\,m$
- Rise, $R = 2.5\,m$
- Run, $r = L/2 = 5\,m$

**(i) Pitch:**

$$
\text{Pitch} = \dfrac{Rise}{Run} = \dfrac{2.5}{5} = 0.5 = \dfrac{1}{2}
$$

> *Pitch is therefore 1:2 (i.e., a moderate-slope roof).*

**(ii) Roof Angle:**

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.5) \approx 26.57^{\circ}
$$

**(iii) Rafter Length:**

$$
\text{Rafter Length} = \dfrac{r}{\cos\theta} = \dfrac{5}{\cos(26.57^{\circ})} = \dfrac{5}{0.8944} \approx 5.59\,m
$$

**Conclusion:** With a pitch of **1:2** and rafter length **5.59 m**, this is a typical Kerala-style residential tile roof requiring a 5.6 m working platform on the scaffold.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in Roof Construction Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Pitch and Angle** — Pitch is a ratio (dimensionless), angle is in degrees. Examiners deduct 1 mark if units are missing.
> 2. **Forgetting Wind Uplift Sign** — Wind acts *upward* on flat roofs. Always use **negative C_p** for uplift. Wrong sign = 2 marks lost.
> 3. **Not Applying Factor of Safety** — Board questions explicitly ask for "factored load." Forgetting to multiply by **1.5** = 2 marks lost.
> 4. **Confusing Run and Span** — Run = Span ÷ 2. Writing Run = Span = full marks deducted.
> 5. **Skipping IS Code References** — KTU examiners award 1 bonus mark for correctly citing **IS 875 / IS 3696 / IS 277**. Always include at least one code.
> 6. **No Sketch in Descriptive Answers** — Even a **textual diagram** (ASCII) is mandatory. No sketch = up to 2 marks lost.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Roof Construction (Module 2, PESFT526)**

- [x] **Roof = structural + weatherproof + safety system**, not just a covering.
- [x] **Pitch = Rise/Run** (e.g., 1:2). Angle = $\tan^{-1}(\text{Rise}/\text{Run})$.
- [x] **Three roof types for KTU:** Flat (RCC), Pitched (Tile/Sheet), Curved (Shell). Kerala favours **pitched** for rain drainage.
- [x] **Dead Load of slab** = $\rho \times t$ where $\rho_{RCC} = 25\,kN/m^{3}$.
- [x] **Live Load** on inaccessible roof = **0.75 kN/m²** (IS 875 Part 2).
- [x] **Wind Load** = $C_p \cdot q_z$; for flat roofs, $C_p$ is **negative** (uplift).
- [x] **Dynamic wind pressure** $q_z = 0.6 \cdot V^2$; Kerala $V_b = 39\,m/s$.
- [x] **Factor of Safety** for roof design = **1.5** (working stress method).
- [x] **Bending moment** of simply supported slab = $wL^{2}/8$.
- [x] **Rafter length** = $\text{Run}/\cos\theta$.
- [x] **Fall protection mandatory** at height ≥ **1.8 m** (Factories Act 1948).
- [x] **Key IS Codes:** IS 875 (Loads), IS 3696 (Scaffolds), IS 277 (Sheets), IS 1911 (Lifting), IS 3521 (Harness).
- [x] **Three-tier fall protection:** Elimination → Passive (rails/nets) → Active (harness).
- [x] **Critical KTU safety hazards:** Fall from height, falling objects, structural collapse, electrical shock, heat stress.
- [x] **Kerala-specific safety note:** Roofs must be designed for **cyclonic wind uplift** and **monsoon water ponding**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Erection of Concrete Framed Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT526 - SAFETY IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Erection of Concrete Framed Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Erection of Concrete Framed Structures** refers to the systematic, sequenced process of assembling, supporting, pouring, and stabilizing the load-bearing skeleton of a building—comprising columns, beams, slabs, shear walls, and roof decks—using either **Cast-in-Place (CIP)** reinforced/prestressed concrete or **Precast Concrete Elements**, executed in strict compliance with occupational safety, structural integrity, and statutory regulations such as **IS 14687**, **IS 7205**, **OSHA 29 CFR Part 1926 Subpart Q**, and the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the Safety in Construction curriculum, "erection" is treated not merely as a construction activity, but as a *Hazard-Risk Controlled Process* where each operational phase (formwork, lifting, placing, shoring, stripping) is mapped against a corresponding **Job Safety Analysis (JSA)** and **Permit-to-Work (PTW)** system.

### 1.2 Classification of Concrete Framed Structures

| S.No. | Type | Method | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cast-in-Place (CIP) / Monolithic** | Concrete poured on-site inside formwork | High-rise buildings, bridges, dams |
| 2 | **Precast Concrete** | Elements cast off-site, lifted & assembled | Industrial sheds, parking structures, mass housing |
| 3 | **Hybrid (Semi-Precast)** | Combination of precast beams/slabs + CIP columns | Stadiums, commercial complexes |
| 4 | **Prestressed Concrete Frame** | High-strength steel tendons tensioned pre/post-pour | Long-span roofs, bridges, decks |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Skeleton of a Giant"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine building the human skeleton inside a living body. You cannot just "drop in" the bones—they must be **temporarily supported (scaffolding & formwork)**, **aligned (plumb & level checks)**, **connected (reinforcement tying & splicing)**, and finally **filled with life (concrete pour)**. The "muscles" (facades, partitions) come later. Any mistake in the skeleton (frame) is **fatal** to the structure. Concrete frame erection follows the same logic—**temporary works are as critical as permanent works** because the wet concrete cannot support itself until it cures to its design strength (typically **M25 = 25 N/mm²** at 28 days).

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Design Metrics

- **Unit weight of reinforced concrete:** **25 kN/m³**
- **Unit weight of plain concrete:** **24 kN/m³**
- **Fresh concrete pressure on formwork:** Up to **~100 kN/m²** (for full hydrostatic head at 1.2 m pour rate)
- **Minimum curing period:** **7 to 14 days** (for OPC), **10 to 14 days** (for blended cement)
- **Safe stripping time for formwork:**
  - Vertical formwork (sides of columns/beams): **24 to 48 hours**
  - Soffit formwork (slab bottom): **3 to 7 days** (props retained)
  - Props for spans > 4.5 m: **14 to 21 days**
- **Standard PPE for erection crew:** Hard hat (Type II, ANSI Z89.1), safety harness (full-body, Class III), steel-toe boots, gloves, high-visibility vest, goggles.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Sequence (Phase-wise Breakdown)

> The erection of a concrete framed structure is divided into **Six Critical Phases**, each governed by a distinct hazard profile.

#### **Phase 1: Site Preparation & Mobilization**

- Site clearance, marking grid lines using **Total Station** or **GPS** (tolerance: **±5 mm** for columns).
- Mobilization of tower crane, batching plant, and formwork system.
- *Hazard:* Collisions between mobile plant and workers.

#### **Phase 2: Reinforcement Erection (Rebar Work)**

- Fixing of rebar cages for footings, columns, beams, and slabs.
- Binding wire (16 SWG) tying with standard "Figure-8" or "Wrap-and-Saddle" methods.
- Spacer blocks (cover blocks) of **25 mm / 40 mm / 50 mm** used to maintain concrete cover.
- *Hazard:* Cuts, punctures, falling rebars (sticking-out bars are **bent or capped**).

#### **Phase 3: Formwork Assembly (Temporary Works)**

- Formwork is the **mold** that gives shape to the concrete. It must resist:
  - Dead load of wet concrete (**24–25 kN/m³**)
  - Live load of workers, equipment (**≥ 1.5 kN/m²** per IS 14687)
  - Hydrostatic pressure of fresh concrete
  - Vibrator loads (**1.0 kN/m²** minimum)

| Formwork System | Load Capacity | Reuse Cycles | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Timber (traditional) | Low | 3–5 | Low |
| Plywood + Steel Props | Medium | 10–20 | Medium |
| Aluminum Alloy | High | 200+ | High |
| Steel (Modular) | Very High | 500+ | Very High |
| Plastic / FRP | Medium | 80+ | High |

#### **Phase 4: Concrete Pouring & Vibration**

- Concrete is delivered via **boom placer, crane bucket, or concrete pump**.
- Maximum free-fall height: **1.5 m** (to prevent segregation of aggregates).
- Vibration by **needle vibrator (40–50 mm dia.)** at spacing of **~8× the diameter** of the needle.

#### **Phase 5: Curing & De-shuttering**

- Curing methods: water ponding, wet hessian, curing compound, steam curing.
- De-shuttering sequence strictly follows design removal loads.

#### **Phase 6: Reshoring & Back-propping**

- **Reshores** are placed after stripping to carry the construction loads of upper floors that are not yet self-supporting.
- Number of reshored floors = **construction live load / 28-day strength ratio** (typically 2 to 3 floors below).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Critical Equations)

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Standard/Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_c = \gamma_c \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure of fresh concrete on formwork | $\gamma_c = 24\text{ kN/m}^3$, $h$ = pour height (m), $P_c$ in kN/m² |
| 2 | $q_{total} = q_{DL} + q_{LL} + q_{vib}$ | Total design load on formwork | $q_{LL} \ge 1.5\text{ kN/m}^2$, $q_{vib} = 1.0\text{ kN/m}^2$ (IS 14687) |
| 3 | $M_{allow} = \sigma_{bend} \cdot Z$ | Bending moment capacity of formwork member | $\sigma_{bend}$ for timber = $10\text{ N/mm}^2$ |
| 4 | $f_s = \frac{N}{A_{net}}$ | Axial stress in prop/shore | IS 806 code, $f_s$ in N/mm² |
| 5 | $WL_{rig} = \frac{W \cdot L}{S \cdot n}$ | Rigging load on lifting sling (vertical) | $W$ = weight, $L$ = sling length, $S$ = vertical height, $n$ = number of legs |
| 6 | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{S}{L}\right)$ | Sling angle from horizontal | Angle $\le 60°$ recommended |
| 7 | $t_{strip} = f(T, C, R)$ | Minimum stripping time function | $T$ = temp, $C$ = cement type, $R$ = required strength ratio |
| 8 | $\sigma_{c,28} = k \cdot \sigma_{c,7}$ | Concrete strength gain relationship | $k \approx 1.33$ for OPC, $1.5$ for rapid-hardening |
| 9 | $S_{vib,max} = 8 \cdot d_{needle}$ | Max. spacing of vibrator insertions | $d_{needle}$ typically 40–60 mm |
| 10 | $h_{fall,max} = 1.5\text{ m}$ | Max. free-fall height of concrete | IS 456:2000, Clause 13.2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the basis of **Formwork Design Drawings (FDD)**, **Lift Plan Studies**, and **Critical Path Method (CPM)** scheduling on KTU real-time project sites. A 5% error in formwork design load estimation can lead to **collapse of a 100 m² slab** during pour, causing fatalities.

### 2.3 Hazard-to-Control Mapping (Theory)

The "Why-How" of each step is grounded in the **Hierarchy of Controls**:

1. **Elimination** → Prefabricate off-site to reduce on-site work at height.
2. **Substitution** → Use self-climbing formwork instead of manual climbing.
3. **Engineering Controls** → Guardrails, safety nets, perimeter screens, edge protection.
4. **Administrative Controls** → PTW system, JSA briefing, toolbox talk, exclusion zones.
5. **PPE** → Last line of defense—harness anchored to independent lifeline.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Fresh Concrete Pressure on Formwork

**Problem Setup:** A column of cross-section **300 mm × 450 mm** is being poured at a rate of **1.5 m/hour** using a concrete mix of unit weight **24 kN/m³**. Determine the lateral pressure on the formwork.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Governing Condition (IS 14687):** If the rate of placement $R \le 7\text{ m/hr}$ and slump $\le 100\text{ mm}$, the formwork may be designed for the full **hydrostatic head** of freshly placed concrete.

**Step 1: Compute the depth of freshly placed concrete in 1 hour.**

$$
h = R \cdot t = 1.5 \cdot 1 = 1.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the hydrostatic pressure equation.**

$$
P_c = \gamma_c \cdot h
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values.**

$$
P_c = 24 \text{ kN/m}^3 \cdot 1.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate.**

$$
P_c = 36.0 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 5: Apply the vibration surcharge (add 1.0 kN/m² per IS 14687 for needle vibrator).**

$$
P_{design} = 36.0 + 1.0 = 37.0 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Result:** The formwork must be designed to resist a lateral pressure of **37.0 kN/m²**.

> **Conversion Logic:** The hydrostatic analogy treats wet concrete as a dense fluid; the vibrator surcharge accounts for the transient thixotropic liquefaction that momentarily increases effective pressure on the form face.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Sling Tension and Rigging Geometry

**Problem Setup:** A precast beam weighing **W = 40 kN** is to be lifted using a **2-leg sling** with horizontal span **L = 6 m** and vertical hook height **S = 4 m**. Determine the tension in each sling leg and the safe sling angle.

**Step 1: Compute the sling angle from horizontal.**

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{S}{L/2}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the angle.**

$$
\theta = 53.13^{\circ}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the tension in each leg using the sling tension formula.**

$$
T = \frac{W}{2 \cdot \sin(\theta)}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute.**

$$
T = \frac{40}{2 \cdot \sin(53.13^{\circ})} = \frac{40}{2 \cdot 0.8} = \frac{40}{1.6}
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate.**

$$
T = 25.0 \text{ kN per leg}
$$

**Step 6: Apply the safety factor (SF = 5 for wire rope slings per IS 2769).**

$$
T_{design} = T \cdot SF = 25.0 \cdot 5 = 125.0 \text{ kN}
$$

**Result:** Each sling leg must be rated for at least **125 kN WLL (Working Load Limit)**.

> **Conversion Logic:** As sling angle decreases (sling becomes more horizontal), the angle $\theta$ approaches zero, $\sin(\theta) \to 0$, and the tension $T \to \infty$. This is why **sling angles below 30° are forbidden**—a near-horizontal sling multiplies the load 4× to 10×.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Stripping Time Based on Concrete Strength Gain

**Problem Setup:** A slab of grade **M25** is to be stripped. Using **Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC)** and a site temperature of **25°C**, estimate the stripping time if **50% of the 28-day strength** is required for safe stripping.

**Step 1: Apply the strength gain ratio.**

$$
\sigma_{c,\,t} = 0.50 \cdot \sigma_{c,\,28}
$$

**Step 2: For OPC at 25°C, the 7-day strength is typically 66% of 28-day strength (Plowman’s empirical relation).**

$$
\sigma_{c,\,7} = 0.66 \cdot \sigma_{c,\,28}
$$

**Step 3: Compare required vs. 7-day strength.**

$$
0.50 \cdot \sigma_{c,\,28} \le 0.66 \cdot \sigma_{c,\,28} \quad \Rightarrow \text{OK, stripping feasible at 7 days.}
$$

**Step 4: For a 14-day strip with **Pozzolana Portland Cement (PPC)**:**

$$
\sigma_{c,\,14} \approx 0.70 \cdot \sigma_{c,\,28} > 0.50 \cdot \sigma_{c,\,28} \quad \Rightarrow \text{OK at 14 days for PPC.}
$$

**Result:** With OPC, slab soffit formwork may be stripped at **7 days**, but props must be retained for an additional 7–14 days.

> **Conversion Logic:** The strength-vs-time curve is exponential, not linear. Early-age strength is sensitive to ambient temperature; below 10°C, gain rate halves, requiring **steam curing** or maturity-based stripping tests.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hazard → Cause → Control (HCC)

| Activity | Hazard | Cause | Engineering Control | Administrative Control | PPE |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rebar tying at height | Fall from edge | Unprotected floor opening | Edge guardrail, safety net | PTW, hole cover registry | Harness |
| Formwork stripping | Falling panels | Premature stripping | Strength test, sequential strip | Stripping permit | Helmet, boots |
| Concrete pumping | Pipe burst | Blockage, high pressure | Pressure relief valve, anchor at bends | Trained pump operator | Goggles, gloves |
| Crane lift | Falling load | Sling failure, swing | Tag lines, exclusion zone | Certified rigger, lift plan | Helmet, vest |
| Column pour | Formwork blowout | Insufficient bracing | Through-bolts, waler beams | Continuous pour supervision | Harness |
| Slab vibration | Hand-arm vibration | Prolonged use | Auto-rotating vibrators | Rotation of operators | Anti-vibration gloves |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram: Concrete Frame Erection Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Site Preparation and Grid Marking] --> B[Foundation Rebar and Concreting]
    B --> C[Column Rebar Erection]
    C --> D[Column Formwork Assembly]
    D --> E[Column Concrete Pour]
    E --> F[Beam and Slab Formwork]
    F --> G[Beam and Slab Rebar Placement]
    G --> H[MEP Sleeves and Inserts]
    H --> I[Slab Concrete Pour]
    I --> J[Curing 7 to 14 Days]
    J --> K[Formwork Stripping]
    K --> L[Reshoring and Back-propping]
    L --> M[Repeat for Next Floor]
    M --> A

    A1[Permit to Work] -.-> A
    B1[Soil Test Report] -.-> B
    C1[Fall Protection Plan] -.-> C
    D1[Formwork Design Check] -.-> D
    E1[Slump and Cube Test] -.-> E
    I1[Pump Line Anchor Check] -.-> I
    J1[Curing Log Register] -.-> J
    K1[Strength Test Certificate] -.-> K
```

**Interpretation:** This flowchart shows the **vertical cycle** of constructing a floor, with safety documents and quality checks (dashed lines) gating each phase. Each step is a *Hold Point*—the next step cannot begin without the previous safety/quality control.

---

### 4.2 Block Architecture: Formwork Safety System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Design Load] --> B1[Hydrostatic Pressure]
    A1 --> C1[Live Load]
    A1 --> D1[Vibration Load]
    A1 --> E1[Wind Load]
    B1 --> F1[Formwork Member Sizing]
    C1 --> F1
    D1 --> F1
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Prop and Shore Design]
    F1 --> H1[Waler and Tie System]
    F1 --> I1[Ply Sheathing Check]
    G1 --> J1[Site Inspection Register]
    H1 --> J1
    I1 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[Pour Approval Permit]
    K1 --> L1[Concrete Placement]
    L1 --> M1[Curing and Stripping]
```

**Interpretation:** The formwork is engineered as an **integrated load path**: each input load (block) flows through the design chain (F1) into the three structural subsystems (G1, H1, I1), which together form the **approved pour** (K1). Any failure at the inspection register (J1) blocks the pour.

---

### 4.3 Precast Element Lift Plan Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Lift Plan Document] --> P2[Element Weight Verification]
    P2 --> P3[Crane Selection by Chart]
    P3 --> P4[Sling and Shackle Selection]
    P4 --> P5[Rigger and Signaller Assignment]
    P5 --> P6[Exclusion Zone Barricade]
    P6 --> P7[Trial Lift at 300 mm]
    P7 --> P8{Stable?}
    P8 -- No --> P9[Re-rig and Reassess]
    P9 --> P2
    P8 -- Yes --> P10[Full Lift and Place]
    P10 --> P11[Plumb and Align]
    P11 --> P12[Permanent Bracing]
    P12 --> P13[Sling Release and De-rig]
```

**Interpretation:** The "trial lift" at **300 mm above ground** is a **mandatory safety stop** to verify rigging balance, sling integrity, and load control before the full vertical lift. This corresponds to **OSHA 1926.1404** and **IS 13367** requirements.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. Define the term "Formwork" and list any two types of formwork used in concrete construction.**

**Model Answer:**
Formwork is the temporary mold or structural assembly used to support and shape freshly placed concrete until it gains sufficient strength to be self-supporting. Types: (i) **Timber formwork** (traditional, low cost, 3–5 reuses), (ii) **Steel modular formwork** (high reuse, accurate dimensions, used in high-rise CIP construction), (iii) Aluminum formwork (lightweight, 200+ reuses), (iv) Plywood formwork (smooth finish).

**Q2. State the importance of "Permit-to-Work (PTW)" system in concrete frame erection.**

**Model Answer:**
The PTW is a **formal written authorization** that defines the scope, hazards, controls, and validity period of a high-risk activity (e.g., hot work, lifting, working at height). It ensures that:
1. Hazards are identified and **mitigated before work starts**.
2. **Competency** of workers is verified (e.g., certified rigger for lifting).
3. There is a **single point of accountability** (Permit Issuer).
4. It serves as a **legal record** for incident investigation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **phases of concrete frame erection** for a multi-storey building, highlighting the safety hazards and control measures at each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A column formwork of dimensions **400 mm × 600 mm × 3.0 m height** is to be designed. The rate of concrete pour is **1.8 m/hr**, and a needle vibrator will be used. Calculate the design lateral pressure on the formwork as per **IS 14687**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The six phases of concrete frame erection are:

1. **Site Preparation & Mobilization:** Layout, grid marking, crane erection. *Hazard:* Struck-by mobile plant. *Control:* Barricading, traffic plan, banksman with radio.

2. **Reinforcement Erection:** Fixing rebar cages, spacers, laps. *Hazard:* Cuts, falling rebar. *Control:* Rebar caps, gloves, covered storage.

3. **Formwork Assembly:** Erecting columns, beams, slab formwork. *Hazard:* Collapse during pour. *Control:* Engineered FDD, prop inspection, certified carpenters.

4. **Concrete Pour & Vibration:** Using pump or crane bucket. *Hazard:* Pipe burst, segregation, blowout. *Control:* Pressure relief, slump test, restricted entry zone.

5. **Curing & De-shuttering:** Water curing, sequential stripping. *Hazard:* Falling panels. *Control:* Stripping permit, barricaded area, trained workers.

6. **Reshoring & Back-propping:** Re-propping lower floors. *Hazard:* Floor collapse. *Control:* Reshoring drawing, load distribution check.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Hydrostatic head for 1 hour of pour.**

$$
h = R \cdot t = 1.8 \cdot 1.0 = 1.8 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Concrete pressure (hydrostatic, since R < 7 m/hr).**

$$
P_c = \gamma_c \cdot h = 24 \cdot 1.8 = 43.2 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Vibrator surcharge (per IS 14687).**

$$
q_{vib} = 1.0 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Design pressure.**

$$
P_{design} = 43.2 + 1.0 = 44.2 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Result:** The column formwork must be designed for a lateral pressure of **44.2 kN/m²**, which governs the waler spacing and tie-rod design.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice*

**(a)** Describe the **safe methods of lifting, placing, and aligning precast concrete elements** during frame erection. Include sling angle considerations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A precast column weighing **W = 60 kN** is to be lifted using a **2-leg wire rope sling** with a horizontal span of **8 m** and a vertical hook height of **3 m**. Compute: (i) the sling angle, (ii) the tension in each leg, and (iii) the required WLL of each sling with a safety factor of **5**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

Safe precast lifting involves:
- **Pre-lift:** Approved Lift Plan, weight check, sling inspection, exclusion zone.
- **Sling angle:** Always **≥ 60°** from horizontal (preferably 90° for vertical lift).
- **Tag lines:** Used to control swing; never ride the load.
- **Trial lift:** Hoist 300 mm, hold 1 minute, check balance.
- **Placing & Aligning:** Use **two theodolites** on perpendicular faces, check plumb, install temporary bracings before sling release.
- **Permanent Fixing:** Grout pockets, weld plates, bolt connections as per drawing.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Sling angle.**

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{S}{L/2}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{4}\right) = 36.87^{\circ}
$$

**Step 2: Tension in each leg.**

$$
T = \frac{W}{2 \sin(\theta)} = \frac{60}{2 \cdot 0.6} = \frac{60}{1.2} = 50.0 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3: Required WLL with SF = 5.**

$$
WLL = T \cdot SF = 50.0 \cdot 5 = 250.0 \text{ kN}
$$

**Result:** The sling angle is **36.87° (below the safe 60° limit — engineer should reconsider geometry)**, each leg carries **50 kN**, and the sling must have a **WLL of 250 kN**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — DO NOT LOSE THESE MARKS:**
> 1. Forgetting to **add the 1.0 kN/m² vibration surcharge** on formwork pressure problems (deduct 2 marks).
> 2. Computing sling angle as $S/L$ (whole span) instead of $S/(L/2)$ (half span) — this **halves the angle** and inflates tension by 2× (deduct 3 marks).
> 3. Omitting the **safety factor (SF)** in the WLL calculation (deduct 2 marks).
> 4. Failing to state the **safe sling angle limit (≥ 60°)** in descriptive answers (deduct 1 mark).
> 5. Confusing **de-shuttering** with **de-propping** — slab soffit forms are stripped first; props are retained.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **Concrete frame erection** = sequential, hazard-controlled process: Foundation → Columns → Beams/Slabs → Roof → Repeat.
- **Formwork** is *temporary works* but treated as a *primary structure* for design.
- **Hydrostatic pressure** governs formwork design when pour rate $\le 7 \text{ m/hr}$.
- Always add **1.0 kN/m² vibration surcharge** to concrete pressure.
- **Sling angle $\ge 60°$** from horizontal — never lift with near-horizontal slings.
- **Stripping time** depends on cement type, temperature, and required strength ratio (50% for slabs, 70% for beams, 100% for cantilevers).
- **Reshoring** must retain **2 to 3 floors** below the active pour to share construction loads.
- **Curing period** is **7 to 14 days minimum**; do not walk on slabs before 48 hours.
- **PPE** for every worker: helmet, boots, gloves, harness (above 1.8 m height), vest.
- **Permit-to-Work (PTW)** is mandatory for all high-risk activities: lifting, hot work, confined space, working at height.
- **Lateral pressure on formwork** = $\gamma_c \cdot h + 1.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$ (vibration).
- **Sling tension** = $W / (n \cdot \sin\theta)$; **WLL** = $T \cdot SF$ (SF = 5 for wire rope).
- **Standard codes:** IS 456, IS 14687 (formwork), IS 2750, IS 13367 (lifting), IS 7205, OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart Q.
- **Concrete unit weight:** 25 kN/m³ (RCC), 24 kN/m³ (PCC).
- **Free-fall limit:** Concrete must not free-fall more than **1.5 m**.
- **Vibrator spacing:** 8× needle diameter (max), inserted vertically.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Erection of Structural Steel Work
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT526 - SAFETY IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Erection of Structural Steel Work

# Module 2 — Erection of Structural Steel Work

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Erection of Structural Steel Work** is the process of assembling, lifting, positioning, aligning, bolting, and welding prefabricated steel members (beams, columns, trusses, gusset plates) at the construction site to form a stable, load-bearing structural frame, in strict accordance with the approved erection drawings, method statements, and safety codes (IS 7205, IS 800, OSHA 1926 Subpart R, IS 15988).

In simpler words — structural steel erection is the *“steel skeleton assembly line in the sky”*. Pre-cut, pre-drilled, pre-welded steel pieces are brought to the site from the fabrication yard and then hoisted (lifted by cranes), aligned, and permanently joined **on-site, often at dangerous heights**, to form the structural skeleton of a building, an industrial shed, a bridge, or a stadium roof.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Giant Toy Building Blocks" View
Imagine a child assembling a giant Meccano / Lego set, but:
- The pieces weigh **tonnes** (a single column can weigh **5,000–50,000 kg**)
- The work happens at heights of **30 m, 60 m, even 100+ m**
- A single mistake can collapse the entire frame
- There is **no second chance** — gravity always wins

Hence, safety in steel erection is not an "extra" — it is the **core operational requirement** that makes the activity physically possible.

### Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU Examinations
> [!NOTE]
> Steel erection is one of the **"Fatal Four"** construction hazards in OSHA statistics. The KTU examiner (per 2024 scheme ESE pattern) frequently tests:
> 1. Definitions of erection terms (Falsework, Guying, Tag Line, etc.)
> 2. Safety hazards unique to steel erection
> 3. OSHA Subpart R / IS 7205 control measures
> 4. Crane and rigging safety
> 5. Fall protection systems (PFAS)

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
| Parameter | Standard Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Standard gravity | **9.81 m/s² (≈ 10 m/s²)** | SI / IS Standards |
| Wind speed for erection stop | **> 50 km/h (≈ 13.9 m/s)** gust | IS 7205:1974 |
| Lightning stop-work distance | **Within 8 km of storm** | OSHA / NFPA |
| Minimum fall arrest trigger height | **1.8 m (6 ft)** | OSHA 1926.501 |
| Safety net drop test load | **220 kg from 7.5 m** | IS 11972 |
| Standard bolt torque (M20, 8.8 grade) | **≈ 478 N·m** | IS 4000 |
| Typical riggers' hand signal distance | **Within visual range, ≤ 30 m ideal** | OSHA 1926.1419 |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Phases of Structural Steel Erection

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Explain the sequence of erection of structural steel work"* (7–14 marks). Memorize this **5-phase logic flow**:

**Phase 1 — Pre-Erection Planning & Site Preparation**
- Review **erection drawings (E-drawings)**, **method statement**, **JSA (Job Safety Analysis)**, and **lifting plan**
- Verify foundation bolts, anchor bolts, and base plates are at correct **±5 mm tolerance**
- Create **hard stand zones** for crane outriggers
- Mark **exclusion zone** (radius = 1.5 × boom radius)
- Conduct **tool-box talk** every morning before any lifting

**Phase 2 — Equipment Mobilization & Rigging Setup**
- Position **crawler crane / tower crane / hydraulic crane** as per lift plan
- Attach **counterweights** and verify load chart
- Inspect **slings (wire rope, synthetic web, chain)**, **shackles**, **spreader beams**, and **lifting beams**
- Each sling leg must have a **Safety Factor (SF) ≥ 5** for personnel lifts and **SF ≥ 3** for material lifts

**Phase 3 — Lifting, Hoisting & Positioning**
- Use **tag lines** (minimum **2 tag lines, each ≥ 10 mm manila rope or 12 mm polypropylene**) to control swing
- Crane operator follows only **ONE designated rigger** (using standard **hand signals**)
- Lift member **slightly off ground (≈ 300 mm)**, **dwell for 10 seconds** to check stability, then continue hoist

**Phase 4 — Alignment, Plumbing & Temporary Bracing**
- Members are **plumbed (vertical alignment) using theodolite or plumb bob**
- **At least TWO bolts tightened (snug-tight)** at every connection **before releasing the crane**
- Install **temporary bracing** (cable guys, pipe braces) to resist lateral loads from wind or accidental impact
- **NEVER release the crane** until at least **50% of final bolts are snug-tight** for trusses, and **100% snug-tight** for cantilevers

**Phase 5 — Final Bolting, Welding, & Inspection**
- Replace snug-tight bolts with **pre-tensioned (TC) or slip-critical (SC) bolts** using calibrated **torque wrench / tension calibrator**
- Perform **NDT (Non-Destructive Testing)** — UT, MPI, DPI — on welds
- Remove temporary bracing **only after permanent structure is stable**
- Sign-off by **structural engineer of record**

### 2.2 Critical Hazards in Steel Erection (The "Fatal Six" — must memorize)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per OSHA 1926 Subpart R, the six leading causes of fatalities in steel erection are:
> 1. **Falls from height** (≈ 33% of deaths)
> 2. **Struck-by falling objects** (≈ 22%)
> 3. **Crane / equipment collapse** (≈ 18%)
> 4. **Electrocution** (from overhead power lines)
> 5. **Structural collapse** (incomplete bracing)
> 6. **Caught-in / between** (rigging failures)

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = m \cdot g$ | Weight of steel member (where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$) | N (Newtons) | Lift planning |
| 2 | $\text{SF} = \dfrac{F_{\text{break}}}{F_{\text{working}}}$ | Safety Factor of sling / shackle | dimensionless | Rigging design |
| 3 | $F_{\text{wind}} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot v^2 \cdot C_d \cdot A$ | Wind force on a member (drag) | N | Wind stop-work decision |
| 4 | $M_{\text{bolt}} = K \cdot d \cdot F$ | Bolt tightening torque (K = nut factor 0.16–0.20) | N·m | Bolted connection design |
| 5 | $L_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{rigid}}}{N_{\text{tag lines}}}$ | Effective swing length reduction with tag lines | m | Lifting geometry |
| 6 | $R_{\text{crane}} = \sqrt{(D_{\text{outrigger}})^2 + (h_{\text{slab}})^2}$ | Required crane set-up radius | m | Crane positioning |
| 7 | $\Delta_{\text{plumb}} \le \dfrac{H}{500}$ | Plumb tolerance for columns (H = column height) | m | Alignment check |
| 8 | $F_{\text{shock}} = F_{\text{static}} \cdot (1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2 \cdot h \cdot EA}{W \cdot L}})$ | Hoisting shock factor (vertical lift dynamics) | N | Dynamic sling load |
| 9 | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A} \le \tau_{\text{allow}}$ | Shear stress in sling (must be ≤ allowable) | N/mm² (MPa) | Sling selection |
| 10 | $P_{\text{anchor}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{uplift}}}{n \cdot \mu}$ | Anchor bolt pull-out capacity check | N | Base plate safety |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala-specific note (KTU Board Examiner Pattern):** When wind speed **exceeds 50 km/h** (≈ 13.9 m/s) all steel erection activities **MUST be suspended** — this is a 3-mark "guaranteed" question. Remember the unit conversion: $1 \text{ m/s} = 3.6 \text{ km/h}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- Used in **PEB (Pre-Engineered Buildings)** for warehouses, factories, aircraft hangars
- Applied in **high-rise composite construction** (steel + concrete)
- Forms the **primary frame** in **bridges, stadiums, transmission towers**
- Critical for **industrial plants** (refineries, power stations) where speed of construction matters

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why TWO Tag Lines Are Mandatory (Swing Control Geometry)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **favourite 7-mark derivation question** in KTU Module 2 examinations. The logic is shown below with full algebraic transparency.

**Step 1 — Define the free-swinging member problem.**
When a steel beam of length $L$ is lifted by a single crane hook, it behaves like a **physical pendulum**. The natural period of swing is given by:

$$T_{\text{swing}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{L_{\text{eff}}}{g}}$$

where $L_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective pendulum length (≈ distance from hook to centre of gravity of the member).

**Step 2 — Identify the hazard.**
Without control, even a small gust of wind or operator jerk causes **resonant swinging**, which:
- Can strike adjacent structures / workers
- Can pull the crane off its load chart limits
- Can cause **dynamic amplification** of the static load by a factor of **1.5 to 2.0**

**Step 3 — Quantify dynamic load amplification.**
The dynamic load experienced by the sling during a sudden swing stop is:

$$F_{\text{dyn}} = F_{\text{static}} \cdot \left( 1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2 \cdot h \cdot E \cdot A}{W \cdot L_{\text{eff}}}} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply numerical values for a typical 6 m beam (m = 800 kg).**
- Static weight: $W = m \cdot g = 800 \times 9.81 = 7848 \text{ N}$
- Beam length: $L = 6 \text{ m}$
- Effective pendulum length: $L_{\text{eff}} \approx 3 \text{ m}$ (rigid bar)
- Sling drop height: $h \approx 1.5 \text{ m}$
- Sling wire-rope $E \cdot A$ (assumed 18 mm dia, 6×19 construction): $E \cdot A \approx 110 \text{ kN} = 1.1 \times 10^5 \text{ N}$

**Step 5 — Substitute and solve.**

$$\dfrac{2 \cdot h \cdot E \cdot A}{W \cdot L_{\text{eff}}} = \dfrac{2 \times 1.5 \times 1.1 \times 10^5}{7848 \times 3} = \dfrac{3.3 \times 10^5}{23544} \approx 14.02$$

$$F_{\text{dyn}} = 7848 \cdot \left( 1 + \sqrt{1 + 14.02} \right) = 7848 \cdot \left( 1 + \sqrt{15.02} \right)$$

$$\sqrt{15.02} = 3.876$$

$$F_{\text{dyn}} = 7848 \cdot (1 + 3.876) = 7848 \cdot 4.876 \approx 38267 \text{ N} \approx 38.27 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the dynamic amplification factor (DAF).**

$$\text{DAF} = \dfrac{F_{\text{dyn}}}{F_{\text{static}}} = \dfrac{38267}{7848} \approx 4.88$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Without tag-line control, the sling load can become **nearly 5× the static weight**! A sling sized for static 8 kN will **fail catastrophically** under 38 kN. **Two tag lines (one at each end of the member, held by trained ground crew) reduce $L_{\text{eff}}$ to near zero**, eliminating the pendulum and the dynamic amplification.

### 3.2 Derivation: Wind Stop-Work Velocity

**Step 1 — Wind pressure on a flat surface (IS 875 / IS 7205 logic).**

$$P = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot v^2$$

where $\rho$ = air density ($\approx 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at sea level), $v$ = wind velocity in m/s.

**Step 2 — Drag force on a steel member.**

$$F_{\text{wind}} = C_d \cdot A \cdot P = 0.5 \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot \rho \cdot v^2$$

For a typical I-section girder, $C_d \approx 1.8$ (bluff body).

**Step 3 — Numerical check at v = 50 km/h.**
Convert: $v = 50 / 3.6 = 13.89 \text{ m/s}$.
Assume member area $A = 4 \text{ m}^2$ (8 m long × 0.5 m deep).

$$F_{\text{wind}} = 0.5 \times 1.8 \times 4 \times 1.225 \times (13.89)^2$$

$$F_{\text{wind}} = 0.5 \times 1.8 \times 4 \times 1.225 \times 192.93$$

$$F_{\text{wind}} = 0.5 \times 1.8 \times 4 \times 236.34 = 850.8 \text{ N} \approx 0.85 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Compare to member self-weight.**
If the same member weighs **2 tonnes (≈ 19.62 kN)**, then wind force is only **4.3%** of weight — *seems* safe.

**Step 5 — BUT, for a LIGHT truss (say 500 kg = 4.9 kN) at 18 m height:**

$$\dfrac{F_{\text{wind}}}{W} = \dfrac{0.85}{4.9} \approx 17.3\%$$

This **17% lateral push** on a slender truss can tip it during a vertical lift. Therefore, **erection must stop above 50 km/h**.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why IS 7205 specifies the **50 km/h limit** — it is not arbitrary; it keeps lateral wind force within a **safe-to-handle ratio** for the lightest members typically lifted.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bolt Torque Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Hot Question:** *"Calculate the torque required for an M20 bolt of grade 8.8."*

**Given:** Bolt = M20, grade 8.8 → $F_{\text{proof}} = 600 \text{ MPa}$, $F_{\text{ult}} = 800 \text{ MPa}$.
**Pre-tension force** (≈ 70% of proof load):

$$F_{p} = 0.7 \times A_{\text{net}} \times F_{\text{proof}}$$

For M20: $A_{\text{net}} \approx 245 \text{ mm}^2$.

$$F_{p} = 0.7 \times 245 \times 600 = 102900 \text{ N} \approx 103 \text{ kN}$$

**Torque equation (K = 0.20 for hot-dip galvanized, K = 0.16 for plain black bolts):**

$$T = K \cdot d \cdot F_{p}$$

**For K = 0.20:**

$$T = 0.20 \times 20 \times 102900 = 411600 \text{ N·mm} = 411.6 \text{ N·m}$$

**For K = 0.16:**

$$T = 0.16 \times 20 \times 102900 = 329280 \text{ N·mm} = 329.3 \text{ N·m}$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU board accepts **T ≈ 411 N·m (K = 0.20)** for general answer; mention the K value clearly in the solution to earn full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Erection Sequence — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Pre-Erection Planning] --> B[Review Drawings and Method Statement]
    B --> C[Verify Anchor Bolts and Foundations]
    C --> D[Conduct Toolbox Talk and JSA]
    D --> E[Set Up Crane and Outriggers]
    E --> F[Inspect Slings Shackles and Spreader Beam]
    F --> G[Lift Member to 300 mm and Dwell 10 s]
    G --> H{Stable and Balanced?}
    H -- No --> I[Lower and Re-rig Immediately]
    I --> G
    H -- Yes --> J[Hoist to Installation Height]
    J --> K[Align Using Tag Lines and Rigger Signals]
    K --> L[Insert Minimum Two Snug-Tight Bolts]
    L --> M[Release Crane Only After Bracing is Fixed]
    M --> N[Install Temporary Cable Guys and Braces]
    N --> O[Final Bolt Torquing or Site Welding]
    O --> P[NDT and Engineer Inspection]
    P --> Q[Remove Temporary Bracing]
    Q --> R[Sign-off and Move to Next Member]
    R --> S[End: All Members Erected]
```

### 4.2 Hazard → Control Mapping (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HR[HAZARD DOMAIN]
        H1[Fall from Height]
        H2[Struck by Falling Object]
        H3[Crane Collapse]
        H4[Electrocution from Power Line]
        H5[Structural Collapse during Lifting]
        H6[Slip and Trip at Height]
    end
    subgraph CR[CONTROL DOMAIN]
        C1[PFAS Anchor 5000 lb and Harness]
        C2[Exclusion Zone and Toe Boards]
        C3[Load Chart and Outrigger Pads]
        C4[Maintain 3 m Clearance from OHTL]
        C5[Temporary Bracing and Snug Bolts]
        C6[Debris Netting and Safe Access]
    end
    H1 --> C1
    H2 --> C2
    H3 --> C3
    H4 --> C4
    H5 --> C5
    H6 --> C6
    C1 --> R1[Reduce Fatality Risk by 65 percent]
    C2 --> R2[Reduce Struck-By Risk by 70 percent]
    C3 --> R3[Prevent Tip-Over of Crane]
    C4 --> R4[Prevent Arc-Flash and Burn]
    C5 --> R5[Ensure Frame Stability]
    C6 --> R6[Slip Resistance ISO Standard]
```

### 4.3 Typical Crane Set-Up Geometry (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph GS[GROUND SETUP]
        OS[Outrigger Pad 2 m x 2 m]
        CR[Crawler or Mobile Crane]
        OP[Operator Cabin]
    end
    subgraph BR[BOOM RIGGING]
        BM[Boom Length Lb]
        JK[Jib and Sheave Block]
        HG[Main Hook]
        SH[Spreader Beam if Multi-Point Lift]
    end
    subgraph LD[LOAD]
        MB[Steel Member Beam or Column]
        TG1[Tag Line End 1]
        TG2[Tag Line End 2]
    end
    OS --> CR
    CR --> BM
    BM --> JK
    JK --> HG
    HG --> SH
    SH --> MB
    MB --> TG1
    MB --> TG2
```

### 4.4 Fall Protection Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fall Hazard at Height above 1.8 m] --> B{Hierarchy of Controls}
    B --> C[Level 1: Eliminate Work at Height]
    B --> D[Level 2: Passive Protection Guardrails and Nets]
    B --> E[Level 3: Travel Restraint System]
    B --> F[Level 4: Personal Fall Arrest System PFAS]
    B --> G[Level 5: Administrative Warning Line and Safety Monitor]
    C --> H[Best Solution]
    D --> I[Highly Effective]
    E --> J[Effective]
    F --> K[Last Resort but Mandatory]
    G --> L[Limited Use Only]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note (for KTU answer scripts):** When asked to "draw a diagram of steel erection", a labelled **block-level flow diagram** (like Section 4.1) combined with a **sketch of crane + load + tag lines** (like Section 4.3) earns full marks. The examiner does NOT expect artistic engineering drawings — clarity of labels is what scores.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]
> **Define the term "Tag Line" as used in structural steel erection. State its safety significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **tag line** is a rope (typically **12 mm polypropylene or 10 mm manila**) attached to a steel member being lifted, used by ground personnel to **control swinging, spinning, and horizontal positioning** of the member during hoisting, *without* touching the load directly with hands. **Safety significance:** (1 mark) it prevents dynamic load amplification due to uncontrolled swing (DAF can reach 4–5× static load, causing sling failure), (1 mark) it keeps the load within the safe exclusion zone and prevents struck-by injuries to workers below, (1 mark) it enables precise alignment of the member with bolts/connections at height.

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B]
> **List any SIX major hazards specific to structural steel erection work.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 mark each):**
1. Fall of workers from height (during connecting bolts at column top)
2. Struck-by falling steel members or tools
3. Crane tip-over due to improper outrigger setup or overloading
4. Electrocution from overhead power lines (minimum **3 m** clearance required)
5. Structural collapse of partially erected frame due to inadequate temporary bracing
6. Rigging failure (broken sling, shackle failure)
7. *(Bonus — for full marks)* Slip on oily/greasy steel surfaces at height

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Compulsory]
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **sequential phases of structural steel erection** with safety precautions at each stage. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **fall protection systems** required during steel erection as per OSHA 1926 Subpart R / IS 7205. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Sequential Phases & Safety (7 marks):**

**Phase 1 — Pre-Erection Planning (1 mark):**
Review erection drawings (E-drawings), method statement, JSA, lift plan. Verify anchor bolts, base plates. Conduct **toolbox talk** every shift. Mark **exclusion zone** = 1.5 × boom radius.

**Phase 2 — Equipment Mobilization (1 mark):**
Crane selection per **load chart** (gross load = member weight + sling weight + shock factor). **Outrigger pads** (min 2 m × 2 m, on stable hard stand). Pre-lift crane inspection.

**Phase 3 — Lifting & Hoisting (2 marks):**
- Use **only certified slings** (visible colour code tag, valid 6-monthly inspection)
- Maintain **SF ≥ 5** for personnel-lift rigs, **SF ≥ 3** for material lifts
- **Dwell test** — lift 300 mm, hold 10 s, then hoist
- **Two tag lines** at opposite ends, never wrap around hands

**Phase 4 — Alignment & Temporary Bracing (2 marks):**
- **Plumb check** using theodolite (tolerance = H/500)
- Install **at least 2 snug-tight bolts per connection before releasing crane hook**
- Install **cable guys / pipe bracing** for lateral stability

**Phase 5 — Final Bolting, Welding & Sign-off (1 mark):**
Torque to design value (e.g., M20 8.8 → 411 N·m), NDT on welds, structural engineer sign-off, *then* remove temporary bracing.

**(b) Fall Protection Systems (7 marks):**

As per **OSHA 1926.760 / IS 15988**, fall protection is mandatory above **1.8 m (6 ft)**:

1. **Personal Fall Arrest System (PFAS)** — (2 marks)
   - Full-body harness (IS 3521)
   - Shock-absorbing lanyard (max free-fall 1.8 m, total arrest distance 5.5 m)
   - **Anchor point** capable of **22.2 kN (5,000 lb)** per worker attached

2. **Safety Net System** — (2 marks)
   - Installed within **7.5 m** of working level
   - Drop-tested with **220 kg sandbag**
   - Border rope minimum breaking strength 22.2 kN (IS 11972)

3. **Guardrail System** — (1.5 marks)
   - Top rail at **1.0–1.1 m**, mid-rail at **0.5 m**, toe board **≥ 100 mm**
   - Must withstand **890 N** horizontal load

4. **Controlled Access Zone (CAZ) & Warning Line System** — (1 mark)
   - Used in leading-edge work; line at **1.8–2.4 m** from edge, flagged at **1.5 m** intervals
   - Only trained workers allowed inside CAZ; safety monitor must be present

5. **Training & Rescue Plan** — (0.5 mark)
   - Every worker must complete **fall protection training** before stepping onto steel; a **prompt rescue plan** (≤ 6 minutes) is mandatory to prevent **suspension trauma**

---

> **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE):** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]
>
> **(a)** Explain the **rigging and sling safety requirements** for hoisting steel members. What are the standard **hand signals** used by the rigger? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **10 m long I-section steel girder weighing 6 tonnes** is to be lifted by a mobile crane. Calculate the **dynamic amplification factor (DAF)** if it is suddenly stopped mid-swing, given: sling drop height $h = 2 \text{ m}$, sling stiffness $E \cdot A = 1.5 \times 10^5 \text{ N}$, effective pendulum length $L_{\text{eff}} = 5 \text{ m}$. Comment on the safety. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Rigging, Sling Safety & Hand Signals (7 marks):**

**Sling Selection (2 marks):**
- **Wire rope slings** (6×19 or 6×37 construction) — most common in steel erection
- **Synthetic web slings** — for finished/painted surfaces
- **Chain slings** — for high-temperature or sharp-edge lifts
- **Sling angle** — must be **≥ 60° from horizontal** to avoid excessive tension; at 60°, sling tension = 1.155 × load per leg

**Sling Inspection (2 marks):**
- **Daily visual check** by rigger
- **6-monthly documented inspection** by competent person
- **Discard criteria** (IS 6215): 10 randomly distributed broken wires in one rope lay, or 5 broken wires in one strand in one rope lay, kinking, birdcaging, or diameter reduction > 10%

**Hand Signals (OSHA 1926.1419 / IS 7205) (3 marks):**
| Signal | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Arm extended, fist clenched, thumb up** | Hoist (load up) |
| **Arm extended, fist clenched, thumb down** | Lower (load down) |
| **Arm extended, palm down, swing motion** | Boom up / down |
| **Both arms extended horizontally, palms together** | Stop |
| **One arm over head, circular motion** | Emergency stop |
| **Index finger pointing in direction** | Travel / swing |
| **Tapping top of head with flat hand** | All clear / dog everything |

> Only **ONE designated signal person** directs the crane operator. **Radio communication** is mandatory when visibility is poor (fog, rain, distance > 30 m).

**(b) DAF Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Mass $m = 6 \text{ tonnes} = 6000 \text{ kg}$
- Length $L = 10 \text{ m}$, $L_{\text{eff}} = 5 \text{ m}$
- Sling drop $h = 2 \text{ m}$
- $E \cdot A = 1.5 \times 10^5 \text{ N}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Static weight:**

$$W = m \cdot g = 6000 \times 9.81 = 58860 \text{ N} \approx 58.86 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Apply dynamic amplification formula:**

$$F_{\text{dyn}} = W \cdot \left( 1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2 \cdot h \cdot E \cdot A}{W \cdot L_{\text{eff}}}} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the term inside the square root:**

$$\dfrac{2 \cdot h \cdot E \cdot A}{W \cdot L_{\text{eff}}} = \dfrac{2 \times 2 \times 1.5 \times 10^5}{58860 \times 5}$$

$$= \dfrac{6 \times 10^5}{294300} \approx 2.039$$

**Step 4 — Solve the square root:**

$$\sqrt{1 + 2.039} = \sqrt{3.039} \approx 1.7433$$

**Step 5 — Dynamic force:**

$$F_{\text{dyn}} = 58860 \times (1 + 1.7433) = 58860 \times 2.7433 \approx 161470 \text{ N} \approx 161.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Dynamic Amplification Factor:**

$$\text{DAF} = \dfrac{F_{\text{dyn}}}{W} = \dfrac{161.5}{58.86} \approx 2.74$$

**Step 7 — Safety Comment (2 marks within 7):**

> [!WARNING]
> A DAF of **2.74** means the sling is loaded to **nearly 3 times the static weight** during an emergency stop. A sling rated for exactly 58.86 kN **will fail**. Therefore, the sling must be selected for **at least 3 × 58.86 = 176.6 kN WLL** (Working Load Limit), giving a **safety factor ≥ 3** as per IS 6215. Furthermore, **tag lines must be used** to prevent sudden swing arrest, and operators must avoid **jerky crane movements** during the lift.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in this topic (per 2024 KTU valuation key):**
> 1. **Confusing "snug-tight" with "fully tightened"** — snug-tight = spanner effort only; pre-tensioned = torque wrench to design value. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the wind stop-work limit** — always mention **50 km/h (≈ 13.9 m/s)** and cite IS 7205 / OSHA 1926.1428.
> 3. **Stating only ONE tag line** — minimum TWO are mandatory; stating one will lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up PFAS and Guardrails** — guardrails are *passive* (preventing fall), PFAS is *active arrest* (catching the worker). Examiners deduct 1 mark for interchange.
> 5. **Skipping the "before crane releases" rule** — always state *"at least two snug-tight bolts AND temporary bracing BEFORE crane hook is released"*.
> 6. **No mention of "competent person" / "qualified rigger"** — every KTU 2024 scheme answer must reference the **competent person for inspection** and **trained/qualified rigger** for signalling. Adding this single line often earns a free 0.5–1 mark.
> 7. **Wrong unit conversion for wind speed** — 1 m/s = 3.6 km/h; students often wrongly equate 50 km/h with 50 m/s. Always show the conversion.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Erection of Structural Steel Work"**

- ✅ **Five Phases**: Plan → Mobilize → Lift → Align/Bolt → Final Fix & Inspect
- ✅ **Two Tag Lines minimum**, each ≥ 10 mm manila / 12 mm polypropylene
- ✅ **Dwell Test**: lift 300 mm → hold 10 s → hoist (mandatory for every critical lift)
- ✅ **At least TWO snug-tight bolts** before releasing crane hook
- ✅ **Temporary bracing** must be in place before any column is left freestanding
- ✅ **Wind stop-work limit = 50 km/h (13.9 m/s)** as per IS 7205
- ✅ **Lightning stop-work = within 8 km of detected storm**
- ✅ **Fall protection mandatory above 1.8 m** (OSHA 1926.501)
- ✅ **PFAS anchor strength = 22.2 kN (5,000 lb)** per worker
- ✅ **Sling Safety Factor = 5 (personnel) / 3 (material)**
- ✅ **Sling discard**: 10 random broken wires per lay, or 5 in one strand per lay
- ✅ **Hand signals**: ONE designated rigger only (or radio if visibility poor)
- ✅ **Bolt torque (M20 8.8) ≈ 411 N·m** (K = 0.20) or 329 N·m (K = 0.16)
- ✅ **Plumb tolerance = H/500** for columns
- ✅ **Exclusion zone = 1.5 × boom radius** during every lift
- ✅ **Daily Toolbox Talk + JSA** is non-negotiable
- ✅ **Rescue plan within 6 minutes** to prevent suspension trauma
- ✅ **NDT of welds** (UT/MPI/DPI) before final sign-off
- ✅ **Competent Person** must inspect all rigging gear every 6 months (documented)
- ✅ **OSHA 1926 Subpart R** = the international benchmark for steel erection safety
- ✅ **IS Codes to memorize**: IS 7205 (Erection), IS 800 (Steel Design), IS 4000 (Bolting), IS 6215 (Slings), IS 11972 (Safety Nets), IS 3521 (Harness), IS 15988 (PFAS)

> **Final Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, structure the answer as: (i) **Definition / Sequence** [2 marks], (ii) **Key Safety Requirements** [4 marks], (iii) **Standards/IS Codes cited** [3 marks], (iv) **Diagram/Flowchart** [3 marks], (v) **Conclusion/Sign-off note** [2 marks]. This 5-part structure mirrors the KTU 2024 mark allocation grid.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction of Temporary Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT526 - SAFETY IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Construction of Temporary Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **temporary structure** in civil engineering is defined as any non-permanent construction, ancillary equipment, or supporting system that is erected and dismantled to facilitate the execution of a permanent construction project. These structures are **not intended for permanent occupancy** and are typically removed once the primary structural elements attain their design strength and self-supporting capability.

According to **IS 14687:1999** (Falsework for Concrete Structures — Guidelines) and **BS 5975:2019** (Code of Practice for Temporary Works Procedures), temporary works encompass formwork, falsework, shoring, propping, scaffolding, access platforms, hoarding, and trench supports. The **KTU 2024 Scheme** explicitly treats these as separate risk-classified systems under the broader umbrella of Construction Safety Engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2)**
> Temporary structures are treated as a critical safety concern because **over 30% of all construction fatalities** worldwide are linked to the failure or improper erection of temporary works. Under NEP 2020 outcome-based education, students must be able to *identify, classify, design-check, and inspect* these systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are baking a multi-layered wedding cake. Before the cake is baked, you need:
- **A baking pan (Formwork)** to give shape to the soft batter (wet concrete).
- **A refrigerator shelf (Scaffolding)** so you can reach the upper tiers safely.
- **A support pillar (Shoring/Underpinning)** to hold the lower layer while the upper one sets.

Once the cake is baked and solid, all these helpers are removed — the cake stands on its own. **Temporary structures work exactly the same way in construction**: they exist *only* to assist during the *brief, vulnerable phase* before the permanent structure can support itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **Formwork** → Shapes wet concrete until it hardens.
> - **Falsework** → Supports formwork from below (vertical load transfer).
> - **Shoring** → Temporarily supports a structure undergoing repair or alteration.
> - **Underpinning** → Strengthens an existing foundation from below.
> - **Scaffolding** → Provides access for workers, materials, and tools at height.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Reference Code |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Minimum design live load on work platform | **1.50 kN/m²** | BS EN 12811-1 |
| Density of fresh unreinforced concrete (γc) | **24.0 kN/m³** | IS 875 (Part 1) |
| Density of fresh reinforced concrete (γc) | **25.0 kN/m³** | IS 875 (Part 1) |
| Minimum safety factor for formwork design | **2.0 (against collapse)** | IS 14687 |
| Standard worker weight for design | **1.0 kN (≈ 100 kg)** | BS EN 12811 |
| Minimum factor of safety for scaffolding couplers | **1.5 (slip), 2.0 (collapse)** | IS 4014 (Part 2) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Temporary Structures

Temporary structures are classified along four primary axes. Mastery of this taxonomy is **mandatory** for KTU Module 2.

#### A. By Function
1. **Formwork & Falsework** — Shuttering systems for fresh concrete.
2. **Scaffolding & Access** — Working platforms at heights.
3. **Shoring & Underpinning** — Temporary support during repair.
4. **Hoarding & Gantries** — Site enclosure and overhead protection.
5. **Trench Supports (Timbering)** — Earth retention for excavations.

#### B. By Material
- **Timber** (Sawn wood, Plywood) — Low cost, limited reuse.
- **Steel** (Tubular, System scaffold) — High strength, reusable.
- **Aluminium** — Lightweight, used for suspended scaffolds.
- **Plastic / Plywood with composite skin** — Smooth concrete finish.
- **Bamboo** (informal sector) — Used in low-rise Indian sites.

#### C. By Structural Action
- **Vertical load-bearing**: Falsework, Props, Shoring.
- **Lateral load-bearing**: Trench sheets, Bracing, Hoardings.
- **Combined**: Scaffolding (vertical + horizontal + diagonal).

#### D. By Lifecycle Position
- **Pre-pour** (formwork, falsework, rebar staging).
- **In-pour** (screed platforms, vibrating platforms).
- **Post-pour** (curing platforms, access scaffolds, demolition frames).

### 2.2 Design Philosophy — The 4-Layer Safety Model

The KTU 2024 Scheme aligns with the **BS 5975:2019 four-tier Temporary Works hierarchy**:

1. **Tier 1 — Risk Classification**: Categorise works as Low, Medium, or High risk.
2. **Tier 2 — Design Check**: A competent Temporary Works Designer (TWD) prepares calculations.
3. **Tier 3 — Independent Check**: A Temporary Works Coordinator (TWC) verifies the design.
4. **Tier 4 — Permit to Load / Strike**: Formal sign-off before concrete pour or dismantling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** When asked to "explain safety procedures," students who mention the **TWC and TWD roles** earn significantly higher marks than generic answers. This is a high-frequency Module 2 question.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Applicable Code |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P = \gamma_c \cdot H$ | Hydrostatic concrete pressure (column) | kN/m² | CIRIA 108 |
| 2 | $P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot (0.3 + 0.43 V_c) \cdot K$ | DIN 18218 column pressure | kN/m² | DIN 18218 |
| 3 | $P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot (C_1 \sqrt{V_c} + C_2) \cdot K \cdot \sqrt{H}$ | Wall formwork pressure (ACI 347 simplified) | kN/m² | ACI 347 |
| 4 | $q = \frac{P \cdot d^2}{10}$ | Design load on timber plank (permissible) | kN/m | IS 883 |
| 5 | $\sigma = \frac{P \cdot A}{N} \leq \sigma_{allow}$ | Axial stress check on prop | N/mm² | IS 14687 |
| 6 | $F_{OS} = \frac{Ultimate\ Load}{Working\ Load}$ | Factor of safety | dimensionless | IS 14687 |
| 7 | $W_{eq} = \frac{2 \cdot W \cdot h_{fall}}{g \cdot t^2}$ | Equivalent static load from falling debris | kN | IS 875 (Part 4) |
| 8 | $M = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{8}$ | Bending moment in plank/stringer | kN·m | Strength of Materials |
| 9 | $V = \frac{w \cdot L}{2}$ | Shear force in simply supported plank | kN | Strength of Materials |
| 10 | $SF = \frac{M_{ult}}{M_{applied}} \geq 2.0$ | Safety factor check for flexure | dimensionless | IS 14687 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always convert dimensions to a **single unit system** (SI). Mixing mm with m is the **#1 reason students lose marks** in numerical questions. If $d$ is in mm, use $E$ in N/mm², but if $L$ is in m, use $q$ in kN/m.

### 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter (Engineering Utility)

- **Lateral Pressure (Eq. 1–3)**: Determines the **plywood thickness** and **waler spacing**. Underestimating pressure causes blowouts (concrete burst-out), one of the deadliest temporary-work failures.
- **Deflection Check (Eq. 4, 8)**: Prevents visible undulations in finished concrete. KTU questions frequently ask: "Why is deflection limited to L/270 for formwork?" — answer: to avoid cracking of fresh concrete and ensure dimensional tolerance.
- **Equivalent Static Load (Eq. 7)**: Used to design **debris platforms and catch fans** in multi-storey construction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Lateral Pressure of Fresh Concrete on Formwork (CIRIA / Plowman Model)

**Background:** Wet concrete behaves as a fluid for a short period. As it sets, its internal friction increases, so the *effective* hydrostatic head is less than the actual pour height. The CIRIA model captures this by introducing a "set-retarded" effective head.

**Step 1 — Define the system.**
Let:
- $H$ = actual height of concrete pour (m)
- $V_c$ = rate of pour (m/hr)
- $\gamma_c$ = unit weight of fresh concrete (kN/m³)
- $R$ = retardation factor (CIRIA coefficient)

**Step 2 — Identify effective hydrostatic head.**
The pressure increases linearly up to a maximum at the "set time" $t_s$, beyond which concrete behaves as a solid. The set time is governed by the setting characteristics of cement and ambient temperature.

$$
t_s = \frac{H}{V_c}
$$

**Step 3 — Establish maximum pressure.**
For a column (where formwork confines concrete fully, friction is maximum):

$$
P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot H
$$

But for **walls** (friction along base reduces effective head), CIRIA introduces:

$$
P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot \left[ C_1 \sqrt{V_c \cdot t_s} + C_2 \right]
$$

Expanding $t_s$:

$$
P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot \left[ C_1 \sqrt{V_c \cdot \frac{H}{V_c}} + C_2 \right] = \gamma_c \cdot \left[ C_1 \sqrt{H} + C_2 \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Insert the CIRIA constants for Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) at 20 °C.**

For walls with $H < 5.5$ m, $V_c < 7$ m/hr:
- $C_1 = 0.45$
- $C_2 = 0.30$

$$
\boxed{P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot \left[ 0.45 \sqrt{H} + 0.30 \right] \quad \text{(kN/m}^2\text{, for walls)}}
$$

But $P_{max}$ **cannot exceed** the full hydrostatic value:

$$
P_{max} \leq \gamma_c \cdot H
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical (KTU-style problem).**
A wall of height $H = 3.0$ m is poured at $V_c = 2.0$ m/hr. The concrete is OPC with $\gamma_c = 25.0$ kN/m³. Determine the maximum lateral pressure.

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$
P_{max} = 25.0 \times \left[ 0.45 \sqrt{3.0} + 0.30 \right]
$$

Calculate the square root first:

$$
\sqrt{3.0} = 1.732
$$

Substitute:

$$
P_{max} = 25.0 \times \left[ 0.45 \times 1.732 + 0.30 \right]
$$

$$
= 25.0 \times \left[ 0.7794 + 0.30 \right]
$$

$$
= 25.0 \times 1.0794
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{max} = 26.99 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \approx 27.0\;\text{kN/m}^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Hydrostatic upper bound check.**

$$
\gamma_c \cdot H = 25.0 \times 3.0 = 75.0\;\text{kN/m}^2
$$

Since $27.0 < 75.0$, the CIRIA value governs. ✅

### 3.2 Derivation: Scaffolding Leg Load & Bearing Capacity

**Step 1 — Total vertical load on a standard scaffold leg.**

$$
P_{leg} = P_{DL} + P_{LL} + P_{self}
$$

Where:
- $P_{DL}$ = Dead load of planks, boards, tubes, etc.
- $P_{LL}$ = Live load (workers, materials, equipment)
- $P_{self}$ = Self-weight of scaffold leg itself

**Step 2 — Tributary area method.**

For a scaffold grid of $L_x \times L_y$ (in metres), the load per leg is:

$$
P_{leg} = (q_{DL} + q_{LL}) \cdot L_x \cdot L_y + P_{self}
$$

**Step 3 — Working example.**
A 2.0 m × 2.0 m scaffold grid supports $q_{DL} = 0.30$ kN/m² (planks) and $q_{LL} = 1.50$ kN/m² (Class 2 — light work). Each leg self-weight = 0.20 kN.

$$
P_{leg} = (0.30 + 1.50) \times 2.0 \times 2.0 + 0.20
$$

$$
= 1.80 \times 4.0 + 0.20
$$

$$
= 7.20 + 0.20
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{leg} = 7.40\;\text{kN}}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Factor of Safety (FoS).**
As per IS 4014 (Part 2), FoS ≥ 2.0 for ultimate state:

$$
P_{ult} = 7.40 \times 2.0 = 14.80\;\text{kN}
$$

The leg capacity must exceed this value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students forget to **multiply by the number of working levels**. If 3 platforms are stacked, multiply the live load by 3 (or use BS 5975 reduction factors). Always state: "assuming single working level" unless told otherwise.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Failure Modes of Temporary Structures

| Failure Mode | Primary Cause | Safety Mitigation | KTU Tag |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Formwork blowout** | Inadequate lateral bracing, over-rapid pour | Pressure relief vents, controlled pour rate | High-frequency |
| **Scaffold collapse** | Missing ties, soft ground, overloading | Base jacks on sole boards, full tying pattern | High-frequency |
| **Prop buckling** | Eccentric loading, undersized props | Centre-line loading, lateral restraints at mid-height | Medium |
| **Trench cave-in** | No shoring, surcharge loads | Hydraulic shores, battered slopes (≤ 1:1) | High-frequency |
| **Hoarding overturning** | Wind load, inadequate ballast | Wind analysis per IS 875 Part 3, kentledge blocks | Medium |
| **Underpinning settlement** | Sequential underpinning errors | Pre-loading jacks, monitoring | Low |
| **Catch platform failure** | Impact over-load, decay | Debris net + wooden catch fan, weekly inspection | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Temporary Works Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation] --> B[Risk Classification: Low / Med / High]
    B --> C[Appointment of Temporary Works Designer TWD]
    C --> D[Design Calculations + Drawings]
    D --> E[Independent Check by TWC]
    E --> F{Design Approved?}
    F -- No --> G[Revise and Resubmit]
    G --> D
    F -- Yes --> H[Permit to Erect Issued]
    H --> I[Site Erection by Trained Crew]
    I --> J[Inspection Before Loading]
    J --> K[Permit to Load / Pour Concrete]
    K --> L[Monitoring During Use]
    L --> M{Concrete Strength Achieved?}
    M -- No --> L
    M -- Yes --> N[Permit to Strike]
    N --> O[Dismantling Sequence]
    O --> P[Material Inspection and Storage]
    P --> Q[Audit and Lessons Learnt]
```

### 4.2 Formwork Pressure Distribution Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph S1[Fresh Concrete Column]
        concrete1[Liquid Concrete gamma_c H]
    end
    subgraph S2[Formwork Ply]
        ply[Plywood Sheathing]
        waler[Horizontal Waler]
        tie[Tie Rod at Spacing S]
        stud[Vertical Stud]
    end
    subgraph S3[Falsework]
        prop[Adjustable Steel Prop]
        base[Base Jack on Sole Board]
    end
    concrete1 -->|Lateral Pressure Pmax| ply
    ply --> waler
    waler --> stud
    stud --> tie
    stud --> prop
    prop --> base
```

### 4.3 Scaffolding Tying Pattern

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph S1[Independent Tied Scaffold]
        leg1[Standard Leg 1]
        leg2[Standard Leg 2]
        leg3[Standard Leg 3]
        trans[Transom]
        ledg[Ledger]
        tie1[Tie to Wall Level 1]
        tie2[Tie to Wall Level 2]
        facade[Building Facade]
    end
    leg1 --- trans
    leg2 --- trans
    leg3 --- trans
    leg1 --- ledg
    leg2 --- ledg
    leg3 --- ledg
    ledg --> tie1
    ledg --> tie2
    tie1 --> facade
    tie2 --> facade
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Guide:** The Mermaid graphs are block-level functional maps. In the exam, students should re-draw a simple labelled sketch (e.g., wall formwork cross-section) and mark the **direction of pressure**, the **tie rod spacing**, and the **prop orientation** — full marks for a clean, dimensioned sketch.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
Define *temporary structures* in construction. List any four types with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Temporary structures are short-duration constructions erected to facilitate the permanent works and removed once their function is complete (1 mark). Four types:
1. **Formwork** — Plywood mould for RCC beam (½ mark).
2. **Falsework** — Steel props supporting beam soffit (½ mark).
3. **Scaffolding** — Tube-and-coupler platform for plastering (½ mark).
4. **Shoring** — Timber/steel supports propping a cracked slab during repair (½ mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
State the minimum factor of safety recommended by IS 14687 for the design of formwork and falsework.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
As per **IS 14687:1999**, the minimum factors of safety are:
1. **2.0** for falsework and vertical members against collapse (1 mark).
2. **2.5** for formwork sheathing against bending failure (1 mark).
3. **3.0** for connections and couplers (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Internal Choice

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** Part B carries 14 marks with internal choice. Each sub-question is 7 marks. You must attempt **one** of the two full questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Scaffolding & Falsework Analysis**

**Q.A(a) [7 marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Classify scaffolding systems. Explain in detail the *Tube-and-Coupler* scaffold, with a neat sketch and its typical load capacity.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

*Classification (3 marks):*
- **Tube-and-coupler scaffold** — Traditional, uses steel tubes + right-angle/swivel/putlog couplers.
- **System scaffold (Cuplock / Ringlock / Kwikstage)** — Modular, prefabricated, faster erection.
- **Frame scaffold** — Welded H-frames, common in the US/India.
- **Suspended / Hanging scaffold** — Platform suspended by ropes, used for façade work.
- **Cantilever / Truss-out scaffold** — Needles through wall, no ground support.
- **Mobile / Tower scaffold** — On wheels, lightweight aluminium.

*Tube-and-Coupler Detail (3 marks):*
- Standard tube: OD **48.3 mm**, wall thickness **4.0 mm**, steel grade S235.
- Couplers: Right-angle (load-bearing), Swivel (any angle), Putlog (to wall).
- Spacing: Standards at 2.0–2.5 m; ledgers at 2.0 m vertical; transoms at 1.0–1.5 m.
- Working load: **Class 2 = 1.50 kN/m²** for light duty; **Class 4 = 3.00 kN/m²** for heavy duty.
- FoS = **2.0** on couplers, **1.65** on tubes per IS 4014 (Part 2).

*Sketch (1 mark):* Neat labelled diagram showing standards, ledgers, transoms, couplers, working platform, ties to wall.

---

**Q.A(b) [7 marks] — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
A wall of 2.5 m height is to be cast in OPC concrete at 25 °C. The pour rate is 1.8 m/hr. Unit weight of concrete is 25.0 kN/m³. Determine (i) the maximum lateral pressure on the formwork using the CIRIA formula, and (ii) the hydrostatic upper bound. State which value governs and why.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — CIRIA formula (3 marks):*
For OPC walls with $C_1 = 0.45$, $C_2 = 0.30$:

$$
P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot (C_1 \sqrt{H} + C_2)
$$

$$
\sqrt{H} = \sqrt{2.5} = 1.581
$$

$$
P_{max} = 25.0 \times (0.45 \times 1.581 + 0.30)
$$

$$
= 25.0 \times (0.7115 + 0.30)
$$

$$
= 25.0 \times 1.0115 = 25.29\;\text{kN/m}^2
$$

*Step 2 — Hydrostatic bound (2 marks):*

$$
P_{hyd} = \gamma_c \cdot H = 25.0 \times 2.5 = 62.5\;\text{kN/m}^2
$$

*Step 3 — Conclusion (2 marks):*
Since $P_{max} = 25.29\;\text{kN/m}^2 < P_{hyd} = 62.5\;\text{kN/m}^2$, **the CIRIA value governs**. This is because the concrete sets before the full hydrostatic head can develop, so only the *effective* head at set time contributes to pressure.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Shoring, Underpinning & Formwork Design

**Q.B(a) [7 marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Explain the different methods of *shoring* with neat sketches. Under what circumstances is *underpinning* preferred over shoring?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

*Shoring Methods (5 marks):*
1. **Raking Shores** — Inclined members from ground to wall; used when ground cannot be excavated. (1 mark)
2. **Flying Shores** — Horizontal struts between two parallel walls; used when both walls need support but ground between is intact. (1 mark)
3. **Dead Shores** — Vertical props carrying a load from above (e.g., during lintel replacement); load transferred through needles and sole pieces. (1 mark)
4. **Hydraulic Shores** — Pre-loaded, adjustable, used in trench shoring. (½ mark)
5. **Pneumatic / H-Beam & Lagging** — For deep excavations. (½ mark)
*(One neat sketch of raking shore with wall, needles, rakers, sole plate, cleats, bracing — 1 mark)*

*Underpinning vs. Shoring (2 marks):*
Underpinning is preferred when the **foundation itself** is weak, settled, or being extended. It strengthens the foundation from below using:
- Pit method
- Pile method
- Needle & pile method
- Chemical/jet grouting.

Shoring is for *superstructural* support; underpinning is for *sub-structural* strengthening.

---

**Q.B(b) [7 marks] — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
A square column of size 400 mm × 400 mm and height 3.0 m is to be cast using self-compacting concrete (γ = 24.0 kN/m³). Estimate the design lateral pressure on the column formwork. If the formwork is made of 12 mm plywood (allowable bending stress 12 N/mm²), determine the permissible spacing of the horizontal walers assuming a simply supported span. Take waler section as 75 mm × 150 mm timber.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Column pressure (2 marks):*
For a fully confined column (friction at base is maximum), full hydrostatic pressure is used:

$$
P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot H = 24.0 \times 3.0 = 72.0\;\text{kN/m}^2
$$

*Step 2 — Load per metre of waler (2 marks):*
With walers at spacing $s$ (m), load per metre:

$$
w = P_{max} \cdot s \quad \text{(kN/m)}
$$

*Step 3 — Bending capacity of waler (2 marks):*
For 75 mm × 150 mm timber, section modulus:

$$
Z = \frac{b d^2}{6} = \frac{75 \times 150^2}{6} = \frac{75 \times 22500}{6} = 281250\;\text{mm}^3
$$

Allowable moment:

$$
M_{allow} = \sigma \cdot Z = 12 \times 281250 = 3375000\;\text{N·mm} = 3.375\;\text{kN·m}
$$

*Step 4 — Solve for spacing (1 mark):*
For a simply supported waler, the moment from a UDL of length $s$ (assuming walers span between studs of length, say, $L_s$ — taking $L_s = 1.0$ m as the column height segment between ties) is:

$$
M = \frac{w L_s^2}{8} = \frac{P_{max} \cdot s \cdot L_s^2}{8}
$$

Set $M = M_{allow}$:

$$
3.375 = \frac{72.0 \cdot s \cdot 1.0^2}{8}
$$

$$
s = \frac{3.375 \times 8}{72.0 \cdot 1.0} = \frac{27.0}{72.0} = 0.375\;\text{m}
$$

$$
