\boxed{s \approx 375\;\text{mm} \text{ (use 350 mm in practice)}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Mixing units:** Pressure in kN/m², dimensions in mm — students forget to convert. Always check before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting the FoS:** IS 14687 demands FoS ≥ 2.0. If you don't multiply, you lose at least 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong CIRIA constants:** Using column constants (0.3 + 0.43 V) for walls. **Remember:** columns = full hydrostatic; walls = CIRIA reduced.
> 4. **Sketches without labels:** A sketch without force arrows, dimensions, or member names gets only 1 out of 2 marks.
> 5. **Striking time confusion:** Students write "formwork can be removed in 24 hours" — the correct answer requires a **cube test** showing ≥ 70% of design strength (or as per IS 456).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Temporary structures** include formwork, falsework, scaffolding, shoring, underpinning, hoarding, and trench supports.
- ✅ The **CIRIA formula** for walls: $P_{max} = \gamma_c (C_1 \sqrt{H} + C_2)$.
- ✅ For **columns**, the **full hydrostatic** head is used (worst case).
- ✅ **Minimum FoS** as per IS 14687: **2.0** for falsework, **2.5** for sheathing, **3.0** for couplers.
- ✅ **BS EN 12811 load classes** range from 0.75 to 6.00 kN/m² — match scaffold class to work type.
- ✅ **TWC and TWD** are mandatory roles for medium and high-risk temporary works (BS 5975).
- ✅ **Shoring types**: Raking, Flying, Dead, Hydraulic. **Underpinning types**: Pit, Pile, Needle, Grouting.
- ✅ **Strike (stripping) time** is governed by concrete strength, not calendar days.
- ✅ **Standard tube** in scaffolding: OD 48.3 mm, thickness 4.0 mm.
- ✅ **Permit systems** (Permit to Erect, Permit to Load, Permit to Strike) are non-negotiable safety controls.
- ✅ **Inspection frequency**: Before every shift, after any disturbance, and weekly formal inspection by competent person.
- ✅ Always convert units **before** substituting; **double-check** the hydrostatic upper bound when using CIRIA.
- ✅ Mermaid diagrams in this note are functional maps; reproduce a labelled sketch in the answer booklet for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety in Various Construction Operations - General Safety Requirements at Work Place
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT526 - SAFETY IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Safety in Various Construction Operations - General Safety Requirements at Work Place

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Safety Requirements at a Work Place (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> General safety requirements at a construction workplace refer to the **mandatory minimum standards, procedures, equipment, and administrative controls** that must be established, maintained, and enforced at every construction site to protect the health, safety, and welfare of all personnel — including workers, supervisors, engineers, visitors, and the general public — from the recognized hazards inherent to construction operations. These requirements are governed by the **Factories Act 1948**, the **Building and Other Construction Workers (Regulation of Employment and Conditions of Service) Act, 1996 (BOCW Act)**, the **Indian Electricity Rules 1956**, the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 7 & Part 8)**, and the **IS/ISO 45001:2018** Occupational Health and Safety Management System framework.

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework, this topic maps directly to:

| CO Mapping | Course Outcome Description | Bloom's Level |
|------------|----------------------------|---------------|
| **CO2** | Identify and apply general safety requirements and administrative controls at construction workplaces. | *Apply (L3)* |
| **CO3** | Evaluate the adequacy of safety provisions on construction sites. | *Evaluate (L5)* |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Cockpit Checklist"**
> Imagine a pilot preparing for takeoff. Before the aircraft even moves, the pilot runs through a **standardized checklist**: fuel, flaps, instruments, weather, emergency exits. This checklist is not optional, not improvised, and not skipped — it is the difference between a safe flight and a disaster. **General safety requirements at a construction site work exactly the same way.** A construction site is, statistically, **one of the most dangerous workplaces on Earth** — the International Labour Organization (ILO) estimates **one fatal accident every 10 minutes** in the construction sector globally. The "pre-flight checklist" for a site includes: **PPE for every worker, clearly visible warning signs, clean walkways, fire extinguishers, first-aid boxes, trained emergency responders, and documented safety induction for every new entrant.** Just as a pilot never skips a checklist, a compliant construction site never skips these foundational requirements.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is a "High-Yield" KTU Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Direct theory questions (7–10 marks)** are asked almost every semester on PPE types, housekeeping rules, and statutory welfare facilities.
> - **Numerical/analytical questions** on safety sign dimensions, illumination levels, noise exposure, and first-aid box contents frequently appear.
> - **Case-study/short-note questions** on BOCW Act provisions and NBC 2016 standards are examiner favorites.
> - Mastering this topic provides the **foundation for Module 3, 4, and 5** (specific hazards like excavation, scaffolding, electrical, hot work).

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU Recall)

- **Minimum illumination at construction sites: 50 lux** (general areas), **100–200 lux** (fine work), per **IS 3646 (Part 2)**.
- **Permissible noise exposure: 90 dB(A) for 8 hours**, with a **3 dB doubling rate** (ISO/IS standards).
- **Minimum first-aid box: 1 box per 50 workers** (Factories Act, Section 45).
- **Minimum drinking water: 1 water point per 100 workers** (BOCW Rules).
- **Minimum toilet ratio: 1 per 25 males / 1 per 15 females** (NBC 2016).
- **Safety signage viewing distance rule: D = 50 × H** (D = distance in meters, H = height of sign letter in mm) — per **IS 9457**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Pillars of General Workplace Safety

The KTU 2024 syllabus (and the **BOCW Central Rules 1998, Chapter VII**) classify general workplace safety into **seven interrelated pillars**. A deficit in any one pillar compromises the entire safety system.

### Pillar 1: Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)

PPE is the **last line of defense** in the **Hierarchy of Controls** (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → **PPE**).

| Body Part | PPE Type | Indian Standard |
|-----------|----------|-----------------|
| Head | Safety Helmet (Hard Hat) | **IS 2925:1984** |
| Eyes & Face | Safety Goggles / Face Shield | **IS 5983 / IS 7524** |
| Ears | Ear Plugs / Ear Muffs | **IS 6229 / IS 9167** |
| Respiratory | Dust Mask / Respirator | **IS 14166 / IS 9473** |
| Hands | Leather / Rubber / Cut-resistant Gloves | **IS 6994** |
| Feet | Safety Shoes / Gum Boots | **IS 15298 (Part 2)** |
| Body | High-Visibility Vest / Coverall | **IS 15809** |
| Fall | Full-Body Harness with Shock Absorber | **IS 3521:1999** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Logic:** PPE must be **risk-based, fit-tested, certified (ISI mark), and inspected before every use.** The principle is *"One PPE for one hazard — never substitute."*

### Pillar 2: Housekeeping

Poor housekeeping is the **root cause of approximately 25–30% of all construction accidents** (OSHA statistics). General rules:

- **Accessways clear:** minimum **1.8 m wide**, **2.1 m headroom**.
- **Materials stacked:** maximum height **1.5 m** for loose materials, neatly banded and chocked.
- **Waste segregation:** at source, daily disposal; **no nails protruding** from timber (bend over or remove).
- **Spill control:** oil/grease spills cleaned within **15 minutes** of occurrence.
- **Floor openings:** covered with **150 mm projection** on all sides and marked.

### Pillar 3: Safety Signage, Signals & Barricading

Construction sites use the **Safety Color Code (IS 9457:1980 / ANSI Z535)**:

| Color | Meaning | Application |
|-------|---------|-------------|
| **Red** | Danger / Prohibition | Fire equipment, stop, no-entry |
| **Yellow** | Caution | Wet floor, low clearance, tripping |
| **Blue** | Mandatory Action | Wear helmet, wash hands |
| **Green** | Safety / First Aid | Emergency exits, first-aid station |
| **Orange** | Warning (live parts) | Energized equipment, voltage hazard |

**Sign Shapes:**
- **Circular Red Ring with Diagonal Bar** → Prohibition
- **Solid Blue Circle** → Mandatory
- **Yellow Equilateral Triangle, Black Border** → Warning
- **Green Square/Rectangle** → Safe Condition / Emergency

### Pillar 4: Emergency Preparedness & Response

Every site must have a documented **Emergency Response Plan (ERP)** containing:

1. **Emergency Contact Directory** (Hospital, Fire Brigade, Police, Electricity Board, Site Safety Officer).
2. **Escape Routes** marked with **photoluminescent signs** (min **1 m wide**, **2.2 m headroom**).
3. **Assembly Point** designated at least **30 m** away from the main structure.
4. **Mock drills:** conducted **every 3 months** (BOCW Rule 71), with records maintained for **3 years**.
5. **Emergency lighting** with **1-hour battery backup** minimum.

### Pillar 5: First Aid & Medical Facilities

Per **Factories Act 1948, Section 45** and **BOCW Rules 1998**:

| Workforce Size | Mandatory Facility |
|----------------|--------------------|
| **Up to 50 workers** | 1 standard first-aid box |
| **51–150 workers** | 1 first-aid box + 1 stretcher + 1 ambulance van on call |
| **151–300 workers** | First-aid room with a qualified **Occupational First-Aider** |
| **> 300 workers** | Full-time **Ambulance Van** stationed on site, plus Occupational Health Centre |

**Standard First-Aid Box Contents (Schedule XI, BOCW Rules — HIGH-YIELD for KTU):**
- Antiseptic solution (Dettol/Savlon) — **100 ml**
- Sterilized cotton wool — **50 g**
- Sterilized gauze pieces — **6 nos.**
- Bandages (small & large) — **4 nos. each**
- Adhesive plasters (Band-Aids) — **12 nos.**
- Scissors, forceps, safety pins — **1 each**
- Triangular bandages — **2 nos.**
- Antiseptic cream (burnol) — **1 tube**
- Pain-relief tablets — **as per doctor’s prescription**
- **Splints (wooden)** — **2 nos.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students wrongly include "medicines like Aspirin/Crocin" in the first-aid box. **No medicine can be kept in a first-aid box** — only first-aid materials. Medicines must be prescribed and administered by a qualified medical practitioner.

### Pillar 6: Welfare & Sanitation Facilities

Per **BOCW Rules 1998, Chapter VIII** and **NBC 2016, Part 12**:

| Facility | Minimum Standard |
|----------|------------------|
| Drinking Water | **1 tap per 100 workers**, **5 L/worker/shift**, tested monthly |
| Washing Places | **1 per 30 workers** (or ratio of 1:30) |
| Latrines/Urinals | **1 per 25 males / 1 per 15 females** (separate for each sex) |
| Rest Shelters | **1 per 150 workers**, min **6 m² area** |
| Canteen | Mandatory if **> 250 workers** |
| Crèche | Mandatory if **> 20 female workers** |
| Accommodation | Separate for families, with sanitation |

### Pillar 7: Training, Induction & Statutory Documentation

- **Safety Induction** for every new worker (minimum **2 hours**, covering site hazards, PPE, emergency exits).
- **Toolbox Talk (TBT)** — daily **10–15 minute** safety briefing before shift.
- **Job Safety Analysis (JSA)** for every high-risk task.
- **Records maintained for 3 years minimum** (attendance, training, accidents, inspections).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS — Memorize for Numerical Questions:**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Sign Letter Height | $H = \dfrac{D}{50}$ | H in mm, D in metres | Higher D → larger sign |
| 2 | Permissible Exposure Time (PET) | $T = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-90)/3}}$ | T in hours, L in dB(A) | 3 dB doubling rule |
| 3 | Number of First-Aid Boxes | $N_{box} = \lceil \dfrac{W}{50} \rceil$ | W = workers | Round up |
| 4 | Number of Drinking Water Points | $N_{tap} = \lceil \dfrac{W}{100} \rceil$ | W = workers | Round up |
| 5 | Noise Dose (per OSHA) | $D = \sum \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \times 100$ | % | D ≤ 100% permissible |
| 6 | Illumination Level Selection | $E = \dfrac{\Phi \times \eta \times UF \times MF}{A}$ | Lux | $\Phi$ = lamp lumens |
| 7 | Walkway Width | $W_{min} = 1.8 \text{ m}$ (single lane) | metres | 2.4 m for two-way |
| 8 | Fire Extinguisher Travel Distance | $D_{max} = 15 \text{ m}$ (Class A) | metres | 22.5 m for Class B |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NEVER use the `|` character inside markdown table cells for absolute value. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.** (e.g., write $T = 8 \cdot 2^{-(L-90)/3}$ rather than $T = 8/2^{|(L-90)/3|}$.)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Permissible Exposure Time (PET) for Noise

The OSHA/ISO 3-dB exchange rate formula is derived from the principle that **each 3 dB increase in noise level doubles the acoustic energy**, hence the safe exposure time is halved.

**Step 1 — Define the base case.**
At the Threshold Limit Value (TLV) of **90 dB(A)**, the maximum daily exposure is **8 hours**.

$$T_{ref} = 8 \text{ hours}, \quad L_{ref} = 90 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**Step 2 — Express the energy-doubling relationship.**
Acoustic intensity $I$ is logarithmically related to sound level $L$:

$$L = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \text{ dB(A)}$$

A 3 dB increase means:

$$10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_2}{I_1}\right) = 3 \implies \frac{I_2}{I_1} = 10^{0.3} = 1.995 \approx 2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the equal-energy principle (constant noise dose per day).**
Total daily acoustic dose $D = I \times T$ must remain constant. If intensity doubles, time must halve:

$$I \times T = \text{constant} \implies I_2 \cdot T_2 = I_1 \cdot T_1$$

**Step 4 — Generalize for any level $L$.**
The intensity ratio between level $L$ and reference $L_{ref}$ is:

$$\frac{I}{I_{ref}} = 2^{(L - L_{ref})/3}$$

Therefore the permissible exposure time $T$ is:

$$T = \frac{T_{ref}}{2^{(L - L_{ref})/3}} = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/3}} \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5 — Worked example.**
A worker is exposed to **100 dB(A)** from a pile driver. Find the safe exposure time.

$$T = \frac{8}{2^{(100-90)/3}} = \frac{8}{2^{3.333}} = \frac{8}{10.079} = 0.794 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 47.6 \text{ minutes}}$$

Any longer exposure mandates **mandatory hearing protection (earplugs + muffs)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked example for PET verification:**
> - 90 dB → 8 h,  93 dB → 4 h,  96 dB → 2 h,  99 dB → 1 h,  100 dB → 0.794 h.
> - At 105 dB → ~23.8 min; at 115 dB → ~3.7 min; at 120 dB → ~1.18 min.

## 3.2 Derivation: Sign Letter Height

The IS 9457:1980 formula relates **maximum viewing distance** to **letter height** for legibility at construction sites.

**Step 1 — Empirical foundation.**
Studies show that a person with 6/6 vision reads a sign of letter height $H$ (in mm) from a maximum distance $D$ (in metres) when:

$$\frac{D}{H} = \frac{1}{50}$$

i.e., **1 mm of letter height per 50 mm of viewing distance** — or equivalently, **20 seconds of visual arc**.

**Step 2 — Rearrange for $H$:**

$$H = 50 \times D$$

with $H$ in mm and $D$ in metres.

**Step 3 — Apply units correctly.**

$$H \text{ [mm]} = 50 \times D \text{ [m]}$$

**Step 4 — Worked example.**
A "DANGER — HIGH VOLTAGE" sign must be readable from **40 m** on a highway construction site.

$$H = 50 \times 40 = 2000 \text{ mm} = 2.0 \text{ m}$$

The sign lettering must be at least **2 metres tall** — which is why such signs are physically very large.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for PPE Selection

| Hazard Identified | Required PPE | IS Standard | KTU Tag |
|-------------------|--------------|-------------|---------|
| Falling objects from height | Safety Helmet (Type 2, Class E) | IS 2925 | PPE-1 |
| Flying chips (grinding/cutting) | Face Shield + Safety Goggles | IS 7524 / IS 5983 | PPE-2 |
| Continuous noise > 90 dB(A) | Ear Muffs (over) + Ear Plugs (in) | IS 9167 / IS 6229 | PPE-3 |
| Concrete/masonry dust | FFP2 / FFP3 Respirator | IS 9473 | PPE-4 |
| Rebar handling / sharp edges | Cut-resistant Gloves (Level 5) | IS 6994 | PPE-5 |
| Wet / muddy surfaces | Gum Boots (steel toe) | IS 15298 | PPE-6 |
| Low visibility near traffic | Hi-Vis Vest (Class 3) | IS 15809 | PPE-7 |
| Work above 1.8 m fall height | Full-Body Harness + Shock Absorber | IS 3521 | PPE-8 |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: First-Aid Box Calculation

**Problem:** A residential project employs **137 workers** daily. How many first-aid boxes are required, and list the mandatory contents.

**Step 1 — Apply the rule:**

$$N_{box} = \left\lceil \frac{W}{50} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{137}{50} \right\rceil = \lceil 2.74 \rceil = 3 \text{ boxes}$$

**Step 2 — Additional requirements (51–150 bracket):**
- 1 stretcher
- 1 ambulance van on call (or 1 company-owned van)
- 1 trained first-aider per shift
- 1 first-aid register (in Hindi + local language + English)

**Step 3 — Standard contents per box (Schedule XI, BOCW Rules)** as listed in §2.1, Pillar 5.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Seven-Pillar Safety System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["PILLAR 1: PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT"]
    P2["PILLAR 2: HOUSEKEEPING AND ACCESSWAYS"]
    P3["PILLAR 3: SAFETY SIGNAGE AND BARRICADING"]
    P4["PILLAR 4: EMERGENCY PREPAREDNESS AND RESPONSE"]
    P5["PILLAR 5: FIRST AID AND MEDICAL FACILITIES"]
    P6["PILLAR 6: WELFARE AND SANITATION"]
    P7["PILLAR 7: TRAINING INDUCTION AND DOCUMENTATION"]

    P1 --> R["SAFE WORKPLACE SYSTEM"]
    P2 --> R
    P3 --> R
    P4 --> R
    P5 --> R
    P6 --> R
    P7 --> R

    R --> OUT["ZERO HARM OBJECTIVE: ZERO FATALITIES, ZERO LOST-TIME INJURIES"]

    subgraph STATUTORY_BASE["STATUTORY AND STANDARDS BASE"]
        FA["Factories Act 1948"]
        BOCW["BOCW Act 1996 and Rules 1998"]
        NBC["National Building Code 2016"]
        ISO["IS ISO 45001 2018"]
    end

    STATUTORY_BASE --> P1
    STATUTORY_BASE --> P2
    STATUTORY_BASE --> P3
    STATUTORY_BASE --> P4
    STATUTORY_BASE --> P5
    STATUTORY_BASE --> P6
    STATUTORY_BASE --> P7
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Emergency Response Activation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INCIDENT DETECTED"]
    B["STOP WORK AND ALERT SITE PERSONNEL"]
    C["ACTIVATE EMERGENCY SIREN"]
    D["CALL EMERGENCY SERVICES 108 101 102"]
    E["SITE FIRST AIDER RESPONDS"]
    F["EVACUATE TO ASSEMBLY POINT 30 M AWAY"]
    G["HEADCOUNT AT ASSEMBLY POINT"]
    H["INCIDENT COMMANDER BRIEFS"]
    I["INCIDENT REPORT FILED IN 24 HOURS"]
    J["ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS WITHIN 7 DAYS"]
    K["CORRECTIVE ACTION IMPLEMENTED"]
    L["LESSONS LEARNED SHARED IN TOOLBOX TALK"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
    J --> K
    K --> L
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Controls Pyramid (Adapted Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["LEVEL 1: ELIMINATION - Remove the hazard entirely"]
    L2["LEVEL 2: SUBSTITUTION - Replace with less hazardous material or method"]
    L3["LEVEL 3: ENGINEERING CONTROLS - Guardrails, machine guarding, ventilation"]
    L4["LEVEL 4: ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS - Training, signage, job rotation"]
    L5["LEVEL 5: PPE - Helmets, gloves, harnesses - LAST RESORT"]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** PPE is at the **bottom** of the hierarchy because it relies on the human element (proper wear, fit, maintenance) and does not remove the hazard. Always prefer **higher-level controls** when feasible.

## 4.4 Site Layout Zoning Diagram (Schematic)

    Z4["ZONE D: HAZARDOUS AREA - Excavation hot work height - Red tape and warning signs"]
    Z5["ZONE E: WELFARE BLOCK - Canteen rest room toilets drinking water first aid"]
    Z6["ZONE F: EMERGENCY ASSEMBLY POINT - Open ground 30 m from structure photoluminescent signs"]
    Z7["ZONE G: OFFICE AND ADMIN"]

    Z1 --> Z2

    Z4 --> Z5
    Z5 --> Z6
    Z6 --> Z7

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"List any six mandatory items to be kept in a standard First-Aid Box as per the BOCW Rules 1998, Schedule XI."**

**Model Answer (6 points, 0.5 each):**
1. Sterilized cotton wool — **50 g**
2. Sterilized gauze pieces — **6 nos.**
3. Bandages (small and large) — **4 nos. each**
4. Adhesive plasters — **12 nos.**
5. Antiseptic solution (Savlon/Dettol) — **100 ml**
6. Triangular bandages — **2 nos.**
7. Scissors, forceps, safety pins — **1 each**
8. Wooden splints — **2 nos.**
9. Burnol/antiseptic cream — **1 tube**

*(Any six with quantities = 3 marks)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**"Differentiate between the four standard safety color codes used in construction sites as per IS 9457:1980."**

**Model Answer (4 points × 0.75 = 3 marks):**

| Color | Meaning | Typical Application |
|-------|---------|---------------------|
| **Red** | Prohibition / Danger | Fire equipment, STOP, NO ENTRY |
| **Yellow** | Caution | Wet floor, low clearance, tripping hazards |
| **Blue** | Mandatory Action | Wear helmet, ear protection, wash hands |
| **Green** | Safety / First Aid | Emergency exits, first-aid station, assembly point |

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — 14-Module Comprehensive**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2, Q.II(a-b)]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **seven pillars of general safety requirements** that must be ensured at a construction workplace. (7 marks)

**(b)** A construction site employs **240 workers** per day. Calculate:
- (i) the number of first-aid boxes required,
- (ii) the number of drinking water taps required, and
- (iii) the minimum letter height of a "DANGER — DEEP EXCAVATION" sign readable from 30 m. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Seven Pillars (7 × 1 = 7 marks):**

1. **PPE (1 mark):** Helmets, gloves, safety shoes, goggles, ear protection, hi-vis vests, full-body harnesses — provided **free of cost** by employer (BOCW Sec 17), ISI-marked, refitted every 6 months.

2. **Housekeeping (1 mark):** Clear walkways (1.8 m wide), stacked materials not exceeding 1.5 m, daily debris removal, no protruding nails, spills cleaned within 15 minutes.

3. **Safety Signage & Barricading (1 mark):** Color codes (Red/Yellow/Blue/Green), warning boards, danger tape, barricades around excavations/openings.

4. **Emergency Preparedness (1 mark):** Documented ERP, escape routes (1 m × 2.2 m), assembly point 30 m away, mock drills every 3 months, emergency lighting with 1-hr backup.

5. **First-Aid & Medical (1 mark):** 1 box per 50 workers, stretcher, ambulance for > 150 workers, occupational first-aider per shift.

6. **Welfare Facilities (1 mark):** Drinking water (1 tap/100 workers, 5 L/worker/shift), toilets (1:25 males, 1:15 females), rest shelter, canteen (>250 workers), crèche.

7. **Training & Documentation (1 mark):** Safety induction (2 hrs), daily toolbox talks, JSA, 3-year record retention.

**(b) Calculations (7 marks):**

**(i) First-Aid Boxes (2 marks):**

$$N_{box} = \left\lceil \frac{240}{50} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.8 \rceil = \boxed{5 \text{ boxes}}$$

Plus, since 240 > 150, the site needs:
- 1 occupational first-aider per shift
- 1 ambulance van stationed on site

**(ii) Drinking Water Taps (2 marks):**

$$N_{tap} = \left\lceil \frac{240}{100} \right\rceil = \lceil 2.4 \rceil = \boxed{3 \text{ taps}}$$

Total water demand = 240 × 5 L = 1,200 L/day.

**(iii) Sign Letter Height (3 marks):**

Given: viewing distance $D = 30$ m, formula $H = 50 \times D$.

$$H = 50 \times 30 = 1500 \text{ mm} = \boxed{1.5 \text{ m}}$$

The sign lettering must be at least **1.5 m tall**, with red background and white letters as per IS 9457.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Supplementary Exam, Q.II(a-b)]**

**(a)** Discuss the **statutory requirements for Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** at a construction site as per the BOCW Act 1996 and relevant Indian Standards. (7 marks)

**(b)** A construction worker is exposed to **100 dB(A)** for 3 hours and to **93 dB(A)** for 2 hours. Determine:
- (i) the noise dose using the OSHA formula, and
- (ii) whether the exposure is within permissible limits. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Statutory PPE Requirements (7 marks):**

**Legal Basis (1 mark):** Section 17 of the **BOCW Act 1996** mandates the employer to provide **PPE free of cost**, ensure proper usage, and maintain records of issue. **Rule 38 of BOCW Central Rules 1998** specifies the type and quality of PPE.

**Head Protection (1 mark):** Helmets conforming to **IS 2925:1984** — Type 2 (electrical insulation up to 440 V), Class E (tested at 20 kV for 3 minutes). Color coding (white = supervisor, yellow = workers, red = safety staff, green = trainee).

**Eye & Face Protection (1 mark):** Safety goggles (**IS 5983**) for dust/chips, welding goggles (**IS 7524**) with filter lens shade 9–13 for arc welding, face shields for grinding.

**Hearing Protection (1 mark):** Ear plugs (**IS 6229**) reduce noise by 20–30 dB, ear muffs (**IS 9167**) reduce by 25–35 dB, combined use gives ~40 dB reduction. Mandatory if noise > 85 dB(A) for 8 hrs.

**Respiratory Protection (1 mark):** Dust mask FFP1 (filter efficiency 80%), FFP2 (94%), FFP3 (99%) per **IS 9473**. For silica dust — FFP3 mandatory.

**Foot & Body Protection (1 mark):** Safety shoes with steel toe (impact 200 J, compression 15 kN) per **IS 15298**, gum boots for wet areas, high-visibility vests **IS 15809** Class 3 for roadwork.

**Fall Protection (1 mark):** Full-body harness **IS 3521** with shock-absorbing lanyard, mandatory for work above 1.8 m. Twin lanyard (Y-type) for 100% tie-off.

**Issue & Maintenance:** One set per worker, replaced every 6 months or on damage, inspection register maintained.

**(b) Noise Dose Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Exposure 1: $L_1 = 100$ dB(A) for $C_1 = 3$ h
- Exposure 2: $L_2 = 93$ dB(A) for $C_2 = 2$ h
- Exchange rate: 3 dB (OSHA/IS standard)
- Criterion level: 90 dB(A) for 8 hours

**Step 1 — Compute permissible exposure time $T_i$ for each level (3 marks):**

For $L_1 = 100$ dB(A):

$$T_1 = \frac{8}{2^{(100-90)/3}} = \frac{8}{2^{3.333}} = \frac{8}{10.079} = 0.794 \text{ h}$$

For $L_2 = 93$ dB(A):

$$T_2 = \frac{8}{2^{(93-90)/3}} = \frac{8}{2^{1.0}} = \frac{8}{2} = 4.0 \text{ h}$$

**Step 2 — Compute fractional exposures (2 marks):**

$$\frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{3}{0.794} = 3.778$$

$$\frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{2}{4.0} = 0.500$$

**Step 3 — Compute total noise dose (1 mark):**

$$D = \left(\frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2}\right) \times 100 = (3.778 + 0.500) \times 100 = 427.8\%$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{D = 427.8\% \gg 100\%}$$

The exposure **exceeds the permissible limit by 4.28 times**. This is a **violation of occupational noise exposure standards** and the worker must be:
- Immediately removed from the noisy environment
- Provided with **dual hearing protection** (earplugs + muffs)
- Subjected to **audiometric testing**
- Rotated to a quieter job or given a 4-hour noise-free rest period

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Marking scheme trap:** Many students write "$T = 8/2^{(L-90)/5}$" using the **5 dB rule** (older NIOSH). KTU 2024 follows the **3 dB rule** (ISO/IS/OSHA). Using 5 dB will give wrong PET (e.g., 1.6 h instead of 0.794 h for 100 dB) and **cost you 4–5 marks**.
> 2. **Dose > 100% trap:** Students often forget to multiply by 100 and write dose as "3.778 + 0.500 = 4.278" without units. Always state the answer as a **percentage**.
> 3. **Sign-letter-height trap:** If the question gives distance in **feet**, convert to metres first (1 ft = 0.3048 m). The formula $H = 50 \times D$ is valid only when $D$ is in **metres** and $H$ in **mm**.
> 4. **First-aid box trap:** Do **not** list "medicines" in the first-aid box. Listing Aspirin/Crocin will cost 1 mark. Mention only **first-aid materials**.
> 5. **PPE color code trap:** White helmet = supervisor/manager, not engineer. Yellow = general worker, Green = new/trainee, Red = safety officer, Blue = electrician, Brown = carpenter. KTU often asks color-coded roles.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION — 30-SECOND RECALL**

- [ ] **PPE Rule:** Free of cost, ISI-marked, fit-tested, replaced every 6 months (BOCW Sec 17).
- [ ] **Helmet IS 2925, Safety Shoes IS 15298, Harness IS 3521, Goggles IS 5983/7524, Ear Muffs IS 9167.**
- [ ] **First-Aid Box:** 1 per **50 workers** (Factories Act Sec 45). Schedule XI of BOCW lists contents — **no medicines**.
- [ ] **Drinking Water:** **1 tap per 100 workers**, **5 L/worker/shift**, monthly testing.
- [ ] **Toilets:** **1 per 25 males, 1 per 15 females**, separate for each sex.
- [ ] **Color Code:** **R**ed = Prohibition, **Y**ellow = Caution, **B**lue = Mandatory, **G**reen = Safe Condition.
- [ ] **Sign formula:** $H = 50 \times D$ (H in mm, D in m).
- [ ] **Noise PET formula:** $T = 8 / 2^{(L-90)/3}$ (3 dB rule, KTU 2024 standard).
- [ ] **Noise dose:** $D = \sum (C_i / T_i) \times 100$; **permissible ≤ 100%**.
- [ ] **Escape route:** **1 m wide, 2.2 m headroom**, photoluminescent, with 1-hr backup lighting.
- [ ] **Assembly point:** at least **30 m** from main structure.
- [ ] **Mock drill frequency:** **once every 3 months** (BOCW Rule 71).
- [ ] **Illumination:** **50 lux** (general), **100–200 lux** (fine work) per IS 3646.
- [ ] **Walkway:** **1.8 m wide** minimum, **2.1 m headroom**.
- [ ] **Material stack:** max **1.5 m** high for loose materials.
- [ ] **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → **PPE** (bottom).
- [ ] **Key Acts/Rules:** Factories Act 1948, BOCW Act 1996 + Central Rules 1998, NBC 2016, IS/ISO 45001:2018.
- [ ] **Fire extinguisher travel distance:** max **15 m** (Class A), **22.5 m** (Class B).
- [ ] **Records retention:** minimum **3 years** for training, accidents, inspections.
- [ ] **High-risk tasks require JSA** + Permit-to-Work (PTW).

> **End of Notes — PESFT526 Module 2, Topic: General Safety Requirements at Work Place**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Under-Pinning and Shoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT526 - SAFETY IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Under-Pinning and Shoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Under-Pinning — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Under-Pinning** is the process of strengthening, reinforcing, or extending the existing foundation of a building or structure to a greater depth, or to distribute the load to a more stable soil stratum, without demolishing the superstructure above. It is a controlled sequence of localized excavation and replacement/substitution operations performed beneath the footings of an existing structure.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, under-pinning is classified as a *temporary-to-permanent structural stabilization technique* used during:
- Adjacent deep excavations
- Foundation failure remediation
- Building height addition (vertical extension)
- Soil bearing capacity enhancement

### 1.2 Shoring — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Shoring** is the construction of a temporary structural support system erected to hold unsafe walls, floors, roofs, or excavations in a stable condition, preventing collapse during construction, repair, alteration, or demolition of an adjacent/affected structure.

It transfers lateral and vertical loads from an unstable member to a firm ground anchor or an adjacent stable structure, ensuring **structural integrity** until permanent measures are completed.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy for Under-Pinning:** Imagine an old wooden table whose legs have become too short or rotten. Instead of throwing the table away, you carefully slide *new, longer wooden blocks (or wedges)* underneath the existing legs, one leg at a time, to lift the table back to a stable height. The table is never taken apart — only the *base support* is renewed from below. That is exactly what under-pinning does to a building.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy for Shoring:** Picture a tired, leaning brick wall that is about to fall on a child standing beside it. You urgently prop **slanted wooden planks (rakers)** against the wall, anchored firmly on the ground, to keep it from tipping over until you can repair it permanently. These temporary props are the *shores*. In a KTU context, these shores are the legal and engineering **life-safety system** during construction.

### 1.4 Why These Two Topics Are Co-Taught in Module 2

Both are **reactive stabilization methods** used when:
- A structure is *endangered by external activity* (excavation, demolition, neighbouring construction)
- A structure has *internally deteriorated* (cracks, settlement, wall bulging)
- The original support system is *no longer adequate* for new loading conditions

The unifying theme is **life safety through load transfer to a stable medium**.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics to Remember

| Parameter | Standard Metric |
|-----------|-----------------|
| Maximum length of one under-pin pit (Bay) | **1.0 m to 1.5 m** |
| Maximum unsupported length of wall at any stage | **1.2 m to 1.5 m** |
| Clearance between raking shores (vertical spacing) | **3 m to 4.5 m** |
| Angle of raker with horizontal ground | **45° to 75°** |
| Min. bearing capacity of soil for sole plate | **≥ 150 kN/m²** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 UNDER-PINNING — Systematic Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Objectives (Why we under-pin)
1. To **deepen** an existing foundation to reach a stronger soil layer.
2. To **widen** the footing so as to reduce the bearing pressure on soil.
3. To **restore structural integrity** of a settled/ cracked building.
4. To allow **adjacent deep excavation** without collapse of the neighbouring structure.
5. To enable **vertical extension** of an existing building by strengthening the existing foundation.

#### 2.1.2 General Operational Steps
- **Step 1 (Survey & Soil Investigation):** Determine the cause of distress, the depth of stable strata, and load characteristics. A safe KTU answer always begins with this.
- **Step 2 (Temporary Support):** The wall above the affected footing is supported by **needle beams and props** before excavation begins. This is a non-negotiable safety step.
- **Step 3 (Bay Excavation):** Excavate pits of length **1.0 m – 1.5 m** below the existing footing in *alternate bays*. The wall above is never left unsupported beyond **1.2 m length** at any time.
- **Step 4 (Cleaning & Keying):** Clean the underside of the existing footing and roughen it to ensure a proper bond with the new concrete. A **20 mm – 40 mm key** is chiseled into the old concrete.
- **Step 5 (Formwork & Pouring):** Place reinforcement, erect formwork on both sides, and pour M20 (or higher) PCC/RCC.
- **Step 6 (Curing & Packing):** Cure for **7 to 14 days**, then dry-pack the gap between the old and new concrete with non-shrink grout to transfer load.
- **Step 7 (Repeat):** Move to the alternate skipped bays and repeat the process.

#### 2.1.3 Types of Under-Pinning (KTU Favourite — High Weightage)

| Type | Key Feature | Best Used When |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| **Mass Concrete Under-pinning** | Done in short pits; new concrete pressed against old footing | Shallow depth, low load, good access |
| **Beam and Base Under-pinning** | A needle beam transfers wall load to new mass concrete bases on either side | Medium depth, brick walls |
| **Pile Under-pinning** | Mini-piles driven on either side, connected by a pile cap | Deep stable strata, restricted access |
| **Pile and Beam Under-pinning** | Piles + needle beams spanning between them | Very deep, large loads |
| **Cantilever Needle Beam** | Cantilever beams anchored into the existing floor/wall | Where external access is impossible |

#### 2.1.4 Safety Precautions in Under-Pinning (KTU Direct Question Source)
- Never excavate adjacent bays simultaneously.
- Continuous monitoring for cracks; install **tell-tales** and **crack gauges**.
- Maintain **dewatering** if water table is encountered.
- Stop work immediately on any abnormal noise or settlement.

---

### 2.2 SHORING — Systematic Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Objectives (Why we shore)
1. To **prevent collapse** of a wall that has lost its structural stability.
2. To provide **lateral support** to walls threatened by adjacent excavation or demolition.
3. To support walls during **cutting of openings** (doors, windows) in load-bearing walls.
4. To brace trenches, deep excavations, and adjacent structures.

#### 2.2.2 Three Principal Types of Shoring (Mandatory for KTU Board Exam)

**A. Raking Shores (Inclined Shores)**
Used when a wall bulges or leans outward. Inclined members called **rakers** (or shores proper) are placed against the wall and anchored to the ground via **sole plates** and **needles**.

- Components: Rakers, needles, cleats, wall plate, sole plate, bracing (hoop iron or dogs).
- Angle of raker: 45° (gentle) to 75° (steep). Steeper angles support more vertical load; gentler angles support more lateral load.

**B. Flying Shores**
A *horizontal* shore used between **two parallel walls** of separate buildings, where neither building can be supported from the ground (e.g., a road lies between them).

- Components: Horizontal shore (beam), struts, needles, cleats, wall plates, folding wedges.
- Span range: Up to **9 m** (timber); up to **30 m** (steel flying shores).
- Used only when both walls are independently stable vertically.

**C. Dead Shores (Vertical Shores)**
A purely **vertical** shore used to support a wall that is being reconstructed at the ground level, or to support a floor/roof load temporarily while a wall is removed.

- Components: Vertical posts (dead shores), needles, props, sole plates, folding wedges, braces.
- Also called **needle shoring**.

#### 2.2.3 Construction Sequence of a Raking Shore (Most Asked)

1. Identify the wall requiring support.
2. Fix a **wall plate** horizontally across the wall using hooks and cleats.
3. Insert **needles** at 1 m – 1.5 m vertical spacing, penetrating into the wall by 75 mm – 100 mm.
4. Place **rakers** against the needles, with their foot on a **sole plate** on the ground.
5. Use **hoop iron or dogs** to fasten the raker to the needle.
6. Drive **folding wedges** between raker and sole plate to tighten the shore.
7. Provide **lateral bracing** to prevent buckling of rakers in their weak direction.

---

### 2.3 KTU FORMULA SHEET / CHEAT SHEET

| # | Concept | Equation / Condition | Meaning / Unit |
|---|---------|----------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Length of one under-pin pit (Bay) | $L_{pit} \le 1.5\ m$ | Maximum unsupported excavation length |
| 2 | Maximum unsupported wall length at any stage | $L_{wall} \le 1.2\ m$ | To prevent wall collapse |
| 3 | Angle of raking shore with horizontal | $45^\circ \le \theta \le 75^\circ$ | Trade-off between vertical/lateral support |
| 4 | Vertical spacing of needles in raking shore | $S_v = 1.0\ m\ to\ 1.5\ m$ | Standard construction spacing |
| 5 | Length of needle embedment in wall | $e \ge 75\ mm\ to\ 100\ mm$ | For load transfer |
| 6 | Minimum section of raker (timber) | $b \times d$ where $b \ge 100\ mm$, $d \ge 150\ mm$ | Practical size per IS 401 |
| 7 | Span of flying shore (timber) | $S_{fly} \le 9\ m$ | Beyond this, use steel |
| 8 | Bearing pressure on sole plate | $q \le q_{allow}$ of soil | Soil capacity check |
| 9 | Soil bearing pressure under sole plate | $q = \dfrac{P}{A_{sole}}$ | kN/m² |
| 10 | Folding wedge tightening ratio (slope) | 1 in 6 to 1 in 10 | Standard wedge taper |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Most students lose marks by writing only *types* without giving the *construction sequence* or *components*. Always list at least **5 components** of any shore and **3 sequential steps** of construction to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Analytical Design Check: Soil Bearing Pressure Under a Sole Plate

When a raking shore transmits load to the ground, the **bearing pressure on the soil** under the sole plate must not exceed the allowable bearing capacity. This is a classic 7-mark derivation in KTU.

#### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
A raking shore of length $L$ makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. The wall exerts a horizontal thrust $H$ on the shore at the needle level.

The raker transfers this thrust along its axis to the sole plate.

#### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Resolve the wall thrust $H$ along the raker axis:**

The component of $H$ along the raker axis (i.e., the axial force in the raker) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{raker} &= \frac{H}{\cos\theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **compressive axial force** that travels down the inclined raker and is delivered onto the sole plate at the ground.

**Step 2 — Resolve $F_{raker}$ into vertical and horizontal components at the sole plate:**

The force arriving at the sole plate can be resolved as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{sole} &= F_{raker} \cdot \sin\theta = H \cdot \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = H \tan\theta \\[6pt]
H_{sole} &= F_{raker} \cdot \cos\theta = H
\end{aligned}
$$

The **vertical component** $V_{sole}$ pushes the sole plate downward into the soil — this is the critical action that the soil must resist via bearing.

**Step 3 — Calculate the bearing pressure on the soil:**

If the sole plate has a rectangular contact area $B \times D$ with the soil, the bearing pressure is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{soil} &= \frac{V_{sole}}{B \cdot D} = \frac{H \tan\theta}{B \cdot D}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the safety check (Design Criterion):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{soil} &\le q_{allowable} \\[4pt]
\frac{H \tan\theta}{B \cdot D} &\le q_{allowable}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the required sole plate area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B \cdot D &\ge \frac{H \tan\theta}{q_{allowable}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the analytical design result: a KTU student should be able to compute the minimum sole plate dimensions from this inequality.

#### 3.1.3 Numerical Illustration (Typical KTU 14-Mark Sub-Question)

**Given:**
- Horizontal thrust on wall, $H = 60\ kN$
- Angle of raker with horizontal, $\theta = 60^\circ$
- Allowable soil bearing pressure, $q_{allow} = 150\ kN/m^2$
- Sole plate is square, $B = D$

**Find:** Minimum side of square sole plate.

**Solution:**

Vertical force on sole plate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{sole} &= H \tan\theta \\[4pt]
&= 60 \times \tan 60^\circ \\[4pt]
&= 60 \times 1.732 \\[4pt]
&= 103.92\ kN
\end{aligned}
$$

Required sole plate area:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\ge \frac{V_{sole}}{q_{allow}} = \frac{103.92}{150} = 0.6928\ m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

For a square plate, side length $s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &\ge \sqrt{0.6928} = 0.832\ m \approx 0.85\ m
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Adopt** a standard $900\ mm \times 900\ mm$ sole plate in practice for safety. The derivation above is the **exact expected KTU answer format**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Check: Number of Needles Required for a Given Wall Length

For a raking shore system on a wall of length $L_w$ and height $H_w$:

**Step 1 — Vertical spacing of needles (from KTU Code):** $S_v = 1.0\ m\ to\ 1.5\ m$. Take $S_v = 1.25\ m$ as average.

**Step 2 — Required number of needles:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{needles} &= \frac{H_w}{S_v} + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

The "+1" accounts for the needle placed at the top of the wall (or first needle at floor level).

**Step 3 — Example:** For a 6 m tall wall with $S_v = 1.25\ m$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{needles} &= \frac{6}{1.25} + 1 = 4.8 + 1 = 5.8 \rightarrow \text{round up to } 6\ needles
\end{aligned}
$$

Always round **up** — never reduce the number of needles, as it is a safety system.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Diagrams:** Because under-pinning and shoring are *physical construction systems* with depth and 3D geometry, the most legible representation in a typed KTU answer is a **labelled block-architecture diagram** (which KTU examiners credit equally to a sketch). Mermaid is used here for clean schematic clarity.

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Process Flow of Mass Concrete Under-Pinning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START Survey of Cracked Wall] --> B[Soil Investigation and Sounding]
    B --> C[Temporary Needle Beam Support on Wall]
    C --> D[Excavate Bay 1 - Length less than 1.5 m]
    D --> E[Clean Underside and Chisel Key in Old Concrete]
    E --> F[Place Reinforcement and Erect Formwork]
    F --> G[Pour M20 PCC and Compact]
    G --> H[Cure for 7 to 14 Days]
    H --> I[Dry Pack Gap with Non Shrink Grout]
    I --> J[Transfer Load from Old to New Foundation]
    J --> K[Repeat Process for Bay 2 and Bay 3]
    K --> L[Remove Needle Beams After Curing]
    L --> M[END Foundation Restored]

    style A fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b30000
    style M fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#0f7a1f
    style C fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a37200
    style J fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003a7a
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each block represents a *stage of work*. The yellow block (temporary support) and the blue block (load transfer) are the two most critical safety milestones in under-pinning.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Raking Shore

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[UNSAFE WALL] --> N[NEEDLES at 1.0 to 1.5 m vertical spacing]
    N --> WP[WALL PLATE horizontal on wall face]
    WP --> R[RAKERS inclined 45 to 75 degrees]
    R --> F[FOLDING WEDGES for tightening]
    F --> SP[SOLE PLATE on firm ground]
    SP --> G[GROUND ANCHOR - soil reaction]
    R -.-> B[HOOP IRON and DOG BRACING]
    B -.-> R
    N -.-> C[CLEATS fixing needles to wall]
    C -.-> W
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **solid arrows** trace the **load path** from the unsafe wall down to the ground. The **dotted arrows** represent the *bracing and fixing connections* that prevent buckling and slippage. KTU examiners expect students to identify the **complete load path** in a diagram.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Selection Logic for Shoring Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[START Identify Problem] --> Q{Is the structure threatened by adjacent activity?}
    Q -- YES, ground access available --> RS[Use RAKING SHORE]
    Q -- YES, two parallel walls and no ground access --> FS[Use FLYING SHORE]
    Q -- YES, ground floor opening or wall removal --> DS[Use DEAD SHORE]
    Q -- NO, foundation issue only --> UP[Use UNDER PINNING method]

    style RS fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003a7a
    style FS fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003a7a
    style DS fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003a7a
    style UP fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a37200
    style P fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b30000
```

**Reading the diagram:** A KTU student answering a *case-study* question can use this exact decision-tree format to determine the correct shore type from a real-world problem statement.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
Define under-pinning. List any three situations in which under-pinning becomes necessary.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Under-pinning is the process of providing additional or new foundation support beneath an existing building, either to deepen it to a stronger soil stratum or to widen it for load redistribution, without disturbing the superstructure.

**Three situations (2 marks):**
1. When the bearing capacity of the existing soil is found to be insufficient for new loading conditions (e.g., building extension).
2. When a deep excavation is planned adjacent to an existing structure, threatening its foundation.
3. When the existing foundation has deteriorated due to ageing, chemical attack, or seismic activity.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
What is a flying shore? State any two situations where flying shores are adopted.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A flying shore is a *horizontal* temporary support system erected between two parallel walls of separate buildings when neither building can be supported from the ground (typically because of a road, lane, or open space between them).

**Two situations (2 marks):**
1. When two adjacent buildings require mutual lateral support, but a street or access lane separates them.
2. When one building is to be demolished or altered, and the facing wall of the neighbouring building must be temporarily held from collapsing.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each 14-mark question has an **internal choice** — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 2]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction procedure of a **raking shore**. List its essential components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **mass concrete method of under-pinning** in detail, including its construction sequence and safety precautions. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction of a Raking Shore (7 marks):**

A *raking shore* consists of inclined timber members (rakers) that transmit the lateral thrust of an unsafe wall to a firm ground anchor. The construction sequence is as follows:

1. **Wall Plate Fixing:** A horizontal *wall plate* (typically 200 mm × 50 mm) is fixed against the wall face at the level of the uppermost floor, secured using **hooks, cleats, and dogs** driven into the wall.
2. **Needle Insertion:** *Needles* (square timber sections, ~100 mm × 100 mm) are inserted through pre-drilled holes in the wall at vertical spacings of **1.0 m to 1.5 m**, projecting about 100 mm beyond the wall face.
3. **Raker Placement:** *Rakers* (inclined shores, typically 100 mm × 150 mm) are placed with one end resting on the wall plate/needle and the other end on a *sole plate* on the ground. The angle of the raker is kept between **45° and 75°** with the horizontal.
4. **Sole Plate and Wedges:** The foot of the raker rests on a horizontal *sole plate* (300 mm × 300 mm or larger) laid on a bed of compacted earth. **Folding wedges** are driven between the raker foot and the sole plate to tighten the system.
5. **Bracing:** The rakers are tied together using **hoop iron straps or dog braces** to prevent buckling in the weak direction.

**Essential Components (any 5 to be named):**
- Rakers, needles, cleats, wall plate, sole plate, folding wedges, hoop iron, dogs, braces.

---

**(b) Mass Concrete Method of Under-Pinning (7 marks):**

**Principle:** This is the simplest and oldest method of under-pinning. Short, alternate *pits* are excavated beneath the existing footing, filled with mass concrete, and the load is gradually transferred to the new concrete via dry packing.

**Construction Sequence:**

1. **Preliminary Support:** The affected wall portion is supported temporarily on **needle beams and props** resting on either side of the section to be underpinned. This eliminates any risk of wall collapse during excavation.
2. **Pit Excavation in Alternate Bays:** A pit of length **1.0 m – 1.5 m** is excavated below the existing footing, never exposing more than **1.2 m of wall length** at a time. Pits are taken to a depth where a stable, hard stratum is reached.
3. **Surface Preparation:** The underside of the existing footing is cleaned, and a key (notch) of 40 mm – 75 mm depth is chiseled into the old concrete to ensure mechanical interlock.
4. **Formwork and Pouring:** Formwork is erected on both sides of the pit, and M20 grade (or higher) mass concrete is poured up to a level just below the existing footing.
5. **Curing and Dry Packing:** The new concrete is cured for **7 to 14 days**. The remaining small gap (about 75 mm) between the new concrete and the old footing is tightly *dry-packed* with a stiff cement-sand mortar (1:2) using a hammer and flat chisel, achieving immediate load transfer.
6. **Repeat in Adjacent Bays:** The process is repeated in the previously skipped alternate bays until the entire affected length is underpinned.

**Safety Precautions:**
- Never excavate adjacent bays simultaneously.
- Use *tell-tales* (glass plates across cracks) and *crack gauges* to monitor existing cracks.
- Dewater continuously if the water table is high.
- Stop work on the slightest sign of distress.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 2]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **raking shores, flying shores, and dead shores** in a tabular form, listing at least **four points of comparison**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A raking shore of length 4 m is placed at an angle of 60° with the horizontal. The wall exerts a horizontal thrust of 50 kN. The soil allowable bearing capacity is 120 kN/m². Design a **square sole plate**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Basis | Raking Shore | Flying Shore | Dead Shore |
|-------|--------------|--------------|------------|
| **Orientation** | Inclined (45°–75°) | Horizontal | Vertical |
| **Position** | Between wall and ground | Between two parallel walls | Below the wall to be supported |
| **Use** | Wall bulging or leaning outward | Two adjacent buildings needing mutual support | Wall reconstruction at ground level |
| **Main Member** | Rakers (inclined shores) | Horizontal shore / strut | Vertical posts (dead shores) |
| **Sole Plate** | Required at ground | Not required | Required at ground |
| **Span Range** | Limited by ground distance | Up to 9 m (timber) / 30 m (steel) | Limited by wall height |

*(Any 4 well-explained points = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Sole Plate Design (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Raker length, $L = 4\ m$
- Angle with horizontal, $\theta = 60^\circ$
- Horizontal wall thrust, $H = 50\ kN$
- Allowable soil bearing pressure, $q_{allow} = 120\ kN/m^2$

**Step 1 — Axial compressive force in the raker:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{raker} &= \frac{H}{\cos\theta} = \frac{50}{\cos 60^\circ} = \frac{50}{0.5} = 100\ kN
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Vertical force on the sole plate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{sole} &= F_{raker} \sin\theta = 100 \times \sin 60^\circ = 100 \times 0.866 = 86.6\ kN
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Required sole plate area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{req} &= \frac{V_{sole}}{q_{allow}} = \frac{86.6}{120} = 0.7217\ m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Side of square sole plate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= \sqrt{0.7217} = 0.849\ m \approx 0.85\ m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Adopt a square sole plate of side **900 mm × 900 mm** (standard practical size) for adequate safety.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing raking vs. flying shores:** Many students write "raking shore" when the question is about a *horizontal* shore. Always check the position — *horizontal between two buildings = flying shore*.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1" needle count:** When asked "how many needles for a 5 m wall at 1.25 m spacing", students write $5/1.25 = 4$. The correct answer is $4 + 1 = 5$ needles. **Always add 1** for the first/top needle.
> 3. **Skipping the temporary needle beam step in under-pinning:** The first action must *always* be to provide temporary support. Examiners deduct 1 mark if this step is missing.
> 4. **Wrong units in design problems:** Soil bearing capacity is in **kN/m²**, force in **kN**, and area in **m²**. Mixing units is the single most common calculation error.
> 5. **Not mentioning "dry packing":** The dry-pack step is what *transfers* the load from old footing to new concrete. Without it, your under-pin does no useful work. Examiners look for this word specifically.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam:**

- **Under-Pinning Definition:** Strengthening existing foundation from below, in *alternate bays*, *without disturbing the superstructure*.
- **Maximum pit length:** **1.0 m – 1.5 m**.
- **Maximum unsupported wall length:** **1.2 m**.
- **Concrete grade used:** Minimum **M20 PCC**.
- **Curing period:** **7 – 14 days** before dry packing.
- **Types of under-pinning:** Mass concrete, Beam & base, Pile, Pile & beam, Cantilever needle beam.
- **Shoring Definition:** Temporary support to unsafe wall/structure.
- **Raking Shore:** Inclined (45°–75°), between wall and ground.
- **Flying Shore:** Horizontal, between *two parallel walls*.
- **Dead Shore:** Vertical, used during wall removal/reconstruction.
- **Needle spacing:** **1.0 m – 1.5 m vertical**; embedment into wall **≥ 75 mm**.
- **Sole plate function:** Distributes raker load over soil.
- **Bearing pressure formula:** $q = \dfrac{H \tan\theta}{B \cdot D} \le q_{allow}$.
- **Standard wedge taper:** 1 in 6 to 1 in 10.
- **First step in any under-pin:** **Always — provide temporary needle beam support first.**
- **Monitoring tools:** Tell-tales, crack gauges, plumb lines, surveying points.
- **When a structure is threatened by ground access blockage → Flying shore.**
- **When a structure's foundation is the issue → Under-pinning.**
- **Always round UP number of needles — never round down.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working at Height - Use of Ladders and Scaffolds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT526 - SAFETY IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Working at Height - Use of Ladders and Scaffolds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Working at Height** refers to any work activity where a worker is exposed to a risk of falling from a height of **2 metres (6 feet) or more** above ground level, or where the worker can fall into an opening, hole, or dangerous substance below. The provision of suitable access equipment — primarily **ladders** and **scaffolds** — forms the cornerstone of fall protection strategy in construction projects governed by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** and the **Building & Other Construction Workers (Regulation of Employment and Conditions of Service) Act, 1996**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> "A *ladder* is a portable, fixed, or mobile vertical/inclined structure consisting of two parallel side rails joined at regular intervals by rungs or steps, used for ascending or descending between different levels. A *scaffold* is a temporary elevated platform and its supporting structure used for supporting workers, materials, or both, during the construction, alteration, demolition, or maintenance of a structure."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Mountain Climber's Toolkit"**
> Think of a construction site as a vertical mountain that workers must climb repeatedly to perform tasks (plastering, painting, brick-laying, electrical wiring). Just as a mountaineer uses two tools — a **ladder** (a single-file, quick, light tool for short, point-to-point ascents) and a **rope-and-platform system** (a broad, stable, load-sharing tool for camping/work stations at height) — a construction worker uses **ladders** for short-duration, low-load access (≤30 minutes, light tools) and **scaffolds** for prolonged work involving multiple workers, heavy materials, and large work-areas.
>
> **The Golden Rule:** Use a **ladder when the job is like picking an apple from a tree** (quick, one person, light load). Use a **scaffold when the job is like painting the entire wall of a house** (prolonged, multi-person, materials needed).

## 1.3 Key Physical & Regulatory Constants

The following constants and threshold values are **statutory** and must be memorized for KTU examinations:

| Parameter | Threshold Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Fall Height Threshold** | **≥ 2.0 m (≈ 6 ft)** | BOCW Act, 1996 |
| Ladder Side-Rail Strength | **≥ 1.27 kN (130 kgf)** | IS 3691 (Part 1) |
| Scaffold Design Load (Light Duty) | **1.5 kN/m² (≈ 150 kg/m²)** | IS 3696 (Part 1) |
| Scaffold Design Load (Medium Duty) | **2.5 kN/m² (≈ 250 kg/m²)** | IS 3696 (Part 1) |
| Scaffold Design Load (Heavy Duty) | **3.75 kN/m² (≈ 375 kg/m²)** | IS 3696 (Part 1) |
| Ladder Inclination Angle | **75° (1:4 ratio)** | IS 3691 / OSHA |
| Max. Height without Rest Platform (Ladder) | **6 m** | IS 3691 (Part 2) |
| Toe-Board Height (Scaffold) | **≥ 150 mm** | IS 3696 |
| Top Guard-Rail Height | **900 mm – 1100 mm** | IS 3696 / OSHA |
| Ladder Rung Spacing | **250 mm – 300 mm** | IS 3691 |
| Min. Ladder Width (Clear) | **300 mm** | IS 3691 |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Ladders

Ladders are systematically classified as follows (this taxonomy is **frequently asked** in KTU Part A questions):

### A. Based on Material of Construction
1. **Wooden Ladders** — Made of straight-grained seasoned timber (typically teak, deodar). Use only single-piece rails; splicing not permitted. Rungs are mortised into rails (not nailed). Suitable for low-voltage electrical work (non-conductive).
2. **Metal/Aluminium Ladders** — Lightweight, high strength, but **electrically conductive** — therefore **PROHIBITED near live electrical circuits**. Anodized for corrosion resistance.
3. **Fibre-Glass (FRP) Ladders** — Non-conductive, weather-resistant, ideal for electrical and chemical exposure work. Highest cost but safest in mixed-hazard environments.

### B. Based on Type / Configuration
1. **Single Ladder (Straight Ladder)** — One section, non-adjustable, leans against a support. Length typically ≤ **6 m**.
2. **Extension Ladder** — Two or more sliding sections with locking dogs. Maximum extended length ≤ **15 m** (with overlap ≥ 1.5 m for 2-section ladders).
3. **Step Ladder (A-Frame / Self-Supporting)** — A-shaped, hinged at top, opens out and stands independently with no wall support. **Maximum safe height ≈ 6 m.**
4. **Mobile Ladder (Roller Ladder / Platform Ladder)** — Mounted on wheels with a top working platform. Wheels must have locking brakes when in use.
5. **Trestle Ladder** — A pair of ladders hinged at top, forming an A-frame, used to support scaffold planks (NOT for free-standing access).
6. **Fixed Ladder** — Permanently attached to a structure (e.g., on towers, chimneys, ship's ladders). Must have a **safety cage** if height > 3 m (OSHA) or a **fall-arrest system** (BIS / KTU preferred).

## 2.2 Classification of Scaffolds

### A. Based on Support Structure
1. **Independent / Brick-Layer's Scaffold** — Has two rows of standards (vertical members); one row close to the wall, another row at a distance of about **1.2 m – 1.5 m**. Used for brick masonry.
2. **Single-Pole / Putlog Scaffold** — Has only one row of standards; the working platform is supported on putlogs (horizontal members) inserted into the wall. Used where wall is being built simultaneously.
3. **Suspended Scaffold (Swinging Scaffold)** — Working platform suspended from roof by ropes/chains. Used for high-rise painting/cladding. Examples: **Boatswain's Chair, Cradle, Gondola**.
4. **Cantilever Scaffold (Outtrigger Scaffold)** — Standards supported on needles (cantilever beams) projecting out of a wall or floor. Used where ground cannot support scaffolding.
5. **Trestle Scaffold** — Platform supported on movable tripods or ladders. Used for indoor low-height work (≤ 5 m).
6. **Tubular / Cuplock / System Scaffold** — Modular steel scaffold using standardized tubes (48.3 mm OD) and couplers. Industry standard for modern construction.
7. **Mobile Scaffold** — Tubular scaffold mounted on castor wheels with locking brakes.

### B. Based on Duty Class (Load-Bearing Capacity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite — Duty Classification of Scaffolds:**
> - **Light Duty:** 1.5 kN/m² — Inspection, painting, electrical work, plastering.
> - **Medium Duty:** 2.5 kN/m² — Bricklaying, tiling, general construction.
> - **Heavy Duty:** 3.75 kN/m² — Stone masonry, heavy material stacking.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H_{ladder} \leq 6\,\text{m}$ | Maximum height of single ladder without rest platform | Ladder selection |
| 2 | $\theta_{ladder} = 75°$ | Inclination angle with horizontal (1 Horizontal : 4 Vertical) | Safe ladder placement |
| 3 | $L_{overlap} = \frac{L_{extended}}{4}$ or $\geq 1.5\,\text{m}$ | Minimum overlap for extension ladder sections | Extension ladder use |
| 4 | $P_{uniform} = \dfrac{W_{total}}{A_{platform}}$ | Distributed load check (must be ≤ design duty) | Scaffold load verification |
| 5 | $F_{design} = 1.5 \times W_{expected}$ | Factor of safety for scaffold design (BIS recommends 1.5 – 2.0) | Scaffold sizing |
| 6 | $D_{stagger} \geq 1.5\,\text{m}$ | Vertical staggering between putlog joints | Putlog scaffold design |
| 7 | $H_{guard-rail} = 900\,\text{mm to 1100}\,\text{mm}$ | Top guard-rail height above platform | Fall protection |
| 8 | $H_{toeboard} \geq 150\,\text{mm}$ | Toe-board height at platform edge | Tool/material fall protection |
| 9 | $\sigma_{rail} = \dfrac{M_{max} \cdot y}{I} \leq \sigma_{allowable}$ | Bending stress check for ladder rails | Ladder strength check |
| 10 | $R_{bracing} = \dfrac{P_{vertical}}{\sin\alpha}$ | Reaction in diagonal bracing of scaffold | Scaffold stability |

> **Note on Notation:** $W$ = total load, $A$ = platform area, $M_{max}$ = max. bending moment, $I$ = moment of inertia, $y$ = distance to extreme fibre, $\sigma$ = stress, $\alpha$ = brace angle with horizontal, $P$ = vertical load.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ladder-and-scaffold system forms the **skeleton of every vertical construction project** — from low-rise residential buildings to high-rise towers, industrial chimneys, and bridge piers. The selection logic between a ladder and a scaffold is not arbitrary; it is governed by three engineering parameters: **(i) Duration of work, (ii) Number of workers, (iii) Magnitude of loads/tools**. Mis-selection results in **falls-from-height (FFH) accidents**, which account for **~32% of all construction fatalities** in India as per the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 4)**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ladder Inclination Geometry (1:4 Rule)

**Problem Statement:** Show that the safe inclination angle for a ladder placed against a wall is **75°** with the horizontal, and derive the **1:4 (H:V) base-to-height ratio** recommended by IS 3691.

**Given:**
- Ladder of total length $L$ rests against a vertical wall.
- Let $h$ = vertical height at the contact point on the wall.
- Let $b$ = horizontal distance from wall to the foot of the ladder.
- Ladder is in static equilibrium under self-weight, normal reactions, and friction at the base.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The ladder makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal ground. By simple trigonometry, the contact height and base distance are related as:

$$
\tan\theta = \dfrac{h}{b}
$$

The classical ladder-equilibrium problem (Coulomb friction model) shows that for a ladder of length $L$ and weight $W$ acting at its mid-point, with coefficient of static friction $\mu$ at the base, the ladder will be on the verge of slipping when:

$$
\tan\theta_{slip} = \dfrac{1}{2\mu}
$$

Assuming a typical coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.30$ (rubber sole on dry concrete — a conservative field value):

$$
\tan\theta_{slip} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times 0.30} = \dfrac{1}{0.60} = 1.667
$$

Taking the inverse tangent:

$$
\theta_{slip} = \tan^{-1}(1.667) \approx 59.0°
$$

This is the **slip angle** — the angle below which the ladder will slip outward. The **safe angle** must be **greater** than this slip angle, but not so steep that the ladder tips backward. The empirically validated safe range, confirmed by OSHA, IS 3691, and international codes, is:

$$
\theta_{safe} \in \left[70°,\ 80°\right] \quad\Rightarrow\quad \theta_{optimum} = 75°
$$

Converting the optimum angle to the famous **1:4 base-to-height ratio**:

$$
\tan(75°) = \dfrac{h}{b} = 3.732 \approx \dfrac{4}{1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{b}{h} = \dfrac{1}{4}}
$$

That is, for every **1 unit of horizontal base distance**, the ladder should rise **4 units of vertical height** — the universally quoted "**4-to-1 rule**" or "**1-in-4 rule**".

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** If the ladder is 5 m long and reaches a height of 4 m on the wall, the foot should be placed at $b = 4/4 = 1$ m from the wall. This places the ladder at exactly $\tan^{-1}(4/1) = 76°$ — within the safe range. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Scaffold Load Verification (Bending Stress Check on Standard)

**Problem Statement:** A **putlog scaffold** has standards (vertical tubes) of outer diameter **48.3 mm** and wall thickness **4.0 mm**, spaced **2.0 m apart longitudinally** and **1.5 m apart transversely**. The scaffold is rated **medium duty (2.5 kN/m²)**. Verify if the scaffold is safe, and compute the maximum allowable height of the scaffold.

**Given Data:**

$$
d_o = 48.3\,\text{mm},\quad t = 4.0\,\text{mm},\quad \sigma_{allow} = 165\,\text{MPa},\quad P_{design} = 2.5\,\text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 1: Compute Cross-Sectional Properties of Standard.**

Inner diameter:

$$
d_i = d_o - 2t = 48.3 - 2(4.0) = 40.3\,\text{mm}
$$

Moment of Inertia:

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}\left(d_o^4 - d_i^4\right)
$$

$$
d_o^4 = (48.3)^4 = 5{,}442{,}829.85\,\text{mm}^4
$$

$$
d_i^4 = (40.3)^4 = 2{,}637{,}134.45\,\text{mm}^4
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}\left(5{,}442{,}829.85 - 2{,}637{,}134.45\right)
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi}{64} \times 2{,}805{,}695.40
$$

$$
I = 137{,}733.5\,\text{mm}^4
$$

Cross-sectional area:

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(d_o^2 - d_i^2\right) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(48.3^2 - 40.3^2\right)
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(2332.89 - 1624.09\right) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 708.80 = 556.65\,\text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 2: Compute Load per Standard.**

Area of platform supported per standard:

$$
A_{plat} = 2.0 \times 1.5 = 3.0\,\text{m}^2
$$

Vertical load per standard (using design duty):

$$
P = P_{design} \times A_{plat} = 2.5 \times 3.0 = 7.5\,\text{kN}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Factor of Safety (FOS = 1.5) for dynamic and impact loads.**

$$
P_{factored} = 1.5 \times 7.5 = 11.25\,\text{kN}
$$

**Step 4: Stress Check on the Standard (Axial Load + Eccentricity).**

Assuming the standard is also subjected to a small bending moment due to imperfect verticality ($M \approx 0.05 \cdot P \cdot L$, per IS 3696 guidelines), the maximum height of the standard is governed by:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{P_{factored}}{A} + \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I} \leq \sigma_{allow}
$$

For a preliminary axial-only check:

$$
\sigma_{axial} = \dfrac{11{,}250}{556.65} = 20.21\,\text{N/mm}^2
$$

Comparing with allowable:

$$
\sigma_{axial} = 20.21\,\text{MPa} \ll 165\,\text{MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Maximum Allowable Height (Euler Buckling Check).**

Effective length factor (fixed at base, pinned at top): $K = 0.85$ (per IS 800:2007).

$$
P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{(K \cdot L)^2}
$$

Assuming $E = 2.0 \times 10^5\,\text{N/mm}^2$ for structural steel:

$$
L_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{K} \sqrt{\dfrac{E \cdot I}{P_{cr}}}
$$

Setting $P_{cr} = 1.5 \times P_{factored} = 1.5 \times 11{,}250 = 16{,}875\,\text{N}$:

$$
L_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{0.85} \sqrt{\dfrac{2.0 \times 10^5 \times 137{,}733.5}{16{,}875}}
$$

$$
L_{max} = 3.695 \times \sqrt{1{,}632{,}115.5}
$$

$$
L_{max} = 3.695 \times 1277.55 = 4{,}720.6\,\text{mm} \approx 4.72\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 6: Number of Lifts.**

With a standard lift height of 2.0 m and a base-lift of 2.0 m:

$$
\boxed{N_{lifts} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{4.72}{2.0} \right\rfloor = 2\,\text{lifts (max scaffold height} \approx 4\,\text{m)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The scaffold is **safe at a height of up to ~4.0 m** under medium-duty loading with the given section. For greater heights, either reduce standard spacing, increase tube diameter, or use a heavier section.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Ladder vs. Scaffold

| Decision Parameter | Ladder | Scaffold |
|---|---|---|
| **Typical Use** | Short-duration, point access | Prolonged, area-wide work |
| **Duration Limit** | ≤ 30 min of continuous use | Hours to months |
| **Number of Workers** | 1 person only | 1 – 6+ workers simultaneously |
| **Load Capacity** | ~ 100 – 150 kgf (worker + tools) | 1.5 – 3.75 kN/m² distributed |
| **Fall Protection** | Inherently insecure; relies on user balance | Top-rail, mid-rail, toe-board provided |
| **Setup Complexity** | Minimal (place & climb) | Requires design, erection, inspection |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate to High |
| **Compliance** | IS 3691, OSHA 1910.23 | IS 3696, OSHA 1926.451 |
| **Platform Size** | 1-foot width | Full work-area platform (≥ 600 mm wide) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Differentiate between a *step ladder* and an *extension ladder*. State any two IS-code-based safety precautions for ladder use.**

**Model Answer:**

A **step ladder** is a self-supporting, A-frame ladder with two side rails hinged at the top, which opens out to form an A-shape and stands independently without any wall support. It is typically used for low-height tasks (≤ 6 m) and has flat steps (not rungs) for comfortable footing.

An **extension ladder** is a non-self-supporting, straight ladder consisting of two or more sliding sections that can be extended to greater heights (up to 15 m). It must be leaned against a wall or other rigid support, and locking dogs (pawls) hold the sections in place.

**Two IS 3691 safety precautions:**
1. The ladder must be placed at an angle of **75° (1:4 base-to-height ratio)** with the horizontal.
2. Ladder side rails must be of uniform cross-section, free from knots and splinters, and able to withstand a horizontal pull of **1.27 kN (130 kgf)** at mid-span without failure.
3. (Bonus) When the ladder length exceeds **6 m**, intermediate rest platforms must be provided every 6 m.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**List the three duty-classes of scaffolds as per IS 3696 and state their respective uniformly distributed load (UDL) capacities.**

**Model Answer:**

As per **IS 3696 (Part 1): Safety Code for Scaffolds and Ladders**, scaffolds are classified into three duty classes based on their uniformly distributed load-bearing capacity:

| Duty Class | UDL Capacity | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Light Duty** | **1.5 kN/m² (150 kg/m²)** | Inspection, painting, electrical work, plastering |
| **Medium Duty** | **2.5 kN/m² (250 kg/m²)** | Brick-laying, tiling, general construction |
| **Heavy Duty** | **3.75 kN/m² (375 kg/m²)** | Stone masonry, heavy material stacking |

A minimum factor of safety of **1.5 – 2.0** is recommended in scaffold design to account for dynamic, impact, and overload conditions.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT skip numerical values** in safety questions — examiners award 1 mark per correct numerical fact (e.g., 75°, 2 m, 900 mm, 150 mm). Vague answers like "proper height" fetch **zero marks**.
> - Always cite the **IS Code** number along with the rule (e.g., "IS 3691 — 75° inclination"). IS code references are valued at **1 mark each**.
> - In scaffold diagrams, **label** the standards, ledgers, transoms, and bracing. Unlabelled diagrams get partial credit only.
> - For load calculations, **state the FOS explicitly** (typically 1.5). Omission of FOS is the **#1 mark-deduction error** reported by KTU valuators.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the classification of ladders based on material and type. State at least **five** important safety precautions to be observed while using a ladder at a construction site.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel tubular scaffold is to be erected for a 6 m high brickwork. The standards are spaced at **2.5 m longitudinally** and **1.5 m transversely**. Each standard is a steel tube of **48.3 mm OD** and **4.5 mm thickness**, with an allowable stress of **165 MPa**. Compute the safe working load per standard, the maximum height the standard can support without bracing, and the number of lifts required.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Classification of Ladders:**

**A. Based on Material of Construction:**
1. **Wooden Ladders:** Made from straight-grained, seasoned, and treated timber (e.g., teak, deodar). Non-conductive, hence safe for electrical work. Rails must be in one piece — splicing is forbidden. Rungs are mortised into the rails (not nailed or screwed) for strength.
2. **Aluminium / Metal Ladders:** Lightweight, high strength-to-weight ratio, but **electrically conductive**. Therefore, **prohibited near live electrical installations** as per IS 3691. Anodized to prevent corrosion.
3. **Fibre-Glass (FRP) Ladders:** Non-conductive, weather-resistant, immune to most chemicals. Most expensive but safest in mixed-hazard environments.

**B. Based on Type:**
1. **Single (Straight) Ladder** — One section, non-adjustable, ≤ 6 m, leans against a support.
2. **Extension Ladder** — Two or more sliding sections, with overlap ≥ 1.5 m, total extended length ≤ 15 m.
3. **Step Ladder (A-frame)** — Self-supporting, hinged at top, ≤ 6 m, with flat steps.
4. **Mobile Ladder** — Wheel-mounted, with locking brakes, used in warehouses.
5. **Trestle Ladder** — Hinged A-frame, used to support scaffold planks.
6. **Fixed Ladder** — Permanently attached to a structure (towers, silos, ships).

**Five Safety Precautions:**
1. Place the ladder at an angle of **75° (1 horizontal : 4 vertical)**.
2. Ladder must extend at least **1 m (3 rungs) above the landing point**.
3. Secure the top of the ladder (tie-off) and the foot (anti-slip mat or shoe).
4. **One person at a time** on a ladder; do not carry heavy/bulky loads while climbing.
5. Inspect the ladder **daily before use** using a colour-tag system (Green / Yellow / Red).
6. (Bonus) Do not use metal ladders near live electrical circuits.
7. (Bonus) Replace any ladder with cracked rungs, split rails, or missing feet immediately.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- Longitudinal spacing: $L_s = 2.5\,\text{m}$
- Transverse spacing: $T_s = 1.5\,\text{m}$
- Outer diameter: $d_o = 48.3\,\text{mm}$
