- Wall thickness: $t = 4.5\,\text{mm}$
- Allowable stress: $\sigma_{allow} = 165\,\text{MPa}$
- Brickwork height: $H_{total} = 6.0\,\text{m}$
- Duty Class: Medium Duty (2.5 kN/m²) assumed for brick-laying.

**Step 1: Cross-Sectional Properties.**

$$
d_i = 48.3 - 2(4.5) = 39.3\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(48.3^2 - 39.3^2\right) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(2332.89 - 1544.49\right) = 619.55\,\text{mm}^2
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}\left(48.3^4 - 39.3^4\right) = \dfrac{\pi}{64}\left(5{,}442{,}830 - 2{,}384{,}117\right) = 150{,}267\,\text{mm}^4
$$

**Step 2: Safe Working Load per Standard (Axial Stress Check).**

$$
P_{safe} = \sigma_{allow} \times A = 165 \times 619.55 = 102{,}225.75\,\text{N} \approx 102.23\,\text{kN}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Factor of Safety (FOS = 1.5).**

$$
\boxed{P_{working} = \dfrac{102.23}{1.5} = 68.15\,\text{kN per standard}}
$$

**Step 4: Verify Against Expected UDL Load.**

Area supported per standard:

$$
A_{plat} = 2.5 \times 1.5 = 3.75\,\text{m}^2
$$

Expected load (medium duty, no FOS):

$$
P_{expected} = 2.5 \times 3.75 = 9.375\,\text{kN}
$$

Factored load:

$$
P_{factored} = 1.5 \times 9.375 = 14.06\,\text{kN}
$$

Since $P_{factored} = 14.06\,\text{kN} \ll P_{working} = 68.15\,\text{kN}$, the **axial stress check is satisfied with a large margin**. ✓

**Step 5: Euler Buckling Height.**

For a standard with effective length factor $K = 0.85$ (fixed-pinned), and $E = 2.0 \times 10^5\,\text{MPa}$:

$$
L_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{K} \sqrt{\dfrac{E \cdot I}{P_{cr}}}
$$

Using $P_{cr} = P_{factored} = 14{,}062.5\,\text{N}$:

$$
L_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{0.85} \sqrt{\dfrac{2.0 \times 10^5 \times 150{,}267}{14{,}062.5}}
$$

$$
L_{max} = 3.695 \times \sqrt{2{,}137{,}285.7} = 3.695 \times 1461.9 \approx 5{,}401\,\text{mm} \approx 5.4\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 6: Number of Lifts.**

With standard lift height of 2.0 m:

$$
\boxed{N_{lifts} = \left\lceil \dfrac{6.0}{2.0} \right\rceil = 3 \text{ lifts}}
$$

> Since $3 \times 2.0 = 6.0\,\text{m} \leq L_{max} = 5.4\,\text{m}$... wait — this is unsafe. Recheck using **uncoupled length per lift** (each lift is 2 m, so each segment length = 2 m < 5.4 m). Each **individual standard segment** must be designed for the full factored load. The unbraced length per segment is 2 m, which is **less than 5.4 m**, so the scaffold is **safe for a 6 m height with 3 lifts of 2 m each**, provided lateral bracing is provided at every lift.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the components of an **independent (brick-layer's) scaffold**. State the spacing standards recommended by IS 3696 for the standards, ledgers, and putlogs.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Colour-Coded Tagging System** for scaffold inspection. Describe the procedure for a **daily pre-use inspection** of a scaffold, including a checklist of at least **eight** items.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Components of an Independent (Brick-Layer's) Scaffold:**

(See the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 for a detailed schematic.)

An independent scaffold has two rows of **standards** (vertical members) — an inner row close to the wall (≈ 1.0 m) and an outer row at a distance of **1.2 m – 1.5 m** from the wall, connected by **ledgers** (longitudinal horizontals) and **transoms** (transverse horizontals). The working platform is supported on **putlogs** placed across the ledgers, with one end resting on the wall (inserted into a putlog hole in the brickwork) and the other on the ledger. Guard-rails (top + mid), toe-boards, and diagonal bracing complete the structure.

**Recommended Spacings (IS 3696):**

| Component | Recommended Spacing |
|---|---|
| Standards (longitudinal) | 2.0 – 2.5 m |
| Standards (transverse) | 1.2 – 1.5 m |
| Ledgers (vertical spacing) | 1.8 – 2.0 m |
| Transoms (transverse) | 1.2 – 1.5 m |
| Putlogs (centre-to-centre) | 1.2 – 1.5 m |
| Vertical staggering of putlog joints | ≥ 1.5 m |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Colour-Coded Tagging System for Scaffold Inspection:**

| Tag Colour | Status | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **🟢 GREEN** | Safe for Use | Recently inspected, complies with design; workers may enter. |
| **🟡 YELLOW** | Conditional Use | Modifications in progress; only trained workers with supervisor escort may enter. |
| **🔴 RED** | Unsafe — Do Not Use | Scaffold is incomplete, damaged, or under erection/dismantling; entry prohibited. |

The tag must be placed at **all access points** (ladders, stair towers) and must show the **date, time, inspector name, and next inspection due date**.

**Daily Pre-Use Inspection Checklist (8 items):**
1. **Base plates and sole boards** — properly seated, no settlement, no movement.
2. **Standards and ledgers** — no bent, dented, corroded, or cracked members.
3. **Couplers / clamps** — all tight, no missing couplers, correct torque applied.
4. **Working platform planks** — no splits, knots, oil stains; thickness ≥ 38 mm; securely supported.
5. **Guard-rails and mid-rails** — top rail at 900 – 1100 mm, mid-rail at ~ 450 mm, both secured.
6. **Toe-boards** — installed at all open platform edges, height ≥ 150 mm.
7. **Access ladder / stairs** — securely fixed, no missing rungs, extending 1 m above landing.
8. **Diagonal and plan bracing** — present at prescribed intervals, no missing braces.
9. (Bonus) **Tag system** — green tag displayed, current date, no defects logged from previous shift.
10. (Bonus) **Fall-arrest equipment** — where required, anchor points intact, harnesses inspected.

A competent person (per IS 3696 / OSHA 1926.451) must perform the inspection **before each work shift** and after any event that could affect structural integrity (rain, wind, accidental impact).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Flash Card — Memorize These 20 Points:**

- ☐ **Fall height threshold** for working-at-height regulations: **≥ 2.0 m** (BOCW Act, 1996).
- ☐ **1:4 base-to-height ratio** for ladder placement; safe angle **75°** with horizontal.
- ☐ Ladder must extend **1 m above the landing**.
- ☐ Maximum single-ladder length: **6 m** (intermediate rest platforms beyond this).
- ☐ Ladder side-rail strength test: **1.27 kN (130 kgf)** at mid-span per IS 3691.
- ☐ Three duty classes of scaffolds: **1.5 / 2.5 / 3.75 kN/m²** (light / medium / heavy).
- ☐ Factor of safety for scaffold design: **FOS = 1.5 – 2.0**.
- ☐ Toe-board minimum height: **150 mm**.
- ☐ Top guard-rail height: **900 mm – 1100 mm** (preferably 1000 mm).
- ☐ Working platform width: **≥ 600 mm** (minimum for 1 person) and **≥ 800 mm** for material stacking.
- ☐ Standard spacing (longitudinal / transverse): **2.0 – 2.5 m / 1.2 – 1.5 m**.
- ☐ Ledger vertical spacing: **1.8 – 2.0 m**.
- ☐ Diagonal bracing: every **3rd bay** vertically and at every **4th lift** in plan.
- ☐ Standard steel tube: **48.3 mm OD, 4.0 mm wall thickness** (IS 1161).
- ☐ Three scaffold tags: **Green (safe), Yellow (caution), Red (unsafe)**.
- ☐ Daily pre-use inspection by a **competent person** is mandatory.
- ☐ Wooden ladders: rungs **mortised**, not nailed; no splicing of rails.
- ☐ Metal ladders: **prohibited near electrical hazards**.
- ☐ Suspended scaffolds: require **two independent suspension ropes** (one safety, one working) per platform.
- ☐ Cantilever scaffolds: needle beams must be **anchored back into the structure** with adequate counter-weight or fixation.

---

**END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — WORKING AT HEIGHT (LADDERS & SCAFFOLDS)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tunnelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT526 - SAFETY IN CONSTRUCTION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Tunnelling # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Tunnelling ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Tunnelling** is a specialized branch of civil engineering that involves the underground excavation of passages through soil, rock, or other geomaterials to establish a continuous underground conduit for transportation (road/rail), water conveyance (hydro/hydropower), sewage disposal, utility services, or strategic defence purposes. The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) and the Indian Railway Standards (IRS) jointly govern the design and safety specifications of tunnels in India, especially under the purview of KTU 2024 Scheme's **PESFT526 – Safety in Construction** syllabus. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 2, tunnelling is taught not as a design subject but as a **high-hazard construction activity**. The emphasis is on hazard identification, risk control, occupational health, and the application of statutory safety regulations (Factories Act, Mines Act, IS 4756, IS 4081, and OSHA Subpart S). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ant Colony" Intuition Imagine a colony of ants building a complex network of tunnels underground. They: 1. **Probe** the soil to find a stable path. 2. **Excavate** small sections at a time. 3. **Support** the walls immediately with mud (a primitive equivalent of shotcrete/rock bolts). 4. **Ventilate** by creating multiple entry/exit points. Engineers do exactly the same, but at a vastly larger scale and under strict safety protocols. The greatest risk in tunnelling is the **time gap between excavation and support** — leave the "walls" unsupported for even a few minutes, and the ground can collapse, much like an ant tunnel caving in when the moisture dries. ## 1.3 Classification of Tunnels (KTU Board Exam Favorite) Tunnels are classified based on **purpose**, **geology**, and **cross-section**. | Basis | Type | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **By Purpose** | Transportation (Road/Rail) | Highway & Metro passes | | | Hydropower / Water Conveyance | Headrace, penstock, tailrace | | | Sewerage | Underground drainage | | | Utility | Power cables, telecom, gas | | | Defence | Strategic bunkers, subways | | **By Cross-Section** | Circular, Horseshoe, Egg-shaped, Rectangular | Geologic stress adaptation | | **By Excavation Method** | Cut-and-Cover, Bored (TBM), Drill-and-Blast, NATM, SEM | Soil/Rock condition | | **By Location** | Mountain (Rock), Underwater, Urban (Soft ground) | Geological setting | ## 1.4 Phases of a Tunnelling Project (Lifecycle View) A tunnelling project progresses through **five critical phases**, each with distinct safety challenges: 1. **Investigation & Survey Phase** — Geological, geophysical, hydrogeological, and environmental studies. 2. **Design Phase** — Lining design, support system specification, ventilation load calculation. 3. **Construction Phase** — Excavation, mucking, primary support, permanent lining. 4. **Commissioning & Fit-out Phase** — MEP (Mechanical, Electrical, Plumbing), track laying, signaling. 5. **Operation & Maintenance Phase** — Inspection, rehabilitation, emergency preparedness. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Focus:** Module 2 dedicates the bulk of marks to **Phase 3 (Construction)** because it carries the **highest fatality and injury risk density** of all construction activities (per ILO & OSHA statistics). ## 1.5 Typical Tunnel Cross-Section Geometry (Standard Horse-Shoe) A standard horse-shoe shaped tunnel, commonly used in railway and road works, has the following typical dimensions: - **Finished diameter (D):** **8.0 m to 12.0 m** for double-lane road tunnels. - **Convergence allowance:** **50 mm to 150 mm** (radial inward displacement of the opening). - **Lining thickness (concrete):** **300 mm to 600 mm**. - **Ventilation cross-sectional area:** Reserved as **20–25% of the gross cross-section**. The inverted U-shape optimally resists the **vertical overburden pressure** and provides adequate headroom for vehicles and ventilation ducts. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Ground Reaction Curve (GRC) and Support Characteristic Line (SCL) — Conceptual Foundation To understand tunnelling safety, one must understand the **interactive behaviour** between the ground and the support system. ### 2.1.1 The Ground Reaction Curve (GRC) When a tunnel is excavated, the surrounding ground deforms inwards. The relationship between the **radial deformation ($u_r$)** of the tunnel wall and the **internal pressure ($p_i$)** required to restrain it is known as the **Ground Reaction Curve**, originally formulated by **Fenner (1938)** and later refined by **Pacher (1956)** and **Carranza-Torres**. The **Fenner–Pacher Curve** indicates that: - If the tunnel is left **unsupported**, deformation $u_r$ increases indefinitely and the ground self-stabilizes at a residual pressure $p_{eq}$ (the "self-supporting pressure"). - If support is installed **too early** (at high $p_i$), it attracts excessive load. - If support is installed **too late** (at low $p_i$), large deformations may have already caused collapse. - The **optimum** is the intersection of the GRC with the **Support Characteristic Line (SCL)**. ### 2.1.2 Support Characteristic Line (SCL) The SCL represents the stiffness response of the installed support (e.g., steel ribs, rock bolts, shotcrete). It is governed by Hooke's Law and the support's geometric properties. ## 2.2 Key Theoretical Principles — Stepwise Logic ### 2.2.1 Overburden Pressure ($P_0$) The primary load on a shallow tunnel is the **weight of the overburden** of soil/rock above the crown. The vertical stress at the crown is: $$P_0 = \gamma \cdot H_c$$ where: - $\gamma$ = unit weight of the overburden soil/rock (kN/m³) - $H_c$ = depth of cover measured from the ground surface to the crown of the tunnel (m) ### 2.2.2 Hoop Stress in a Circular Lining (Thin-Walled Pressure Vessel Analogy) For a circular tunnel lining treated as a closed thin-walled cylinder under uniform external radial pressure $p$: $$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{p \cdot R}{t}$$ where: - $R$ = internal radius of the lining (m) - $t$ = thickness of the lining (m) - $\sigma_{hoop}$ = circumferential (hoop) tensile stress (kN/m² or MPa) ### 2.2.3 Convergence–Confinement Method (CCM) The Convergence–Confinement Method, developed by **Prof. Marc Panet (1969)** and codified by **AFTES (French Tunnelling Association)**, relates three quantities: 1. **Longitudinal Deformation Profile (LDP)** — axial deformation along the tunnel axis near the face. 2. **Ground Reaction Curve** — radial pressure vs. radial closure. 3. **Support Characteristic Curve** — support pressure vs. radial closure. The equilibrium of the system is found at the **intersection point** of GRC and SCL, which gives the **design support pressure $p_{sup}$** and the **final radial closure $u_{eq}$**. ### 2.2.4 Stand-Up Time (Critical Concept for Safety) **Stand-up time** is the **maximum duration** for which an unsupported excavation can remain stable before failure. Factors controlling stand-up time: - **Rock Mass Rating (RMR)** of Bieniawski (1973) — geological quality. - **Q-System** of Barton (1974) — Norwegian Geotechnical Institute method. - **Geological Strength Index (GSI)** of Hoek (1995). - **In-situ Stress Ratio ($k_0$)** — horizontal-to-vertical stress. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Safety Implication:** Stand-up time is the **direct trigger** for emergency evacuation protocols. KTU examiners frequently frame this as: *"Justify the need for immediate primary support in squeezing ground conditions."* The answer must cite a stand-up time **shorter than the installation cycle time**. ## 2.3 Hazards in Tunnelling (Module 2 Core Content) Tunnelling is statistically among the **top three most dangerous construction operations** worldwide. The hierarchy of hazards, as per **IS 4756:1984 (Code of Practice for Safety in Tunnelling)** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart S**, is: | # | Hazard Category | Description | Typical Consequence | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Ground Collapse / Cave-in** | Roof or wall failure | Mass fatalities (e.g., Silkyara, 2023) | | 2 | **Flooding / Water Inrush** | Sudden ground water or river ingress | Drowning, mud rush | | 3 | **Fire & Explosion** | Combustible gases (CH₄, H₂S) or equipment fire | Burns, asphyxiation | | 4 | **Toxic / Oxygen-Deficient Atmosphere** | Engine exhaust, dust, low O₂ | Asphyxiation, lung disease | | 5 | **Falls from Height / Falling Objects** | Shaft openings, Muck cars | Crush injuries, fractures | | 6 | **Noise & Vibration** | Drill/blast operations | NIHL, HAVS | | 7 | **Electrocution** | High-voltage traction, water pumps | Cardiac arrest | | 8 | **Mechanical Entanglement** | TBM cutterhead, conveyors | Amputation | | 9 | **Silicosis & Dust Diseases** | Crystalline silica from drilling | Progressive pulmonary fibrosis | | 10 | **Pneumoconiosis** | Diesel Particulate Matter (DPM) | Lung cancer, COPD | ## 2.4 Safety Control Hierarchy (Per ISO 45001:2018 & KTU Module 2) The **hierarchy of controls** applied to tunnelling hazards (most effective → least effective) is: 1. **Elimination** — e.g., reroute the tunnel alignment to avoid a known fault zone. 2. **Substitution** — e.g., use a Tunnel Boring Machine (TBM) instead of drill-and-blast in urban areas. 3. **Engineering Controls** — e.g., robust primary support, dust suppression ventilation, dewatering systems. 4. **Administrative Controls** — e.g., safe work permits, JSA (Job Safety Analysis), gas testing, exclusion zones. 5. **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)** — Hard hat, steel-toe boots, respirator, gas detector, harness. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Overburden vertical stress | $P_0 = \gamma \cdot H_c$ | kN/m² | Crown load calculation | | 2 | Hoop stress (thin cylinder) | $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{p \cdot R}{t}$ | kN/m² or MPa | Lining design | | 3 | Convergence loss | $u_r \propto \dfrac{1 - \nu}{E} \cdot p \cdot R$ | m | Support stiffness check | | 4 | Air quantity for ventilation | $Q = q \cdot N$ where $q$ = per-person air | m³/min | Duct sizing | | 5 | Minimum air velocity | $v_{min} \geq 0.15$ to $0.5$ m/s | m/s | To prevent gas stratification | | 6 | Hoek–Brown failure criterion | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 + \sigma_{ci}\left(m_b \dfrac{\sigma_3}{\sigma_{ci}} + s\right)^{a}$ | MPa | Rock mass strength | | 7 | Q-System rock quality | $Q = \dfrac{RQD}{J_n} \cdot \dfrac{J_r}{J_a} \cdot \dfrac{J_w}{SRF}$ | dimensionless | Support category | | 8 | Stand-up time correlation (Lauffer) | $t_s \propto \dfrac{f(\text{RMR})}{f(\text{span})}$ | hours/days | Cycle time vs. support timing | | 9 | Dust suppression water rate | $W = 5$ to $10$ L/min per drill | L/min | Drill-jumbo safety | | 10 | Critical air velocity for fire | $v_c = 1.0$ to $1.5$ m/s (smoke control) | m/s | Emergency ventilation | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** The single most-tested numerical question in Module 2 is the **ventilation air quantity calculation** for a given workforce and diesel equipment count. Always carry forward units, and state assumptions clearly. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The GRC–SCL intersection principle is the **safety design cornerstone** of every modern metro, hydropower headrace, and underwater road tunnel in India — including the **Rohtang Atal Tunnel (8.8 km, 3,000 m altitude)**, the **Chenab Railway Bridge Approach Tunnel**, and the **Kochi Metro underground section**. By selecting the **stiffness and installation timing** of the support, engineers literally control the "breathing" of the mountain to prevent catastrophic collapse. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hoop Stress in a Circular Concrete Lining (Thin-Walled Cylinder Approximation) ### 3.1.1 Problem Setup Consider a circular tunnel of **internal radius $R = 5.0$ m** lined with **M30 concrete** of thickness $t = 0.4$ m. The lining is subjected to a uniform external radial pressure $p = 250$ kN/m² (= 0.25 MPa) due to overburden and ground relaxation. Determine the **hoop (circumferential) tensile stress** in the lining and verify whether M30 concrete (permissible tensile strength $f_t = 0.45 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ per IS 456) is safe. ### 3.1.2 Given Data - $R = 5.0$ m - $t = 0.4$ m - $p = 250$ kN/m² - $f_{ck} = 30$ MPa (characteristic compressive strength of M30) ### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — State the governing equation.** For a thin-walled circular cylinder subjected to uniform external pressure $p$, the hoop stress is obtained by equating the **radial force on a half-cylinder** to the **resisting tensile force** along the two diametrical cuts: $$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{p \cdot R}{t}$$ **Step 2 — Verify the thin-walled assumption.** $$\frac{R}{t} = \frac{5.0}{0.4} = 12.5$$ Since $R/t > 10$, the thin-walled assumption is **valid**. **Step 3 — Substitute numerical values** (ensure unit consistency: convert $p$ from kN/m² to MPa or keep both in kN/m²). Using $p = 250$ kN/m², $R = 5.0$ m, $t = 0.4$ m: $$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{250 \times 5.0}{0.4}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate.** $$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{1250}{0.4} = 3125 \text{ kN/m}^{2} = 3.125 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the permissible tensile strength of M30 concrete** (per IS 456:2000, Clause 6.1.5): $$f_t = 0.45 \sqrt{f_{ck}} = 0.45 \sqrt{30}$$ $$f_t = 0.45 \times 5.477 = 2.465 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 6 — Compare.** $$\sigma_{hoop} = 3.125 \text{ MPa} \quad > \quad f_t = 2.465 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 7 — Conclusion and Safety Decision.** The induced hoop stress **exceeds** the permissible tensile strength of plain M30 concrete. The lining must be **reinforced with steel** (a reinforced concrete lining, $f_{steel} = 415$ MPa, per IS 456), or the thickness must be increased to $t \geq 0.51$ m, or a **secondary support** (e.g., rock bolts + steel ribs) must be installed. ### 3.1.4 Reverse Check — Required Thickness for Plain Concrete Setting $\sigma_{hoop} = f_t$: $$t_{req} = \frac{p \cdot R}{f_t} = \frac{0.25 \times 5000}{2.465} = 507 \text{ mm} \approx 0.51 \text{ m}$$ This confirms the need for either **thicker lining** or **reinforcement**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Ventilation Air Quantity for a Tunnelling Face ### 3.2.1 Problem Setup A 6.0 m finished diameter tunnel is being driven by the **NATM (New Austrian Tunnelling Method)** through rock. The face crew consists of **30 workers**. Diesel-powered equipment on-site includes **2 nos. of 75 kW load-haul-dump (LHD) loaders** and **1 no. of 60 kW jumbo drill**. Compute the **required ventilation air quantity** using the **combined method** (per ASHRAE & IS 4756 guidelines). ### 3.2.2 Given Data - Number of personnel, $N = 30$ - Diesel engine power (LHD), $P_1 = 2 \times 75 = 150$ kW - Diesel engine power (Jumbo), $P_2 = 60$ kW - Total diesel power, $P_{total} = 210$ kW - Air requirement per person, $q_{person} = 3.0$ m³/min (per IS 4756) - Air requirement per kW of diesel, $q_{diesel} = 3.0$ m³/min per kW (CECAR standard) - Air requirement per unit face area (for dust dilution), $q_{area} = 0.06$ m³/min per m² (typical) ### 3.2.3 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Air required for personnel (occupational health).** $$Q_{person} = q_{person} \times N = 3.0 \times 30 = 90 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/min}$$ **Step 2 — Air required for diesel engines (toxic exhaust dilution, primarily CO and NOₓ).** $$Q_{diesel} = q_{diesel} \times P_{total} = 3.0 \times 210 = 630 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/min}$$ **Step 3 — Air required for face area dust dilution (during drilling/mucking).** Cross-sectional area of tunnel: $$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 6.0^{2}}{4} = 28.27 \text{ m}^{2}$$ $$Q_{area} = q_{area} \times A = 0.06 \times 28.27 = 1.70 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/min}$$ **Step 4 — Total air quantity (sum of the above, because the contaminants are independent).** $$Q_{total} = Q_{person} + Q_{diesel} + Q_{area}$$ $$Q_{total} = 90 + 630 + 1.70 = 721.70 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/min}$$ **Step 5 — Apply safety factor of 1.25** (per IS 4756:1984, Clause 5.2) to account for duct leakage, unaccounted heat load, and emergency reserve: $$Q_{design} = 1.25 \times Q_{total} = 1.25 \times 721.70 = 902.13 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/min}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to standard reporting unit (m³/s).** $$Q_{design} = \frac{902.13}{60} = 15.04 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}$$ ### 3.2.4 Final Design Decision A **forced axial fan** with a capacity of **≥ 15 m³/s** at a duct static pressure head of **≥ 1500 Pa** (for a 1.0 km duct length with 1000 mm diameter flexible duct) must be deployed. Continuous **real-time gas monitoring** (CH₄, CO, O₂, NO₂) at the face is mandatory. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lifting / Hoisting Load on a Tunnel Shaft Winch ### 3.3.1 Problem Setup A vertical access shaft of depth $L = 80$ m is used to lower muck skips. A fully loaded skip weighs $W_{skip} = 50$ kN. The wire rope has a mass of $m_{rope} = 5$ kg/m. Determine the **maximum tension in the wire rope** at the **top of the shaft** (worst case, when the skip is at the bottom). ### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Weight of the rope.** $$W_{rope} = m_{rope} \cdot L \cdot g = 5 \times 80 \times 9.81 = 3924 \text{ N} \approx 3.92 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2 — Total maximum tension at the top of the shaft (skip + rope + dynamic factor).** The static tension is: $$T_{static} = W_{skip} + W_{rope} = 50 + 3.92 = 53.92 \text{ kN}$$ Apply a **dynamic amplification factor** of 1.20 (per IS 3177 for shaft hoisting) to account for acceleration and jerk: $$T_{max} = 1.20 \times 53.92 = 64.70 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3 — Safety Check on the Wire Rope.** For a **6×37 construction** rope with breaking load $T_{break} = 540$ kN (for 24 mm dia), the **factor of safety (FoS)** is: $$\text{FoS} = \frac{T_{break}}{T_{max}} = \frac{540}{64.70} = 8.35$$ Since the statutory minimum FoS for **personnel/goods hoisting** is **6.0** (per IS 3177), the design is **safe**. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns | Pattern Type | Input Parameters | Governing Equation | Output | |---|---|---|---| | Lining hoop stress | $R, t, p$ | $\sigma_{hoop} = pR/t$ | Tensile stress (MPa) | | Air quantity | Personnel, kW, area | $Q = \sum q_i$ | m³/min | | Wire rope FoS | $T_{break}, W, L$ | $\text{FoS} = T_{break} / (W_{total} \times \text{DAF})$ | Dimensionless | | Stand-up time | RMR, span | Lauffer's chart | Hours | | Q-System | $RQD, J_n, J_r, J_a, J_w, SRF$ | $Q = (RQD/J_n)(J_r/J_a)(J_w/SRF)$ | Support category | | Critical velocity for fire | Heat release rate | $v_c \approx 0.4 \sqrt{H/W}$ | m/s | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tunnelling Safety Management System ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["GEOTECHNICAL INPUT
RMR, Q, GSI, k0"] nodeB["DESIGN PHASE
Lining, Support Spec"] nodeC["HAZARD REGISTER
Per IS 4756 and ISO 45001"] nodeD["EXCAVATION CONTROL
TBM or D and B"] nodeE["PRIMARY SUPPORT
Shotcrete Rock Bolts Steel Ribs"] nodeF["VENTILATION PLANT
Duct Fans Gas Monitors"] nodeG["EMERGENCY RESPONSE
Escape Shaft Rescue Plan"] nodeH["PERMANENT LINING
Cast In Situ Concrete"] nodeI["COMMISSIONING AND HANDOVER
DOCUMENTATION"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeC --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeG ``` ## 4.2 Tunnel Cross-Section — Functional Component Map ```mermaid graph LR nodeX["CABLE DUCT AND
VENTILATION DUCT"] nodeY["INVERT CONCRETE
AND DRAINAGE"] nodeZ["SIDEWALK AND
WALKWAY"] nodeW["CARRIAGEWAY OR
TRACK BED"] nodeV["PRIMARY LINING
SHOTCRETE 250 MM"] nodeU["SECONDARY LINING
RCC 400 MM"] nodeT["WATERPROOFING
MEMBRANE"] nodeS["ROCK BOLT PATTERN
1.5 M BY 1.5 M"] nodeR["OVERBURDEN ROCK
AND SOIL"] nodeQ["INVERT ARCH
ANTI-HEAVE"] nodeR --> nodeS nodeS --> nodeV nodeV --> nodeT nodeT --> nodeU nodeU --> nodeW nodeW --> nodeZ nodeZ --> nodeY nodeY --> nodeQ nodeQ --> nodeX ``` ## 4.3 Tunnelling Method Selection — Decision Flow ```mermaid graph TD nodeStart["START METHOD
SELECTION"] nodeGround["GROUND TYPE
SOFT SOIL OR ROCK"] nodeDepth["DEPTH OF COVER
SHALLOW LESS THAN 30 M
OR DEEP MORE THAN 30 M"] nodeWater["WATER TABLE
ABOVE OR BELOW TUNNEL"] nodeUrban["URBAN LOCATION
YES OR NO"] nodeDec1["USE CUT AND COVER METHOD
IF SHALLOW AND URBAN"] nodeDec2["USE SLURRY TBM OR EPB
IF SOFT SOIL AND BELOW WATER"] nodeDec3["USE DRILL AND BLAST
IF HARD ROCK AND DEEP"] nodeDec4["USE NATM OR SEM
IF VARIABLE GROUND AND SHORT LENGTH"] nodeEnd["SELECT METHOD AND
REVIEW SAFETY PLAN"] nodeStart --> nodeGround nodeGround --> nodeDepth nodeDepth --> nodeWater nodeWater --> nodeUrban nodeUrban --> nodeDec1 nodeUrban --> nodeDec2 nodeUrban --> nodeDec3 nodeUrban --> nodeDec4 nodeDec1 --> nodeEnd nodeDec2 --> nodeEnd nodeDec3 --> nodeEnd nodeDec4 --> nodeEnd ``` ## 4.4 Hazard-to-Control Mapping (Cause-Effect-Safeguard Logic) ```mermaid graph TD nodeH1["GROUND COLLAPSE
CAUSE LOSS OF STAND UP TIME"] nodeH2["WATER INRUSH
CAUSE FLOODING AND DROWNING"] nodeH3["GAS ACCUMULATION
CAUSE CH4 EXPLOSION OR ASPHYXIATION"] nodeH4["FIRE AT FACE
CAUSE TOXIC SMOKE AND BURN INJURY"] nodeC1["CONTROL PROBE AHEAD DRILLING
ROCK BOLTS SHOTCRETE STEEL RIBS"] nodeC2["CONTROL DEWATERING PUMPS
GROUTING AND BULKHEAD DOORS"] nodeC3["CONTROL CONTINUOUS GAS MONITORING
VENTILATION FANS AT LEAST 15 M3 PER S"] nodeC4["CONTROL FIRE SUPPRESSION AND
EMERGENCY ESCAPE ROUTE"] nodeH1 --> nodeC1 nodeH2 --> nodeC2 nodeH3 --> nodeC3 nodeH4 --> nodeC4 ``` ## 4.5 Ventilation Network Schematic (Block Flow) ```mermaid graph LR nodeF["MAIN AXIAL FAN
OUTSIDE PORTAL"] nodeD1["STEEL DUCT 1000 MM DIA
DELIVERY SIDE"] nodeD2["FLEXIBLE DUCT 1000 MM DIA
TO FACE"] nodeDM["DUST MONITOR AT FACE"] nodeGM["GAS MONITOR CH4 CO O2 NO2"] nodeEL["ELECTRICAL PANEL
EXPLOSION PROOF"] nodeBV["BACKUP FAN AND
EMERGENCY MODE"] nodeF --> nodeD1 nodeD1 --> nodeD2 nodeD2 --> nodeDM nodeDM --> nodeGM nodeGM --> nodeEL nodeEL --> nodeBV ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define stand-up time in tunnelling. List any two geological parameters that influence it.** **Model Answer:** Stand-up time is the **maximum duration** for which an unsupported underground excavation (e.g., a tunnel face) can remain stable without significant roof fall or wall collapse. It is a critical input to the construction cycle time and the deployment of primary support. Two geological parameters that influence stand-up time are: 1. **Rock Mass Rating (RMR)** — a higher RMR (better rock quality) increases stand-up time. 2. **In-situ stress ratio ($k_0$)** — high horizontal stresses in deep tunnels reduce stand-up time by inducing squeezing or spalling. ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **State the purpose of a "muck car" and "segment erector" in a Shield-TBM tunnelling project.** **Model Answer:** A **muck car** is a rail-mounted or rubber-tyred wagon used to transport the excavated soil/rock (muck) from the tunnel face to the portal or shaft for disposal. It is part of the **mucking cycle** and is critical for cycle time optimization. A **segment erector** is a **rotating, hydraulically actuated arm** mounted on the rear of a Shield Tunnel Boring Machine (TBM). Its purpose is to **lift and place precast concrete segments** (typically 6–7 per ring) to form the **permanent tunnel lining** as the shield advances. It is operated remotely from the TBM control cabin for safety. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain the **New Austrian Tunnelling Method (NATM)** with a neat block diagram. Discuss its **safety advantages** over the conventional drill-and-blast method in urban and weak-rock conditions. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A circular road tunnel of **internal diameter $D = 10$ m** is driven through rock with a cover $H_c = 80$ m and rock unit weight $\gamma = 24$ kN/m³. The final concrete lining has a thickness $t = 0.5$ m. Compute the **hoop tensile stress** induced in the lining, assuming the full overburden pressure is transferred to the lining. Compare with the permissible tensile strength of M30 concrete ($f_{ck} = 30$ MPa) and state whether plain concrete is safe. If not, suggest **two remedial measures**. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — NATM Explanation The **New Austrian Tunnelling Method (NATM)**, developed by **Prof. Ladislaus von Rabcewicz (1957–1962)**, is a **"design-as-you-go"** philosophy that exploits the **self-supporting capacity** of the rock mass. The key principles are: 1. **Mobilize rock strength** — The surrounding rock is treated as a *load-bearing structural element*, not a load to be carried. Controlled deformation releases stress. 2. **Shotcrete protection** — A thin layer of **steel-fibre-reinforced shotcrete** is applied immediately after excavation to seal the rock and prevent weathering. 3. **Systematic rock bolting** — **Rock bolts (typically 3–6 m long)** are installed in a regular pattern to knit the rock mass into a self-supporting arch (the "Voussoir arch" analogy). 4. **Deformation monitoring** — **Convergence gauges**, **extensometers**, and **load cells** are installed to monitor the rock–support interaction in real time. 5. **Flexible support, not rigid** — The lining is allowed to deform (within limits) before final stiffening, avoiding the "breathing" problem of rigid linings. **Safety advantages over drill-and-blast:** - **No blast-induced vibrations** — critical in urban areas to protect adjacent buildings. - **Reduced overbreak** — better stress redistribution. - **Continuous support application** — minimizes stand-up time exposure. - **Lower airborne dust and toxic fumes** — improves occupational health. - **Adaptable to variable ground** — sequencing can be changed based on real-time monitoring. A simple block diagram: ``` EXCAVATE (1.0–1.5 m round) ↓ IMMEDIATE SHOTCRETE SEAL ↓ INSTALL ROCK BOLTS + WELDED WIRE MESH ↓ MONITOR CONVERGENCE & ROCK LOADS ↓ IF DEFORMATION > LIMIT → ADD STEEL RIBS / INVERT CLOSURE ↓ CAST PERMANENT RCC LINING (after deformation stabilizes) ``` #### Part (b) — Hoop Stress Calculation **Given Data:** - $D = 10$ m, so $R = 5$ m - $H_c = 80$ m - $\gamma = 24$ kN/m³ - $t = 0.5$ m - $f_{ck} = 30$ MPa **Step 1 — Overburden pressure at crown.** $$P_0 = \gamma \cdot H_c = 24 \times 80 = 1920 \text{ kN/m}^{2} = 1.92 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 2 — Hoop stress in lining (thin-walled cylinder).** $$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P_0 \cdot R}{t} = \frac{1.92 \times 5}{0.5} = 19.2 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3 — Permissible tensile strength of M30 concrete.** $$f_t = 0.45 \sqrt{30} = 0.45 \times 5.477 = 2.465 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4 — Comparison.** $$\sigma_{hoop} = 19.2 \text{ MPa} \quad \gg \quad f_t = 2.465 \text{ MPa}$$ The induced hoop stress is **~7.8 times** higher than the permissible tensile strength. **Plain concrete is grossly unsafe**. **Step 5 — Two remedial measures.** 1. **Reinforce the lining** with double layers of **T32 steel bars @ 150 mm c/c** on both faces (a typical design strength $f_{steel} = 415$ MPa). This transfers the entire tensile load to the steel. 2. **Increase the lining thickness** to $t \geq \dfrac{1.92 \times 5}{2.465} = 3.90$ m, OR **install an inner secondary lining** (a 2-stage lining system) which is far more economical. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Discuss the **"hierarchy of controls"** as applied to the **10 major hazards** in tunnelling, with at least **two engineering controls** and **two PPE** examples for each hazard category. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 1.8 km long tunnel is being ventilated by a single axial fan delivering **25 m³/s** of air through a 1.2 m diameter duct. The leakage factor in the duct is **12% per 100 m**. Compute the **air quantity actually reaching the face**. If the face requires a minimum of **15 m³/s**, determine whether the design is adequate. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — Hierarchy of Controls in Tunnelling (Detailed table is presented in Section 2.4 above.) The student must present the hierarchy (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE) and then map the 10 major hazards to the controls. Sample for two hazards: | Hazard | Engineering Control | Administrative Control | PPE | |---|---|---|---| | **Ground collapse** | Probe drilling, rock bolts, shotcrete, dewatering | Permit-to-work, JSA, exclusion zone | Hard hat, harness, boots | | **Flooding** | Bulkhead doors, sump pumps, grouting | Continuous monitoring, evacuation drill | Life jacket, emergency lamp | #### Part (b) — Air Quantity at Face **Given Data:** - $Q_{fan} = 25$ m³/s - $L_{duct} = 1800$ m - Leakage rate = 12% per 100 m **Step 1 — Cumulative leakage fraction over 1800 m.** $$\text{Leakage fraction} = \frac{12}{100} \times \frac{1800}{100} = 0.12 \times 18 = 2.16$$ **Step 2 — Air quantity at face (assuming the 12% is compounded over 18 segments):** Using the compounded leakage model $Q_{face} = Q_{fan} \times (1 - k)^{n}$ where $k = 0.12$ and $n = 18$: $$Q_{face} = 25 \times (1 - 0.12)^{18} = 25 \times (0.88)^{18}$$ Compute $(0.88)^{18}$: $$\ln(0.88^{18}) = 18 \times \ln(0.88) = 18 \times (-0.1278) = -2.3004$$ $$(0.88)^{18} = e^{-2.3004} = 0.1003$$ Therefore: $$Q_{face} = 25 \times 0.1003 = 2.51 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}$$ **Step 3 — Decision.** Required = **15 m³/s**; Available at face = **2.51 m³/s** ⟹ **Massively inadequate**. **Remedies:** - Install a **booster (inline) fan** at the mid-duct (at ~900 m). - Use a **rigid steel duct** instead of a flexible duct to reduce leakage to ~2% per 100 m. - Increase the fan capacity to **~150 m³/s** at the source (impractical — confirms the booster is the correct engineering choice). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do not forget to apply the leakage as a compounded (geometric) factor, not a linear sum.** A common mistake is to write $25 \times (1 - 2.16) = -28$ m³/s, which is mathematically meaningless. Always use the **multiplicative leakage model** $Q_{face} = Q_{fan} \cdot (1-k)^{n}$. > - **Units carry-forward marks:** Forgetting to convert m³/s to m³/min (or vice versa) costs **0.5 to 1 mark** per sub-question. > - **IS Code citation:** Naming **IS 4756:1984** or **IS 4081:1986** (Code of Practice for Construction of Tunnels) earns a **+1 bonus mark** in KTU valuation. > - **No assumptions = no marks:** Every numerical solution must state the **assumed formula** at the top, e.g., "Assuming thin-walled cylinder, $R/t > 10$" or "Assuming compounded duct leakage." ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Stand-up time** = maximum unsupported stable duration. Reduces with poor RMR/Q-value and high stress. - **GRC–SCL intersection** is the **safety design sweet spot** for support installation timing. - **Hoop stress** in a circular lining: $\sigma_{hoop} = pR/t$ — thin-walled assumption valid for $R/t > 10$. - **Overburden pressure** at the crown: $P_0 = \gamma H_c$ — the **primary load** for shallow tunnels. - **Ventilation air quantity** = sum of personnel, diesel, and dust-dilution components, multiplied by a **1.25 safety factor**. - **Duct leakage** is **compounded**, not additive: use $Q_{face} = Q_{fan} \times (1-k)^n$. - **Top 10 hazards** in tunnelling: ground collapse, flooding, fire/explosion, toxic atmosphere, falls, noise, electrocution, mechanical, dust diseases, DPM exposure. - **Hierarchy of controls** = Elimination > Substitution > Engineering > Administrative > PPE. - **Statutory codes for tunnelling safety in India** = **IS 4756:1984**, **IS 4081:1986**, **IS 3177**, **Mines Act 1952**, **Factories Act 1948**, **OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart S**. - **NATM core philosophy** = mobilize the rock's self-supporting capacity; real-time monitoring is non-negotiable. - **TBM (TBM/EPB/Slurry)** preferred for urban soft ground; **Drill-and-Blast** for hard rock; **NATM/SEM** for variable ground. - **Critical fire ventilation velocity** = **1.0 to 1.5 m/s** to prevent smoke back-layering. - **Wire rope Factor of Safety** for personnel hoisting ≥ **6.0** (IS 3177). - **RMR / Q-system / GSI** — the three pillars of rock mass classification for support design. - **Convergence–Confinement Method (CCM)** — the analytical framework linking face advance, support timing, and rock deformation. - **Probe drilling (≥ 20 m ahead)** is mandatory in TBM/NATM work to detect water-bearing zones (lesson from the **Silkyara rescue, 2023**). - **Emergency escape route** must be maintained at **all times** during construction — typically a parallel pilot tunnel or cross-passage every **500 m**.

topics.title: Blasting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT526 - SAFETY IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Blasting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Blasting** in construction engineering refers to the controlled use of **explosives** to fracture, fragment, or displace rock, soil, concrete, or other hard materials for the purpose of excavation, demolition, trenching, tunneling, or site preparation. It is a specialized high-hazard operation governed by strict statutory controls (in India, primarily the **Explosives Act, 1884** and the **Explosives Rules, 2008**, along with the **Indian Explosives Act** and DGMS guidelines).

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – PESFT526 (Safety in Construction)**, blasting is studied as a high-risk activity requiring an integrated safety management approach covering:
- Selection and storage of explosives
- Charging, stemming, and firing sequence
- Fly-rock, ground vibration, and air-overpressure (noise) control
- Misfire management
- Personal protective equipment (PPE)
- Statutory clearances and trained personnel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):** Blasting is treated as a *high-hazard construction activity* where the principles of **hazard identification, risk assessment, and control (HIRAC)** are applied before, during, and after the blast event.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a giant chocolate bar (rock) and you want to break it into small pieces. You cannot use a hammer because it is too large. Instead, you carefully drill a tiny hole inside it, place a small controlled chemical reaction (explosive) at the bottom of the hole, fill the rest of the hole with clay (stemming) so the energy cannot escape upward easily, and then trigger it from a safe distance.

The energy that is released has nowhere to go except *into the rock*, so it cracks the rock in a controlled pattern.

> That tiny controlled chemical reaction is the **explosive charge**, and the entire science of making sure it cracks the rock *just enough* — without throwing stones at workers, damaging nearby buildings, or causing a misfire — is what **blasting safety** is all about.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Explosive** | A substance that undergoes rapid, self-propagating decomposition releasing gas and heat |
| **Detonator / Blasting Cap** | A small explosive device used to initiate a larger explosive charge |
| **Blasting Agent** | A mixture (e.g., ANFO — Ammonium Nitrate Fuel Oil) that requires a booster for detonation |
| **Charge** | The quantity of explosive loaded into a borehole |
| **Borehole / Blast Hole** | The drilled hole into which the explosive charge is placed |
| **Stemming** | Inert material (drill cuttings, sand, clay) placed above the charge to confine gases |
| **Misfire** | A charge that fails to detonate when expected |
| **Fly-rock** | Unwanted rock fragments projected beyond the blast zone |
| **Blast Pattern** | Geometric arrangement of boreholes (e.g., staggered, square) |
| **Burden** | Distance from the borehole to the nearest free face |
| **Spacing** | Distance between adjacent boreholes in a row |

> [!NOTE]
> **Statutory Constant to remember:** The minimum legal clearance distance for storage of explosives from inhabited buildings, railways, and highways in India is prescribed under the **Explosives Rules, 2008** and varies from **100 m to 800 m** depending on the quantity stored (net explosive quantity, NEQ).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Explosives Used in Construction

Construction blasting uses two broad families of explosives:

### A. High Explosives (Detonating explosives)
- Detonate by shock wave at velocities of **3,000 to 9,000 m/s**.
- Used for **hard rock** blasting, tunneling, and demolition.
- Examples: **Dynamite (NG-based), Slurry emulsions, Water gels, RDX/TNT compositions**.

### B. Low Explosives (Deflagrating explosives)
- Burn rapidly but do not produce a true shock wave.
- Velocity of reaction ≈ **300 to 1,000 m/s**.
- Examples: **Black powder (gunpowder), Smokeless powder**.
- Used for soft rock, boulder splitting, and special applications.

### C. Blasting Agents
- **ANFO (Ammonium Nitrate 94% + Fuel Oil 6%)** is the most widely used blasting agent worldwide.
- **Velocity of detonation ≈ 3,500–4,500 m/s** (in confined conditions).
- Not cap-sensitive — requires a **booster charge** (e.g., emulsion cartridge or cast booster) to initiate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use ANFO in construction?** It is cheap, safe to handle (insensitive to friction, impact, and stray electric current), easy to load pneumatically, and produces minimal toxic fumes. However, it is **not water-resistant** and cannot be used in wet boreholes without an emulsion blend.

## 2.2 Components of a Typical Blast Design

A proper blast involves the following sequence:

1. **Survey and marking** of the blast area
2. **Drilling** of boreholes to designed depth and diameter
3. **Loading** the borehole with primer + main charge
4. **Stemming** the upper portion of the hole
5. **Connecting** the detonating circuit (series, parallel, or series-parallel)
6. **Firing** using a blasting machine (exploder)
7. **Inspection** after blast for misfires
8. **Misfire treatment** if required

## 2.3 Steps to Make Blasting Safe — Engineering Logic

The safety of blasting is built layer by layer, much like an onion. Each layer addresses a specific hazard.

- **Step 1 — Hazard Identification:** Identify fly-rock, ground vibration, air overpressure, dust, noxious fumes, misfire, fire, and electric initiation hazards.
- **Step 2 — Risk Assessment:** Quantify the *probability* and *consequence* of each hazard using tools such as **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** or **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effect Analysis)**.
- **Step 3 — Engineering Controls:** Design the blast pattern correctly — burden, spacing, stemming length, sub-drill, charge weight per delay.
- **Step 4 — Administrative Controls:** Permits-to-work, exclusion zones, trained blaster, blast warning sirens, public notice.
- **Step 5 — PPE:** Helmets, ear protection, blast-resistant goggles, dust masks, high-visibility clothing.
- **Step 6 — Emergency Preparedness:** Misfire-handling procedure, fire-fighting equipment, first-aid, evacuation plan.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **the most important** for solving numerical/analytical questions on blasting in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = K \times \left(\dfrac{Q^{1/2}}{D}\right)^{1.6}$ | Ground vibration (Peak Particle Velocity) — Bureau of Indian Standard predictor; $K$, $n$ are site constants ($n \approx 1.6$) | $V$ in mm/s, $Q$ in kg, $D$ in m |
| 2 | $SD = \dfrac{D}{\sqrt{Q}}$ | **Scaled Distance** — controls vibration and air overpressure | m / kg$^{1/2}$ |
| 3 | $L = B \times S \times H \times d$ | Volume of rock broken in a single blast ($B$ = burden, $S$ = spacing, $H$ = bench height, $d$ = density of rock) | m³ |
| 4 | $Q = L \times q$ | Total explosive charge required (L = volume of rock, $q$ = powder factor) | kg |
| 5 | $q = \dfrac{Q}{V_{rock}}$ | **Powder Factor** — kg of explosive per m³ of rock | kg/m³ |
| 6 | $PPL_{safe} = 5 \,\text{mm/s}$ | Safe Peak Particle Velocity for residential structures (BIS / DGMS) | mm/s |
| 7 | $Q_{max} = \left(\dfrac{D_{safe}}{SD}\right)^{2}$ | Maximum charge per delay for a given safe distance | kg |
| 8 | $T = 0.05 \times B$ (approx.) | Empirical rule for stemming length ($T$ ≥ burden, or use $\geq$ hole diameter × 12) | m |
| 9 | $B = 25 \text{ to } 40 \times D_{hole}$ | Burden in terms of hole diameter | m |
| 10 | $S = 1.0 \text{ to } 1.5 \times B$ | Spacing in terms of burden | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE SYNTAX RULE (engine safeguard):** Notice that absolute values are written using the **bold** notation, e.g. **$SD = 12 \, m / kg^{1/2}$** — never use the vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ inside a markdown table row.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The formulas above are not just exam material — they are used **daily** on infrastructure projects such as:
- **Highway and railway cuttings** in the Western Ghats
- **Hydroelectric dam construction** (e.g., Idukki, Tehri)
- **Metro tunneling** in cities like Kochi, Bengaluru, and Delhi
- **Quarrying** for aggregate in cement and concrete production
- **Building demolition** in urban redevelopment projects

A miscalculation in the powder factor or scaled distance can shatter windows in a neighboring village, crack a dam, or send a fly-rock through a worker's chest. **This is why the subject carries full 14-mark questions in the ESE.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Peak Particle Velocity (PPV) Predictor

The most commonly used vibration predictor in India is the **BIS / DGMS empirical formula**, derived from a large number of monitored blasts. The relationship between charge weight, distance, and resulting ground vibration is established by dimensional analysis and field calibration.

**Given:**
- $Q$ = Maximum charge per delay (kg)
- $D$ = Distance from blast face to monitoring point (m)
- $V$ = Peak particle velocity (mm/s)
- $K$, $n$ = Site-specific constants

**Derivation Logic (Step by Step):**

**Step 1 — Assume a power-law relationship** between vibration, charge, and distance. This is the form observed in hundreds of blasting studies worldwide.

$$V = K \cdot Q^{a} \cdot D^{b}$$

**Step 2 — Apply dimensional reasoning and field regression.** Studies by **Duvall & Petkof (1959)** and adopted by **BIS (IS 6922)** show that vibration scales with the **square root of charge**, and falls off with distance raised to a power that depends on local geology.

$$V = K \cdot \left(\frac{Q^{1/2}}{D}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to introduce the *Scaled Distance* $SD$**, which is the cornerstone of blast vibration analysis.

$$SD = \frac{D}{\sqrt{Q}}$$

**Step 4 — Combine** to obtain the standard form.

$$V = K \cdot (SD)^{-n}$$

For Indian conditions (as per **DGMS circulars and IS 6922**), the most commonly accepted value of the exponent is:

$$n = 1.6$$

So the working equation becomes:

$$V = K \cdot \left(\frac{\sqrt{Q}}{D}\right)^{1.6} = K \cdot \left(\frac{Q^{1/2}}{D}\right)^{1.6}$$

**Step 5 — Site calibration.** For a *typical* hard-rock site in India, the constant $K$ ranges between **300 and 1,000**. For a conservative design (residential structures nearby), use $K = 1{,}000$ with $n = 1.6$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Problem)

> **Problem:** A blast is to be designed in a quarry located 150 m from a residential colony. Using the BIS predictor equation with $K = 800$ and $n = 1.6$, calculate the **maximum charge per delay** that will keep the peak particle velocity below the safe limit of $5 \, \text{mm/s}$.

**Solution (each step explained):**

**Step 1 — Write the BIS vibration equation.**

$$V = K \cdot \left(\frac{\sqrt{Q}}{D}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values: $V = 5$, $K = 800$, $n = 1.6$, $D = 150$ m.**

$$5 = 800 \cdot \left(\frac{\sqrt{Q}}{150}\right)^{1.6}$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the bracket term.**

$$\left(\frac{\sqrt{Q}}{150}\right)^{1.6} = \frac{5}{800} = 0.00625$$

**Step 4 — Take both sides to the power $1/1.6$** (numerically ≈ 0.625).

$$\frac{\sqrt{Q}}{150} = (0.00625)^{0.625}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the right-hand side.**

$$(0.00625)^{0.625} \approx 0.0177$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\sqrt{Q}$.**

$$\sqrt{Q} = 150 \times 0.0177 \approx 2.66$$

**Step 7 — Square both sides.**

$$Q = 2.66^{2} \approx 7.07 \, \text{kg}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{Q_{max} \approx 7 \, \text{kg per delay}}$$

**Conclusion:** The blaster must ensure that no more than 7 kg of explosive detonates in any single delay (millisecond delay) to keep ground vibration within safe limits at the colony.

## 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Charge from Scaled Distance

**Given:** A safe scaled distance of $SD = 12 \, m/kg^{1/2}$ (a typical safe value for residential structures in India).

**Step 1 — Starting definition.**

$$SD = \frac{D}{\sqrt{Q}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $Q$.**

$$Q = \left(\frac{D}{SD}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — For $D = 200$ m and $SD = 12$:**

$$Q = \left(\frac{200}{12}\right)^{2} = (16.67)^{2} \approx 277.8 \, \text{kg}$$

So at 200 m, up to 277.8 kg of charge per delay can be safely fired using a $SD = 12$ design criterion.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Control of Common Blast Hazards

| Hazard | Cause | Control Measure |
|---|---|---|
| **Fly-rock** | Insufficient burden, over-charging, poor stemming | Use blast mats, design burden correctly, increase stemming length |
| **Ground Vibration** | Large charge per delay, short distance | Use **millisecond delay detonators**, limit charge per delay |
| **Air Overpressure (noise)** | Stemming ejection, gas release | Use adequate stemming, good stemming material, blast mats |
| **Dust & Fumes** | Detoxification of explosive, rock dust | Water spray before blast, use emulsions (less fumes) |
| **Misfire** | Cut-off detonator, damaged wiring, water in charge | Use **explosive-grade** detonators, waterproof charges, careful circuit testing |
| **Stray Current** | Nearby electrical installations, lightning | Use **non-electric detonators (NONEL / shock tube)** |
| **Unauthorized Access** | Public curiosity, lack of guards | Multi-layered cordon, sirens, public notice, trained guards |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **ground vibration (PPV)** with **air overpressure**. They are *different* physical phenomena and have *different* safe limits. Air overpressure is measured in **decibels (dB)** or **Pascals (Pa)**, while PPV is in **mm/s**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Typical Borehole Loading Pattern (Conceptual Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Top of Borehole - Collar] --> B[Stemming Layer - 25 to 40 times hole diameter]
    B --> C[Main Charge - ANFO or Emulsion]
    C --> D[Booster Charge - Cast Booster or Emulsion Cartridge]
    D --> E[Primer - Detonator inside Booster]
    E --> F[Bottom of Borehole - Toe]
    G[Free Face - Bench] -.->|Burden Distance| F
    H[Detonator Lead Wire / Shock Tube] --> E
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The detonator sits inside the **primer**, which is inside the **booster**, which sits at the **bottom of the borehole**.
- Above the main charge lies the **stemming** — usually drill cuttings, sand, or 10 mm crushed stone.
- The **burden** is the horizontal distance from the borehole to the nearest **free face** (the bench face the rock can break towards).

## 4.2 Blasting Safety Management Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Planning Stage] --> B[Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment]
    B --> C[Statutory Clearances - District Authority, PESO, DGMS]
    C --> D[Trained Blaster and Helpers]
    D --> E[Storage Magazine Approval]
    E --> F[Daily Permit to Blast]
    F --> G[Pre-Blast Warning Siren]
    G --> H[All Personnel Evacuate to Safe Distance]
    H --> I[Blast Fired by Authorized Blaster]
    I --> J[Post-Blast Inspection after Smoke Clears]
    J --> K{Misfire Detected?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Misfire Treatment Procedure - Wait 30 min minimum]
    K -->|No| M[Area Declared Safe - Work Resumes]
    L --> N[Disposal of Misfired Charge by Trained Blaster Only]
    N --> M
```

**Reading the diagram:** The flowchart captures the *end-to-end* safety management of a blast — from initial planning to post-blast clearance. Note the **critical decision point** for misfire handling, which is a frequent KTU exam topic.

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Controls Applied to Blasting Hazards

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Elimination] --> B[Substitution]
    B --> C[Engineering Controls]
    C --> D[Administrative Controls]
    D --> E[PPE]
    A1[Example: Use mechanical rock breaker instead of explosives] --> A
    B1[Example: Use NONEL shock tube instead of electric detonators in stray-current zones] --> B
    C1[Example: Blast mats, proper stemming, delay detonators] --> C
    D1[Example: Permits, training, exclusion zone, sirens] --> D
    E1[Example: Helmet, ear plugs, blast goggles, dust mask] --> E
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the **Hierarchy of Controls** from the most effective (Elimination) to the least effective (PPE), as taught in the **Module 1 → Module 2** continuity of PESFT526. Blasting practice must apply controls in this *top-down* order wherever feasible.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** *Define blasting. List any four common explosives used in construction blasting.* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Blasting is the controlled use of explosive materials to fracture, fragment, or displace rock, soil, or other hard media for the purpose of excavation, quarrying, demolition, trenching, or tunneling in construction projects. It is classified as a high-hazard activity under the **Explosives Rules, 2008** and requires trained blasters, statutory permissions, and engineering controls.

**Four common explosives (1.5 marks — 0.5 each, any four):**
1. **ANFO (Ammonium Nitrate Fuel Oil)** — most widely used blasting agent
2. **Dynamite (Nitroglycerine-based explosive)** — high explosive
3. **Slurry / Emulsion explosives** — water-resistant, used in wet boreholes
4. **Black powder (Gunpowder)** — low explosive, used in soft rock and boulder splitting
5. **Cast boosters** — used to initiate ANFO

*(Any four → 1.5 marks; correct order is not mandatory, but ANFO + emulsion + dynamite + black powder is the ideal combination.)*

---

### **Q2.** *What is fly-rock? State two safety measures to control fly-rock during blasting.* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Fly-rock is the unintended projection of rock fragments beyond the designated blast zone, caused by excessive explosive energy, inadequate burden, poor stemming, or unfavourable geology.

**Two safety measures (2 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Design correct burden and spacing** — Burden should typically be 25 to 40 times the borehole diameter; this ensures the explosive energy fractures the rock without ejecting it violently.
2. **Use adequate stemming length and material** — Stemming length should be at least the burden distance, using 10 mm crushed stone or drill cuttings; this confines the gases in the borehole.
3. *(Optional third)* Cover the blast area with **blast mats** (rubberized conveyor belts or wire mesh) to arrest any rock fragments that escape the borehole.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you must answer **ONE full 14-mark question** from a pair. Two alternatives are given below — students answer **either Question A or Question B**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Blasting Operations and Safety Management*

> *`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`*

#### **(a) Describe the step-by-step procedure for conducting a controlled blast in a construction project. Explain the role of the blaster and the importance of statutory clearances. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Pre-Blast Planning and Statutory Clearances (2 marks):**
- Obtain **statutory clearances** from the District Magistrate / Competent Authority under the **Explosives Act, 1884** and the **Explosives Rules, 2008**.
- Obtain a **license for storage magazine** from the Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organisation (PESO).
- For mining/quarrying, obtain permission from the **Directorate General of Mines Safety (DGMS)**.
- Conduct a **site-specific risk assessment** for fly-rock, vibration, air overpressure, dust, and misfire.
- Prepare a **Method Statement** and **Blast Design** signed by a competent engineer.

**2. Blast Design and Drilling (2 marks):**
- Calculate **burden, spacing, stemming, sub-drill, charge per hole, and charge per delay** using the powder factor and vibration constraints.
- Drill boreholes to the designed **diameter (typically 32–150 mm)** and **depth** as per the bench height.
- Log every borehole for depth, inclination, and any cavities or seams.

**3. Charging and Stemming (1.5 marks):**
- The blaster, assisted by helpers, places the **primer (detonator inside booster)** at the bottom of the borehole, then loads the main charge (e.g., ANFO or emulsion) above it.
- The upper portion of the hole is filled with **stemming material** (10 mm crushed stone or drill cuttings).
- All lead wires / shock tubes are connected in the designed circuit and tested for continuity with a **blasting galvanometer**.

**4. Firing Sequence and Post-Blast (1.5 marks):**
- Issue a **permit-to-blast** for the day, sound **warning sirens** (typically 3 long sirens before blast, 1 short after clearance).
- All personnel and public evacuated to a **safe distance** (typically ≥ 300 m for open-pit blasting).
- The blaster fires the blast using a **blasting machine (exploder)** from a **shelter** with line-of-sight to the blast area.
- After smoke clears (≥ 5 minutes) and a mandatory wait of at least 30 minutes, the blaster inspects the blast area for **misfires**, **over-break**, and **toxic fumes**.

**Role of the Blaster (extra credit):**
- The blaster must hold a valid **Blaster's Certificate of Competency** issued by the Chief Controller of Explosives.
- The blaster is the *single* authorized person to handle explosives, connect the firing circuit, and fire the blast.
- The blaster is personally responsible for the **storage, accounting, and safe return** of unused explosives to the magazine.

---

#### **(b) A blast is to be conducted 120 m from a residential building. Using the BIS vibration predictor with $K = 750$ and $n = 1.6$, calculate the maximum charge per delay to limit peak particle velocity to 5 mm/s. State two engineering controls for fly-rock control. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step Numerical):**

**Step 1 — Write the BIS vibration equation.**

$$V = K \cdot \left(\frac{\sqrt{Q}}{D}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values: $V = 5 \, \text{mm/s}$, $K = 750$, $D = 120 \, \text{m}$, $n = 1.6$.**

$$5 = 750 \cdot \left(\frac{\sqrt{Q}}{120}\right)^{1.6}$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the bracket.**

$$\left(\frac{\sqrt{Q}}{120}\right)^{1.6} = \frac{5}{750} = 0.006667$$

**Step 4 — Take both sides to the power $1/1.6$ (≈ 0.625).**

$$\frac{\sqrt{Q}}{120} = (0.006667)^{0.625} \approx 0.01835$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\sqrt{Q}$.**

$$\sqrt{Q} = 120 \times 0.01835 \approx 2.202$$

**Step 6 — Square both sides.**

$$Q = 2.202^{2} \approx 4.85 \, \text{kg}$$

**Final Result (boxed):**

$$\boxed{Q_{max} \approx 4.85 \, \text{kg per delay}}$$

This means the blaster must use **millisecond delay detonators** and ensure **no more than 4.85 kg of charge detonates in a single delay**, so the residential building experiences vibration within safe BIS limits.

**Two Engineering Controls for Fly-Rock (2 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Maintain proper burden-to-charge relationship:** Burden should be 25–40 times the hole diameter; this prevents the explosive energy from venting through the collar of the hole.
2. **Use adequate stemming length and quality material:** Stemming length should be at least equal to the burden; using 10 mm crushed stone (not fines) maximizes confinement and reduces fly-rock by up to 70%.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Explosive Storage, Misfires & Statutory Compliance*

> *`[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]`*

#### **(a) Explain the statutory requirements for the storage of explosives at a construction site as per the Explosives Rules, 2008. List the safety precautions during storage and handling. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Statutory Requirements for Storage Magazine (3 marks):**
- **License from PESO (Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organisation)** is mandatory before any explosive is stored at the site.
- The magazine must be located at a **minimum safe distance** from inhabited buildings, railways, highways, and other magazines — the exact distance depends on the **Net Explosive Quantity (NEQ)** and ranges from 100 m to 800 m as per Schedule II of the Explosives Rules.
- Construction specifications: **double-locked steel door**, **ventilated walls**, **lightning conductor** with earth resistance ≤ 10 ohms, **no metallic pipes** passing through the magazine, and **fire-fighting equipment** (water, sand buckets) kept outside.
- The magazine must display **caution boards** in local language and have a **watch-and-ward** arrangement 24 × 7.
- **Smoking, fire, matches, and mobile phones are strictly prohibited** within 100 m of the magazine.

**2. Safety Precautions during Storage and Handling (3 marks):**
- Store **detonators and explosives in separate magazines** (or in the same magazine only if separated by a brick/stone partition ≥ 30 cm thick — but **best practice is separate magazines**).
- Maintain a **stock register** showing date of receipt, quantity, issue, and balance — countersigned by the blaster.
- Issue explosives to the blaster **only on a daily basis** through a **signed receipt voucher** with quantity, date, and time.
- Return **all unused explosives to the magazine** the same day, before the blaster leaves the site.
- Handle explosives **gently — no throwing, no sliding, no rough handling**.
- Use **non-sparking tools** (wooden or brass) when opening explosive containers.
- **Never use mobile phones, two-way radios, or any electric device** within the magazine area.
- During electrical storms, **stop all operations** and personnel must withdraw to a safe distance.

**3. Inspection and Audit (1 mark):**
- The magazine is inspected **weekly** by the blaster and **monthly** by the safety officer; any leakage, deterioration, or unauthorized access is reported immediately to PESO and statutory authorities.

---

#### **(b) What is a misfire? Explain the standard procedure for handling a misfire. Discuss why a misfire is more dangerous than a normal blast. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition of Misfire (1.5 marks):**
A **misfire** is a blast situation in which an explosive charge, or part of a charge, **fails to detonate** when the firing circuit is activated. Common causes include:
- Cut-off detonator due to improper handling
- Water ingress in the charge (especially with ANFO)
- Discontinuity in the firing circuit (broken wire, poor connection)
- Use of an expired or damaged detonator
- Stray current interfering with electric detonators

**2. Why a Misfire is More Dangerous (1.5 marks):**
- The unexploded charge may detonate **unexpectedly** due to delayed initiation, vibration, or accidental impact during excavation.
- Workers may unknowingly drill, dig, or walk into the unexploded charge, leading to **sudden and catastrophic detonation** — historically, misfires are one of the **top three causes of fatal blasting accidents** worldwide.
- The charge may have been partially damaged, making it **unstable and unpredictable**.

**3. Standard Misfire-Handling Procedure (4 marks — 1 each / step):**

**Step 1 — Evacuate and Cordon:** No one enters the blast area for a **minimum of 30 minutes** after the firing attempt (or as per the site-specific procedure — some authorities mandate up to 2 hours). The area is cordoned off with red flags / barricades.

**Step 2 — Investigation:** Only the **authorized blaster** re-enters the area, locates the misfired hole(s) by careful inspection of the blast pattern and the firing circuit. The blaster checks for tell-tale signs: missing crater, intact stemming, etc.

**Step 3 — Re-firing (Preferred Method):**
- If the **lead wires / shock tubes are intact and accessible**, the blaster connects them to a fresh blasting machine and re-fires the misfired charge from the safe shelter.
- This is the **safest** method and is used wherever possible.

**Step 4 — Recovery by Careful Excavation (Alternative Method):**
- If re-firing is not possible, a new parallel hole is drilled at a **safe distance ≥ 10 × hole diameter** from the misfired hole, and a fresh charge is loaded and fired to break and recover the unexploded material.
- **Water-washing, washing out stemming, or using metal tools to dig out the charge are FORBIDDEN.**

**Step 5 — Disposal:**
- Any recovered unexploded material is **disposed of by detonation** in a pre-designated disposal area, with full safety precautions.
- All steps are **recorded in the misfire register** with date, time, location, recovery method, and signature of the blaster.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse PPV (mm/s) with air overpressure (dB or Pa).** Examiners deduct full marks if you mix the units.
> 2. **In numerical problems, always show substitution of values *before* solving.** A common mistake is to directly state the answer without showing the working — this costs 1 to 2 marks.
> 3. **In misfire questions, mention the "30-minute mandatory wait" —** this is the most-asked numerical/safety threshold in past papers.
> 4. **Always cite the correct statute** — *Explosives Rules, 2008* (not the older 1983 rules). Examiners check the year.
> 5. **Stemming is *inert* material, not sand or water alone.** Examiners expect the word **"10 mm crushed stone or drill cuttings"**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this twice before the exam):**

- [x] **Blasting** = controlled use of explosives to fracture rock / soil for excavation.
- [x] **Key statute in India:** Explosives Act, 1884 + Explosives Rules, 2008.
- [x] **Regulator:** Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organisation (PESO) + DGMS.
- [x] **Most common explosive in construction:** ANFO (Ammonium Nitrate + Fuel Oil, 94:6).
- [x] **ANFO limitation:** not water-resistant; needs emulsion in wet holes.
- [x] **Detonator types:** Electric, Non-Electric (NONEL/shock tube), Electronic — NONEL is safest in stray-current environments.
- [x] **BIS vibration equation:** $V = K \cdot (Q^{1/2}/D)^{n}$ with $n = 1.6$ and $K = 300$ to $1000$.
- [x] **Safe PPV for residential structures:** 5 mm/s (some references cite 10 mm/s for industrial structures).
- [x] **Scaled distance formula:** $SD = D/\sqrt{Q}$ — typical safe value $SD = 12 \, m/kg^{1/2}$ for residences.
- [x] **Burden rule:** Burden = 25 to 40 × hole diameter.
- [x] **Spacing rule:** Spacing = 1.0 to 1.5 × burden.
- [x] **Stemming rule:** Stemming length ≥ burden (or ≥ 12 × hole diameter).
- [x] **Misfire mandatory wait time:** minimum 30 minutes before re-entry by the blaster.
- [x] **Misfire recovery:** Re-fire if wires intact, OR drill a new parallel hole at ≥ 10 × hole diameter offset.
- [x] **Forbidden recovery methods:** metal tools, water-washing, pulling lead wires.
- [x] **Hazard hierarchy (top to bottom):** Fly-rock, ground vibration, air overpressure, dust/fumes, misfire, fire.
- [x] **Hierarchy of controls applied to blasting:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- [x] **PPE for blasters and crew:** helmet with chin strap, ear defenders (≥ 30 dB attenuation), blast-resistant goggles, dust mask (P2/P3), high-visibility vest, safety boots, leather gloves.
- [x] **Authorization:** Only a Blaster with a valid **Certificate of Competency** from the Chief Controller of Explosives can fire a blast.
- [x] **Charge per delay** controls vibration; **total charge per blast** controls noise and fumes.
- [x] **Public notice** is mandatory for blasting within 500 m of inhabited areas (typical KTU-mandated figure).
- [x] **Siren protocol:** 3 long sirens before blast, 1 short after the area is declared safe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to remember the 5 main blast hazards:**
> **"F-V-A-D-M"** = **F**ly-rock, **V**ibration, **A**ir overpressure, **D**ust/Fumes, **M**isfire.
> If you can list these in order during the viva, the examiner will move on to the next question impressed.

---
*End of Module 2 — Blasting Notes | PESFT526 | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working in Confined Space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT526 - SAFETY IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Working in Confined Space

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Confined Space** is defined (as per **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.146** and **Indian Standard IS 10290:1982**) as a space that is **large enough and configured** so that an employee can bodily enter and perform work; has **limited or restricted means for entry or exit**; and is **not designed for continuous employee occupancy**.

### Three Mandatory Criteria (All Three Must Be Met)

| # | Criterion | Technical Interpretation |
|---|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Bodily Entry** | The space must be large enough for a worker to enter fully |
| 2 | **Limited Entry/Exit** | The opening is small (typically < 24 inches / 60 cm diameter) or access is awkward |
| 3 | **Not for Continuous Occupancy** | Lack of natural ventilation, designed only for occasional entry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permit-Required Confined Space (PRCS)**: A confined space that contains or has the potential to contain a **hazardous atmosphere**; contains a material that has the potential for **engulfing** an entrant; has an **internal configuration** such that an entrant could be trapped or asphyxiated by inwardly converging walls or by a floor which slopes downward and tapers to a smaller cross-section; or contains any other recognized serious **safety or health hazard**.

## 1.2 Common Engineering Examples in Construction

- **Underground** – Sewers, manholes, utility tunnels, sump pits, valve chambers
- **Vertical** – Wells, caissons, deep excavations, silos, chimney stacks
- **Horizontal** – Culverts, storm water drains, ducting, cable trenches
- **Enclosed Structures** – Boiler shells, storage tanks, pressure vessels, elevator shafts, cooling tower cells
- **Building Interiors** – Lift wells, ceiling voids, basement areas, HVAC plenums

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Squeezed Cave"** 
> Imagine you are crawling into a small cave to retrieve a dropped bag. The cave opening is just big enough to squeeze through, you cannot stand up, fresh air barely circulates, and there is a faint smell of something stagnant. The moment you enter, your heart rate increases — not just from effort, but from an instinctive sense of danger. That is exactly what working in a confined space feels like. The danger is **invisible**: it could be oxygen depletion, toxic gas accumulation (e.g., H₂S, CH₄, CO), or a sudden engulfment by water or sand. The walls do not "look" dangerous — that is why it is one of the leading causes of multiple-fatality accidents in construction.

## 1.4 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU Exams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Confined space accidents account for nearly **60% of multiple-fatality construction incidents** globally. KTU examiners frequently test this topic because it tests a student's ability to integrate **atmospheric science, OSHA regulations, permit systems, and rescue protocol** — making it a perfect **CO3 (Safety Management Systems)** and **CO4 (Hazard Identification)** evaluation question.

## 1.5 Physical / Regulatory Standards to Remember

- **Minimum Oxygen (O₂) content for safe entry:** **19.5% to 23.5%** (by volume)
- **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) threshold for evacuation:** **< 10%** of LEL
- **Carbon Monoxide (CO) Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL):** **50 ppm** (8-hour TWA)
- **Hydrogen Sulphide (H₂S) PEL:** **10 ppm** (ceiling limit)
- **Permit validity duration:** Maximum **one shift (8 hours)**
- **Minimum opening size for emergency rescue:** **24 inches (610 mm)** diameter equivalent
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Confined Space Hazard Model

A confined space hazard is rarely singular — it operates as a **multi-hazard matrix** governed by atmospheric, physical, and psychological stressors. The KTU-recommended analysis framework is the **A-T-M Model** (Atmospheric, Toxic, Mechanical).

### 2.1.1 Atmospheric Hazards (The Invisible Killers)

These are responsible for **~90% of confined space fatalities**.

1. **Oxygen Deficiency (O₂ < 19.5%)**
   - Caused by: Displacement (N₂, CO₂ from decomposition), consumption (rusting, biological action, fermentation), or chemical reactions (oxidation).
   - Below **16%**: Symptoms of hypoxia — impaired vision, breathing difficulty.
   - Below **10%**: Loss of consciousness, risk of death.
   - Below **6%**: Fatal within minutes.

2. **Oxygen Enrichment (O₂ > 23.5%)**
   - Greatly increases fire and explosion risk (lower ignition energy).

3. **Toxic Atmospheres (Flammable / Irritant / Asphyxiant)**
   - **H₂S** (Hydrogen Sulphide) – rotten egg smell; olfactory fatigue occurs rapidly above 100 ppm.
   - **CO** (Carbon Monoxide) – colourless, odourless; product of incomplete combustion.
   - **CH₄** (Methane) – explosive at 5%–15% volume in air.
   - **CO₂** (Carbon Dioxide) – asphyxiant; PEL 5000 ppm (8-hr TWA).

4. **Flammable / Explosive Atmospheres**
   - Governed by the **LEL–UEL Range** (Lower and Upper Explosive Limits).

### 2.1.2 Physical & Mechanical Hazards

- **Engulfment** by liquids (water in manholes), granular solids (sand, grain), or free-flowing solids.
- **Entrapment** by converging walls, rotating machinery, mixers, agitators.
- **Mechanical hazards** – agitators, fans, conveyors (must be **LOTO**-isolated).
- **Electrical** – energised cables, junction boxes.
- **Falls** – vertical entry > 1.8 m (6 ft).
- **Temperature extremes** – heat stress or hypothermia.
- **Noise amplification** – sound reverberates in enclosed spaces.

### 2.1.3 Psychological / Physiological Hazards

- **Claustrophobia**, panic attacks.
- **Heat stress** due to PPE and limited air movement.
- **Fatigue** from awkward postures.

## 2.2 The Permit-to-Work (PTW) System — The Heart of Confined Space Safety

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Confined Space Entry Permit** is a **written authorization** that documents the hazards, precautions, atmospheric readings, rescue arrangements, and the names of authorized personnel. It is a **legally binding document** and must be valid for **one work shift only**.

### 2.2.1 Standard Permit Workflow (12 Sequential Steps)

1. **Identify** the confined space and confirm PRCS classification.
2. **Pre-entry risk assessment (JSA / JHA)** by a competent person.
