# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question, Module 2]**
**"List any six safety precautions to be observed during underwater concreting by tremie method."**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. The tremie pipe must be **watertight** and of diameter **at least 200 mm** (8 inches) to allow free flow of concrete.
2. The bottom of the tremie pipe must be **kept embedded 1.5 to 3 m** into freshly placed concrete at all times to prevent water ingress.
3. Concrete mix must be **highly workable** with **slump 150–200 mm** and **rich in cement (≥ 400 kg/m³)** to resist washout.
4. A **go-devil (plug or pig)** must be placed inside the pipe before the first charge to separate water from concrete.
5. The pipe must be **continuously supplied with concrete**; interruptions cause "**honey-combing**" and cold joints.
6. A **diver or ROV** must verify that the pipe outlet is always buried and that no **scour or segregation** occurs at the discharge point.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**"What is Decompression Sickness? Mention two preventive measures."**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decompression Sickness (DCS), popularly called "The Bends,"** is a medical condition occurring when a diver or caisson worker ascends too rapidly from a high-pressure environment. Dissolved nitrogen in blood and tissues comes out of solution as bubbles, blocking blood vessels and causing joint pain, paralysis, or death.

**Preventive Measures (any two for full marks):**

1. **Staged Decompression:** Ascend in pre-calculated pressure steps (e.g., 3.0 → 2.0 → 1.6 → 1.3 → 1.0 atm) with timed halts per the **US Navy Dive Tables**.
2. **Controlled Ascent Rate:** Never ascend faster than **9 m/min** in the first 30 m, and **3 m/min** thereafter.
3. **Pre- and Post-Dive Health Check:** Avoid alcohol, heavy meals, and strenuous exercise 12 hours before/after the dive; stay well-hydrated.
4. **Availability of Hyperbaric Recompression Chamber (HRT):** On-site for emergency recompression within 5 minutes.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **one full question** (either Q.A OR Q.B).

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 2]**

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]:** *"With a neat sketch, explain the construction procedure and safety measures adopted in a **Pneumatic Caisson** for sinking a well foundation to a depth of 25 m below water level."*

**Model Solution (a):**

**Definition & Principle (1 mark):**
A pneumatic caisson is an open-top watertight chamber (the "working chamber") at the bottom of the caisson well into which compressed air is pumped at a pressure equal to the hydrostatic head of water outside. This keeps the chamber dry for excavation while workers dig out the soil beneath the **cutting edge**, allowing the caisson to sink under its own weight.

**Construction Procedure (4 marks):**
1. **Casting the Well Curb & Cutting Edge:** A steel cutting shoe (15–20 mm thick) is fixed at the lowest RCC ring to facilitate soil penetration.
2. **Initial Sinking by Open Well Method:** Sink the caisson from the water surface up to a depth of ~5–6 m using open dredging.
3. **Sealing Top with Air Lock:** Fit a **manned air lock** (for personnel) and a **material air lock** (for muck buckets) on the top deck.
4. **Pressurizing Working Chamber:** Pump compressed air to gauge pressure = (depth × ρg) − atmospheric, so water cannot enter.
5. **Excavation by Diver / Worker:** Soil is removed by grabs or manual labour; muck is hoisted via the material lock.
6. **Adding Curb Rings:** As caisson sinks, additional RCC rings are cast on top, increasing weight.
7. **Sealing the Bottom Plug:** Upon reaching founding strata, the working chamber is filled with **tremie concrete** to form a permanent seal.

**Safety Measures (2 marks):**
- Maximum working pressure limited to **3.4 atm absolute** (≈ 24 m of water) to avoid oxygen toxicity.
- Mandatory **medical lock** adjacent for treating decompression sickness.
- **Standby compressor** with auto-start within 30 seconds of main failure.
- Two **independent pressure gauges** with audio-visual alarms.
- Communication system (audio + signal rope) between chamber and surface.
- **Strict adherence to decompression tables**; no worker allowed more than two shifts of 4 hours each per 24 h.

**Diagram:** Refer to the **Mermaid block diagram of pneumatic caisson** in Section 4.2 — reproduce as a labelled line drawing in the answer script.

---

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]:** *"A rectangular cofferdam of plan dimensions **10 m × 6 m** is to be built in **sea water** (ρ = 1025 kg/m³) to allow dewatering for a foundation. The depth of water inside the cofferdam is to be **5 m**. Calculate:*
*(i) Total hydrostatic thrust on one of the 10 m long sides,*
*(ii) Position of resultant force from the base,*
*(iii) Minimum thickness of tremie concrete seal (unit weight 24 kN/m³) at the base to resist uplift, assuming the water table inside is drawn down to 1 m above base during inspection."*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- Plan: 10 m × 6 m → Choose the 10 m side for analysis.
- Water depth inside (after dewatering) = 5 m
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Concrete $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³
- Water unit weight $\gamma_w = 9.81 \times 1025 / 1000 = 10.055$ kN/m³

**(i) Total Hydrostatic Thrust on 10 m side (2 marks):**

Pressure at base = $\gamma_w \cdot h = 10.055 \times 5 = 50.275$ kN/m²
Thrust per metre run = $\frac{1}{2} \cdot \gamma_w \cdot h^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 10.055 \times 25 = 125.69$ kN/m

Total thrust on 10 m side:
$$F = 125.69 \times 10 = 1256.9 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Position of Resultant from Base (2 marks):**
For triangular pressure distribution, centroid is at $h/3$ from base:
$$\bar{y} = \frac{5}{3} = 1.667 \text{ m from base}$$

**(iii) Minimum Tremie Concrete Thickness for Uplift (3 marks):**
During inspection, water inside is 1 m above base, so the **uplift pressure** at the base of tremie plug is:
$$p_{uplift} = \gamma_w \cdot h_{outside} - \gamma_w \cdot h_{inside} = 10.055 \times (5 - 1) = 40.22 \text{ kN/m²}$$

Downward pressure from concrete slab (per m², thickness $t$):
$$p_{conc} = \gamma_c \cdot t = 24 t \text{ kN/m²}$$

For equilibrium with Factor of Safety = 1.0 (minimum, board often uses 1.1 to 1.25):

$$24 t \geq 40.22$$

$$t \geq 1.676 \text{ m}$$

> **Provide a minimum thickness of 1.7 m**, and **1.9 m** if a Factor of Safety of 1.1 is desired.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]:** *"Discuss the various types of **cofferdams** used in underwater construction. Compare their suitability with respect to depth of water, soil type, and risk of flooding."*

**Model Solution (a) — Tabular Comparison (7 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Type of Cofferdam | Suitable Depth | Soil Type | Flood Risk | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Earthen / Embankment Cofferdam** | 0–3 m | Impervious (clay) | Low | Cheapest; not for flowing water |
| 2 | **Single Sheet Pile Cofferdam** | 3–6 m | Stiff clay, soft rock | Moderate | Braced with wales & struts |
| 3 | **Double Sheet Pile Cellular Cofferdam** | 6–18 m | Any except rock | Low–Moderate | Self-supporting circular cells |
| 4 | **Diaphragm / Secant Pile Wall Cofferdam** | Up to 30 m | Alluvial, granular | Very Low | Modern; used for metro shafts |
| 5 | **Rockfill Cofferdam** | 0–4 m | Rocky riverbed | Low | Used for permanent weirs |
| 6 | **Concrete Caisson Cofferdam** | 15–35 m | Deep soft strata | Very Low | Forms part of permanent work |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Selection depends on **depth, soil, water velocity, available time, and cost**. For depths <6 m, single sheet pile is most economical; for deep urban sites, diaphragm walls are safest; for deep foundations in soft alluvium, pneumatic caissons remain the gold standard.

---

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]:** *"A diver working at **20 m depth** in fresh water uses a **surface-demand scuba system**. The air consumption rate is **40 L/min at the surface**. Calculate:*
*(i) The actual air consumption rate at 20 m depth,*
*(ii) The volume of a 12-litre cylinder at 200 bar pressure, and*
*(iii) The safe working duration at depth, allowing a 50-bar reserve for emergency ascent."*

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Air Consumption at 20 m (Boyle's Law) (2 marks):**
Absolute pressure at 20 m = 1 atm + (20/10) atm = 3 atm
Air consumption scales linearly with absolute pressure (assuming constant tidal volume):

$$\dot{V}_{20m} = \dot{V}_{surface} \times \frac{P_{abs}}{P_{atm}} = 40 \times 3 = 120 \text{ L/min}$$

**(ii) Total Air in Cylinder (2 marks):**
Volume at atmospheric pressure = Cylinder volume × Gauge pressure = 12 L × 200 bar

$$V_{total} = 2400 \text{ L at 1 atm}$$

**(iii) Safe Working Duration (3 marks):**
Usable air = Total − Reserve = 2400 − (12 × 50) = 2400 − 600 = 1800 L at surface equivalent
At 20 m, this volume lasts:

$$t = \frac{1800}{120} = 15 \text{ minutes}$$

> **Answer: 15 minutes** safe working time at 20 m. Always plan for **2 minutes reserve** even after reaching the surface for the diver to remove gear and communicate.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on Under-Water Works (KTU 2024 Board Report Analysis)**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert gauge pressure to absolute pressure** when applying Boyle's Law to divers — leads to 1–2 mark error in every Part B numerical.
> 2. **Drawing the wrong shape of the pressure diagram** (rectangle vs triangle) for vertical vs inclined walls. The KTU examiner instantly deducts 2 marks for an incorrect or unlabelled pressure prism.
> 3. **Skipping the "go-devil" detail** in tremie concrete answers. Examiners *specifically* test this in 2-mark sub-questions.
> 4. **Confusing "caisson" with "cofferdam."** A **caisson becomes part of the permanent structure**; a **cofferdam is removed** after construction. Many students lose 1 mark by not stating this distinction explicitly.
> 5. **No mention of medical/hyperbaric facilities** in safety questions. KTU mandates at least one line on this for full marks in 7-mark safety questions.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **MUST-KNOW FACTS (Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam)**

- [ ] **Hydrostatic Pressure:** $P = \rho g h$; doubles every 10 m of depth (approx.).
- [ ] **Buoyancy:** $F_b = \rho g V$; submerged unit weight $\gamma_{sub} = \gamma_{sat} - 9.81$ kN/m³.
- [ ] **Boyle's Law:** $P_1V_1 = P_2V_2$ — governs lung expansion and air supply duration.
- [ ] **Henry's Law:** Gas solubility ∝ partial pressure — root cause of "the bends."
- [ ] **Tremie Pipe Embedment:** Must be **1.5 to 3 m** into fresh concrete at all times.
- [ ] **Tremie Concrete Slump:** 150–200 mm; cement content ≥ **400 kg/m³**.
- [ ] **Maximum safe caisson working pressure:** 3.4 atm abs (≈ 24 m depth).
- [ ] **Ascent rate:** ≤ 9 m/min in upper 30 m; ≤ 3 m/min thereafter.
- [ ] **Decompression Sickness Treatment:** 100% Oxygen + Recompression Chamber.
- [ ] **Types of Cofferdams:** Earthen, Single Sheet Pile, Double Cellular, Diaphragm, Rockfill.
- [ ] **Types of Caissons:** Open, Box, Pneumatic, Monolithic.
- [ ] **Key Standards:** IS 4138 (Diving), IS 9556 (Caisson), OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart Y.
- [ ] **Blow-out risk:** Sudden loss of air pressure → water rushes up → caisson "explodes" upward.
- [ ] **Go-devil:** A plug placed inside the tremie pipe to keep water out until concrete arrives.
- [ ] **Factor of Safety for Cofferdam Stability:** ≥ 1.5 for overturning, ≥ 1.25 for sliding.
- [ ] **Piping / Sand Boil:** Granular soil failure under upward seepage; prevented by **Terzaghi's critical hydraulic gradient** $i_c = (G_s - 1)/(1 + e)$.
- [ ] **Tidal Range Impact:** Every metre of tide change alters pressure by 0.1 atm — monitor continuously.
- [ ] **Diver Bottom Time:** Recorded from "feet wet" to "feet dry" — never exceed table values.
- [ ] **Standby Diver:** MUST be present on the surface, fully geared, whenever the working diver is in the water — non-negotiable safety rule.
- [ ] **Communication:** Hard-wired audio + signal rope + red international diver-down flag.
- [ ] **Emergency Response Plan:** Rescue boat, first-aid station, recompression chamber within 30 minutes of every dive site.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety in Demolition of Buildings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT526 - SAFETY IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Safety in Demolition of Buildings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Demolition (KTU 2024 Definition):** Demolition is the deliberate, systematic, and controlled dismantling, razing, or wrecking of a building, structure, or any part thereof using pre-planned methods, sequence, and safety protocols to ensure the protection of human life, adjoining property, and the environment.

**Safety in Demolition** refers to the comprehensive set of engineering controls, regulatory compliance measures, supervisory protocols, and worker protection systems applied before, during, and after a demolition activity to prevent accidents such as:
- Uncontrolled structural collapse
- Falling debris striking workers or bystanders
- Exposure to hazardous materials (asbestos, lead, silica)
- Utility strikes (electrocution, gas explosions)
- Noise-induced hearing loss and dust-related respiratory illnesses

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Perspective)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Reversing the Recipe of a Cake":** Building a structure is like baking a multi-layer cake — you lay the foundation (bottom) first and build up. Demolition is the reverse: you must carefully remove the top, then the middle, and finally the foundation. But if you just stab the cake with a fork from any direction, it collapses unpredictably. A controlled demolition is like a surgeon carefully removing layers one at a time, with the patient (neighboring structures) and the surgical team (workers) fully protected.

Think of demolition as performing **"surgery in reverse"** — precision, planning, and protection are not optional; they are the only path to success.

### 1.3 Why Demolition Safety is a KTU High-Priority Topic

| **Statistical Reality (Global Construction Safety Data)** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| Demolition workers' fatality rate vs. general construction | **4× higher** |
| Leading cause of demolition deaths | **Falls from height (~35%)** |
| Second leading cause | **Struck-by falling objects (~25%)** |
| Electrocution share | **~12%** |
| Average dust concentration (unsafe demolition) | **>10 mg/m³ (TLV: 3 mg/m³)** |
| Permissible noise exposure (8-hr TWA, OSHA) | **85 dB(A)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 emphasizes the *planning* and *procedural* aspects of demolition safety — students must be able to **list, sequence, and justify** safety controls at every stage, not merely recall PPE types.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pre-Demolition Survey — The Foundation of Safe Demolition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Theorem:** *"No demolition shall commence without a comprehensive pre-demolition survey, structural assessment, and documented method statement."* — This is a **2-mark guaranteed question** in any KTU Module 2 exam.

A pre-demolition survey is a **systematic, documented investigation** of the structure to identify:
1. **Construction materials** present (RCC, steel, masonry, timber)
2. **Structural load paths** and load-bearing members
3. **Hazardous substances** (asbestos, lead paint, PCBs, chemical residues)
4. **Adjacent structures** and their proximity
5. **Underground and overhead utilities** (gas, water, electricity, telecom)
6. **Previous use** of the building (industrial, hospital, laboratory)
7. **Existing damage, cracks, or weakening**

### 2.2 Demolition Methods — Classification Logic

Demolition methods are chosen based on:
- **Structure type** (RCC frame, load-bearing masonry, steel, composite)
- **Height** (low-rise ≤ 15 m, mid-rise 15–30 m, high-rise > 30 m)
- **Location** (urban dense, open site, near critical infrastructure)
- **Hazardous material presence**
- **Time and budget constraints**

| **Method** | **Best Suited For** | **Risk Level** |
|---|---|---|
| **Manual (Hand Demolition)** | Low-rise masonry, interior strip-out, areas near live utilities | **High to workers, low to surroundings** |
| **Mechanical (Excavator/breaker, ball & crane, pusher arm)** | Mid-rise, isolated buildings | **Medium** |
| **Top-Down (Floor-by-floor deconstruction)** | High-rise urban buildings | **Medium to Low** |
| **Implosion (Controlled explosive)** | Large isolated structures (chimneys, silos) | **Very High control, low time exposure** |
| **Selective / Deconstruction (salvage-oriented)** | Heritage buildings, material recovery | **Low to medium** |
| **Hydraulic / Chemical (non-explosive cracking agents)** | Concrete slabs, rock | **Low** |
| **Thermal / Cutting (diamond saw, water jet, plasma)** | Precise removal in tight spaces | **Low** |

### 2.3 The 5-Stage Demolition Safety Framework

#### **Stage 1 — Planning & Permits (Pre-Work)**
- Engineering survey by a **Competent Person (Structural Engineer)**
- Method Statement & Risk Assessment (MSRA) preparation
- Utility isolation confirmation (LOTO — Lockout/Tagout)
- Permits to Work (PTW): Hot Work, Confined Space, Working at Height
- Notification to local authority, neighbors, emergency services
- Public protection plan (hoardings, pedestrian detours)

#### **Stage 2 — Site Establishment & Isolation**
- Install **perimeter hoarding (≥ 2.4 m height)** with warning signs
- Establish **exclusion zone** based on debris throw calculations
- Provision of **first-aid station, fire-fighting equipment, eyewash**
- Welfare facilities (toilets, drinking water, rest area)
- Designated **drop zones** for debris (chutes, hoppers, skip bins)
- **Site supervisor and safety officer** assigned

#### **Stage 3 — Hazard-Specific Controls**
- **Asbestos**: Licensed removal contractor; Type-3 enclosure; negative-pressure units
- **Lead paint**: Wet methods; HEPA vacuums; blood lead monitoring
- **Silica dust**: Wet cutting; local exhaust ventilation (LEV)
- **Noise**: Engineering controls (silencers, acoustic screens); PPE (ear muffs ≥ NRR 25 dB)
- **Working at heights**: Scaffolds (Tag inspected daily), catch platforms, safety nets
- **Falling objects**: Debris netting, toe boards, restricted access below

#### **Stage 4 — Sequence of Dismantling**
The **KTU 2024 mandated sequence** (general rule):
1. Disconnect all utilities (electricity, gas, water, telecom)
2. Remove hazardous materials (asbestos, chemicals, biological)
3. Strip non-structural elements (doors, windows, partitions, fixtures)
4. Remove roofing, cladding, services (HVAC, electrical, plumbing)
5. Dismantle floor by floor from top-down
6. Remove floor slabs and beams
7. Finally, dismantle columns and foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reverse-Order Rule:** Demolition must generally proceed in the **reverse order of construction**. Roof → Upper floors → Lower floors → Columns → Foundation.

#### **Stage 5 — Post-Demolition**
- Debris segregation and disposal (tested for hazardous content)
- Site clearance and ground levelling
- Final structural assessment of adjacent buildings
- Documentation closure and lessons learned report

### 2.4 Structural Stability & Member Removal Rules

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL RULE:** A load-bearing member shall **NEVER** be removed until all loads it supports have been safely transferred to temporary supports or alternative load paths.

**Load Redistribution Logic:**
- When a column is removed, beams spanning to it transfer load to adjacent columns → these adjacent columns may become **overloaded** → temporary shoring required.
- When a wall is removed, floors and roof above may deflect → **shoring props and needle beams** needed.

### 2.5 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) Matrix

| **PPE Item** | **Standard Reference** | **Mandatory When** |
|---|---|---|
| Hard hat (Type II, Class E) | IS 2925 / ANSI Z89.1 | Always on site |
| Safety boots (steel toe, puncture-resistant) | IS 15298 | Always on site |
| High-visibility vest (Class 2 or 3) | IS 15802 | Always on site |
| Safety harness + lanyard | IS 3521 / EN 361 | Working at height > 1.8 m (India) or > 2 m (OSHA) |
| Respiratory protection (N95 / FFP2 minimum) | IS 14746 | Dust, asbestos, silica exposure |
| Ear protection (muffs or plugs) | IS 6229 | Noise > 85 dB(A) |
| Cut-resistant gloves | EN 388 | Glass, metal, sharp edges |
| Eye protection (safety glasses + goggles) | IS 5983 | Cutting, grinding, chemical work |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Formulas Relevant to Demolition Safety Calculations**

| **Formula / Quantity** | **Expression** | **Variables & Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Exclusion zone radius (manual) | $R = H \times 1.5$ | $R$ = radius (m), $H$ = structure height (m) | Minimum distance for personnel |
| Debris fall time | $t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{g}}$ | $t$ = time (s), $h$ = height (m), $g$ = 9.81 m/s² | Estimating worker escape time |
| Kinetic energy of falling debris | $KE = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | $m$ = mass (kg), $v$ = velocity (m/s) | Catch platform design |
| Noise exposure (TWA) | $TWA = 16.61 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{100}\right) + 90$ | $D$ = dose (%) | OSHA noise compliance |
| Wind speed cancellation limit | $V_{cancel} > 40$ km/h | $V$ = sustained wind | Crane/ball operations stop |
| Shoring load per prop | $P = \dfrac{W_{total}}{n \times FS}$ | $W$ = total load (N), $n$ = number of props, $FS$ = factor of safety (≥ 2.5) | Temporary support design |
| Dust suppression water rate | $Q = A \times r$ | $Q$ = water flow (L/min), $A$ = activity area (m²), $r$ = 1–2 L/min·m² | Misting system sizing |
| Stop work limit (wind for crane ball) | $v \geq 30$ km/h sustained | — | Mechanical demolition halt |

### 2.7 Engineering Real-World Utility

These formulas and procedures are used by:
- **Site Safety Engineers** for daily method statements
- **Structural Engineers** for temporary works design
- **Project Managers** for permit-to-work issuance
- **Insurance and Loss Adjusters** for incident investigation
- **HSE Auditors** for compliance verification

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Exclusion Zone Radius for a Demolition Site

This is a classic KTU 14-mark question scenario. Let us derive the minimum safe exclusion zone for a building undergoing mechanical demolition.

**Given:**
- Building height: $H = 30$ m
- Debris throw multiplier for mechanical (breaker) demolition: $K = 1.5$
- Additional safety buffer for wind/secondary fragments: $B = 5$ m
- Adjacent property line distance: must be verified

**Step 1 — Define Exclusion Zone**

The exclusion zone $R_{excl}$ is the minimum distance beyond which the probability of being struck by demolition debris is statistically negligible.

**Step 2 — Apply the Standard Empirical Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{excl} &= (K \times H) + B \\
R_{excl} &= (1.5 \times 30) + 5 \\
R_{excl} &= 45 + 5 \\
R_{excl} &= 50 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the Result**
- Personnel must remain at least **50 m** from the active demolition face.
- The hoarding must be at the **property boundary**, but the active work zone is **50 m**.
- Adjacent buildings within 50 m require **protective screening (catch fans or scaffold debris netting rated to Class B per EN 1263)**.

**Step 4 — Validation Check**
- For top-down manual demolition: $K = 1.0$ → $R_{excl} = 35$ m (acceptable for tighter urban sites)
- For implosion: $R_{excl} = 1.5H + 100$ → $R_{excl} = 145$ m (much larger controlled zone)

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Debris Fall Time and Worker Escape Window

**Scenario:** A concrete block (mass $m = 25$ kg) dislodges from a floor at height $h = 12$ m.

**Given Constants:**
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Air resistance neglected for short fall

**Step 1 — Time to reach ground**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \tfrac{1}{2} g t^{2} \\
t &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{g}} \\
t &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 12}{9.81}} \\
t &= \sqrt{\dfrac{24}{9.81}} \\
t &= \sqrt{2.4465} \\
t &= 1.564 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Velocity at impact**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= g \times t \\
v &= 9.81 \times 1.564 \\
v &= 15.34 \text{ m/s} \approx 55.2 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Kinetic energy of the falling block**

$$
\begin{aligned}
KE &= \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \\
KE &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 25 \times (15.34)^{2} \\
KE &= 0.5 \times 25 \times 235.32 \\
KE &= 2941.5 \text{ J} \approx 2.94 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation**
- The block hits the ground at ~55 km/h, carrying **~2.94 kJ** of kinetic energy.
- A human skull fracture threshold is **~7–10 J** at the point of impact → the block is **lethally dangerous** even after a 12 m fall.
- A worker has only **1.56 seconds** to react — hence the rule: **no one below an active demolition face, ever.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** A 25 kg object falling 12 m produces energy sufficient to cause a fatal head injury. This is why exclusion zones, debris chutes, and overhead protection (catch platforms) are non-negotiable.

### 3.3 Risk Assessment Matrix (Analytical Tool for KTU)

The **5×5 Risk Matrix** is a standard KTU 14-mark question component.

| **Severity ↓ \\ Likelihood →** | **Rare (1)** | **Unlikely (2)** | **Possible (3)** | **Likely (4)** | **Almost Certain (5)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Catastrophic (5)** | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | **25** |
| **Major (4)** | 4 | 8 | 12 | **16** | 20 |
| **Moderate (3)** | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 |
| **Minor (2)** | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| **Negligible (1)** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

**Score Interpretation (KTU Standard):**
- 1–4: **Low risk** → routine precautions
- 5–9: **Medium risk** → specific controls + supervision
- 10–15: **High risk** → senior management approval + detailed method statement
- 16–25: **Very high / Intolerable** → work must **not proceed** until risk is reduced

**Example KTU Application:**
- Hazard: "Worker struck by falling brick during manual demolition"
- Severity: Major (4) — could be fatal
- Likelihood: Possible (3) — common on demolition sites without overhead protection
- **Risk score: 12 → HIGH** → must install catch fans, debris nets, restrict access below

### 3.4 Hazard Identification — Demolition-Specific

| **Hazard** | **Consequence** | **Control Measure** |
|---|---|---|
| Premature collapse | Multiple fatalities | Engineered sequence, temporary shoring, competent person supervision |
| Asbestos exposure | Mesothelioma (cancer) | Licensed removalist, Type-3 enclosure, medical surveillance |
| Electrical contact | Electrocution, cardiac arrest | LOTO, cable identification, minimum 3 m clearance from overhead lines |
| Gas line rupture | Explosion, fire | Pre-survey, hand-dig within 0.5 m of known lines, gas detection |
| Falls from height | Fatal trauma | Edge protection, harness, scaffold, catch nets |
| Falling tools/materials | Head injury, death | Tool lanyards, debris nets, exclusion zone, hard hats |
| Dust inhalation | Silicosis, lung disease | Wet cutting, LEV, respirators (P2/P3) |
| Noise | Permanent hearing loss | Silenced equipment, rotation, ear protection |
| Manual handling | Musculoskeletal injury | Mechanical aids, training, lifting limits |
| Plant/vehicle collision | Crushing injury | Banksman, segregation, reversing alarms, marked routes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Demolition Decision Flowchart — Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Pre-Demolition Survey] --> B{Structure Type?}
    B --> C[Masonry Load-Bearing]
    B --> D[RCC Frame]
    B --> E[Steel Frame]
    B --> F[Composite / Special]
    C --> G{Height <= 15 m?}
    G -->|Yes| H1[Manual Demolition]
    G -->|No| H2[Mechanical - Pusher Arm]
    D --> I{Location Urban Dense?}
    I -->|Yes| J1[Top-Down Deconstruction]
    I -->|No| J2[Mechanical - Breaker]
    E --> K{Hazardous Material Present?}
    K -->|Yes| L1[Strip Hazardous First]
    K -->|No| L2[Flame Cutting + Crane]
    L1 --> L3[Then Mechanical]
    F --> M[Engineer-Specific Method]
    H1 --> N[Apply 5-Stage Safety Framework]
    H2 --> N
    J1 --> N
    J2 --> N
    L2 --> N
    L3 --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Execute with PPE + MSRA + PTW]
    O --> P[Post-Demolition Clearance]
```

### 4.2 Demolition Sequence — Top-Down Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 1: Utilities Isolation LOTO] --> S2[Stage 2: Hazardous Material Removal]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Non-Structural Strip-Out]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Roofing and Cladding Removal]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Top Floor Dismantling]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6: Floor-by-Floor Deconstruction]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7: Column and Foundation Removal]
    S7 --> S8[Stage 8: Site Clearance and Documentation]
    S1 -.-> R1[Permit to Work]
    S2 -.-> R2[Licensed Contractor]
    S3 -.-> R3[Catch Platforms Active]
    S4 -.-> R4[Edge Protection]
    S5 -.-> R5[Exclusion Zone Enforced]
    S6 -.-> R6[Daily Method Statement Review]
    S7 -.-> R7[Engineer Sign-Off]
    S8 -.-> R8[Lessons Learned Report]
```

### 4.3 Exclusion Zone and Site Layout Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PERIMETER[PERIMETER HOARDING - MIN 2.4 M]
        direction TB
        A1[Warning Signs + Lighting] --- A2[Site Office]
        A2 --- A3[First Aid Station]
        A3 --- A4[Tool Store]
    end
    subgraph EXCL[EXCLUSION ZONE - R EXCL METERS]
        direction TB
        B1[Active Demolition Face] --- B2[Debris Chute]
        B2 --- B3[Catch Fan / Net]
        B3 --- B4[Water Spray Misting]
    end
    subgraph SVC[SUPPORT ZONE]
        direction TB
        C1[Skip Bins] --- C2[Plant Parking]
        C2 --- C3[Generator / Compressor]
    end
    PERIMETER --> EXCL --> SVC
```

### 4.4 PPE Donning Logic for a Demolition Worker

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Worker Arrives on Site] --> B[Site Induction Complete]
    B --> C[Hard Hat + Hi-Vis Vest + Safety Boots]
    C --> D{Working at Height?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Add Harness + Lanyard]
    D -->|No| F{Dust / Noise Hazard?}
    E --> F
    F -->|Yes| G[Add Respiratory + Ear Protection]
    F -->|No| H[Task-Specific Gloves and Eye Protection]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Pre-Use Inspection of All PPE]
    I --> J[Permit-to-Work Verified]
    J --> K[Commence Task]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Define demolition. List any four safety hazards associated with the demolition of buildings.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Demolition is the systematic dismantling or razing of a building or structure using controlled techniques, with the primary objective of ensuring safety of workers, public, and adjacent property.
- **Four safety hazards (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**
  1. Uncontrolled structural collapse
  2. Falls from height
  3. Falling objects striking workers
  4. Exposure to hazardous materials (asbestos, lead, silica)
  5. Electrocution from live cables
  6. Dust and noise exposure

*(Any four — half mark each)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**
**What is a pre-demolition survey? Mention any four points that are checked during such a survey.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A pre-demolition survey is a systematic, documented inspection of a structure prior to demolition to identify construction type, structural condition, hazardous materials, utilities, and surrounding risks, in order to plan a safe method of work.
- **Four points checked (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**
  1. Type of construction (RCC, steel, masonry)
  2. Presence of hazardous substances (asbestos, lead, chemicals)
  3. Condition of structural members (cracks, corrosion, prior damage)
  4. Location of underground and overhead utilities
  5. Proximity and condition of adjacent structures
  6. Previous use of the building

*(Any four — half mark each)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has an internal choice. Both choices carry equal marks. Sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Explain in detail the sequential procedure for safe demolition of an RCC framed multi-storey building. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Pre-Demolition Survey and Engineering Assessment (1.5 marks)**
- Conduct detailed structural survey by a competent structural engineer.
- Identify load-bearing elements, column-beam-grid, slab spans, and previous modifications.
- Identify presence of hazardous materials (asbestos, lead paint, chemicals).
- Verify utilities mapping (electrical, gas, water, telecom, drainage).

**Step 2 — Documentation and Permits (1 mark)**
- Prepare Method Statement & Risk Assessment (MSRA).
- Obtain Permit to Work (PTW) for hot work, working at height, confined space.
- Notify statutory authorities, neighbors, and emergency services.

**Step 3 — Site Establishment (1 mark)**
- Erect perimeter hoarding (≥ 2.4 m), warning signs, site lighting.
- Establish welfare facilities: first-aid, drinking water, toilets, rest shelter.
- Designate and barricade exclusion zone using the formula $R = 1.5H + B$.

**Step 4 — Utilities Isolation (0.5 mark)**
- Apply Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) to all electrical panels, gas valves, and water mains.
- Confirm isolation by testing with calibrated instruments.

**Step 5 — Hazardous Material Removal (0.5 mark)**
- Engage licensed asbestos removal contractor for ACM (Asbestos-Containing Material).
- Wet-strip lead paint; HEPA vacuum; medical surveillance of workers.

**Step 6 — Top-Down Demolition Sequence (1.5 marks)**
- Remove non-structural elements (doors, windows, partitions, fixtures).
- Strip services (HVAC ducts, electrical conduits, plumbing).
- Remove roofing slab and cladding.
- Demolish floor by floor from the top using mechanical breakers or manual tools.
- Provide temporary shoring wherever a load-bearing column is temporarily removed.

**Step 7 — Foundation and Column Removal (0.5 mark)**
- Remove columns and footings only after all superstructure loads are relieved.
- Backfill and compact foundation pits.

**Step 8 — Post-Demolition (0.5 mark)**
- Debris segregation (concrete, steel, hazardous).
- Site clearance, levelling, environmental testing.
- Document lessons learned and close all permits.

---

**(b) Discuss the various methods of demolition with their suitability and risk profile. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Manual Demolition (Hand Demolition) (1.5 marks)**
- *Technique:* Workers use hand tools (sledgehammers, breakers, cutting torches) to dismantle the structure piece by piece from top to bottom.
- *Suitability:* Low-rise masonry, interiors, areas near live utilities where mechanical vibration is unacceptable.
- *Risk:* High to individual workers (falls, struck-by, manual handling). Low to surrounding structures.
- *Safety controls:* Scaffolds, catch platforms, harnesses, exclusion zones below, trained operatives.

**2. Mechanical Demolition (1.5 marks)**
- *Technique:* Hydraulic excavators fitted with breakers, crushers, shears, or pulverizers; long-reach arms for taller structures; crane-and-wrecking ball for isolated buildings.
- *Suitability:* Mid-rise RCC and steel structures in open or semi-open sites.
- *Risk:* Medium — operator cabs are protected (FOPS/ROPS), but the high reach arm can fail or drop debris; exclusion zones are critical.
- *Safety controls:* Wind speed monitoring (stop ≥ 30 km/h), exclusion zone, trained operator, banksman, daily machine inspection.

**3. Top-Down (Floor-by-Floor) Demolition (1.5 marks)**
- *Technique:* Mini-excavators placed on top floors progressively demolish each floor and lower themselves via temporary platforms.
- *Suitability:* High-rise urban buildings where perimeter space is limited.
- *Risk:* Low to medium — controlled, sequential, and largely weather-protected.
- *Safety controls:* Floor load verification, perimeter screens, debris removal via internal hoist, fall arrest on exposed edges.

**4. Implosion (Explosive Demolition) (1.5 marks)**
- *Technique:* Carefully placed shaped charges weaken key columns so that gravity pulls the structure down into its own footprint.
- *Suitability:* Large isolated structures (chimneys, silos, stadiums) with adequate clear zone (≥ 1.5H + 100 m).
- *Risk:* Very high if miscalculated; entire operation runs in seconds but pre-event risk is significant.
- *Safety controls:* Specialist licensed blaster, evacuation of zone, structural pre-weakening plan, dust and vibration monitoring, full perimeter isolation.

**5. Selective Demolition / Deconstruction (1 mark)**
- *Technique:* Careful dismantling to recover reusable materials (timber, bricks, steel, fixtures).
- *Suitability:* Heritage structures, sustainability-focused projects, material-recovery targets.
- *Risk:* Low — but labor-intensive and time-consuming.
- *Safety controls:* Skilled tradespeople, careful sequencing, dust and noise management.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Explain the Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) requirements during demolition. List at least six items with their applicable standard. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **PPE in Demolition — Six+ Mandatory Items:**

**1. Head Protection — Hard Hat (Type II, Class E) (1 mark)**
- *Standard:* IS 2925 / ANSI Z89.1
- *Purpose:* Protects against falling tools, debris, and impact from low-hanging structural members. Class E rated for electrical contact up to 20 kV.

**2. Foot Protection — Safety Boots (1 mark)**
- *Standard:* IS 15298 / EN ISO 20345 S3
- *Features:* Steel toe cap (200 J impact), steel midsole (puncture resistance), anti-slip sole.
- *Purpose:* Protects against falling bricks, sharp rebar, nails.

**3. Fall Protection — Full-Body Harness with Shock-Absorbing Lanyard (1 mark)**
- *Standard:* IS 3521 / EN 361 / EN 355
- *Trigger:* Mandatory above 1.8 m (India Factories Act) or 2 m (OSHA).
- *Purpose:* Arrests a fall before the worker strikes the ground or a lower level.

**4. Respiratory Protection (1 mark)**
- *Standard:* IS 14746 / EN 149 (FFP2/FFP3) for dust; half/full-face respirator with P100/HEPA for asbestos.
- *Purpose:* Prevents inhalation of silica, asbestos fibers, and toxic fumes from cutting operations.

**5. Hearing Protection (1 mark)**
- *Standard:* IS 6229 / EN 352
- *Threshold:* Mandatory when noise > 85 dB(A) TWA.
- *Options:* Ear muffs (higher NRR, faster donning) or ear plugs (more comfortable in heat).

**6. Eye and Face Protection (1 mark)**
- *Standard:* IS 5983 / EN 166
- *Options:* Safety glasses (general), goggles (dust/chemical splash), face shield (grinding/cutting).
- *Purpose:* Protects against flying particles, sparks, UV from welding.

**7. Hand Protection — Cut-Resistant Gloves (1 mark)**
- *Standard:* EN 388 (cut score ≥ 3)
- *Purpose:* Protects against sharp edges of cut metal, broken glass, jagged concrete.

**Conclusion:** PPE is the **last line of defense** — engineering controls (scaffolds, nets, LEV) must always come first per the **Hierarchy of Controls**.

---

**(b) Discuss the safety precautions to be observed when demolishing a building adjacent to an occupied structure and a public road. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Engineering Survey and Adjacent Property Assessment (1.5 marks)**
- Survey adjoining structures for pre-existing cracks, settlement, or instability.
- Document pre-demolition condition with photographs and crack-monitoring gauges.
- Establish a baseline to defend against future damage claims.

**2. Protective Works Installation (1.5 marks)**
- Erect a **scaffold with full debris netting / catch fan** between the demolition building and the adjacent structure.
- The scaffold should be designed for impact load: minimum 75 kg/m² debris load per BS 5975 / OSHA 1926.452.
- Install **dust screens (fire-retardant fabric)** to prevent particulate drift.

**3. Pedestrian and Traffic Management (1.5 marks)**
- Hoard the entire site boundary with solid panels (≥ 2.4 m height) on the road side.
- Provide covered walkway (gantry) for pedestrians if footpath is partially obstructed.
- Coordinate with traffic police for lane closure, signage, diversions, and night-time reflective markings.
- Deploy trained flagmen/banksmen at all vehicle entry/exit points.

**4. Vibration and Noise Control (1 mark)**
- Limit mechanical demolition methods that generate high vibration (use hand tools or low-impact saws near occupied buildings).
- Monitor peak particle velocity (PPV) — limit to **< 5 mm/s** for fragile masonry, **< 25 mm/s** for robust RCC per DIN 4150.
- Acoustic barriers to limit noise to **< 75 dB(A) at site boundary** during day, < 65 dB(A) at night.

**5. Utilities Isolation Verification (0.5 mark)**
- Hand-dig within 0.5 m of any known utility to confirm position.
- Use cable/pipe locators and ground-penetrating radar (GPR) for unknown services.

**6. Emergency Preparedness (0.5 mark)**
- Pre-arrange evacuation route and assembly point for the adjacent occupants.
- First-aid trained personnel and fire extinguishers (Class A, B, C) on site.
- 24-hour site security to prevent trespassing.

**7. Continuous Monitoring (0.5 mark)**
- Daily crack inspection of adjacent building.
- Vibration and dust monitoring with real-time alarms.
- Stop-work triggers documented and enforced.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Writing generic safety text** (e.g., "use PPE, train workers") without specifying **standard codes, types, or trigger values** → loses 1–2 marks per question.
> 2. **Forgetting the exclusion zone calculation** in any demolition question — it is a **must-include** for 7+ mark answers.
> 3. **Confusing the demolition sequence** (e.g., saying "start from foundation") → this is an **automatic 1-mark deduction** plus conceptual zero.
> 4. **Skipping the pre-demolition survey** in any answer — it carries standalone weight in KTU marking.
> 5. **Not distinguishing manual vs. mechanical vs. implosion** in method-of-demolition answers — examiners award marks for differentiation, not listing.
> 6. **Omitting the Hierarchy of Controls** in PPE-related answers — shows shallow understanding.
> 7. **Ignoring hazardous materials** (asbestos, lead) — a frequent KTU question stem; omitting it forfeits 1–2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition (1-liner):** Demolition is the controlled dismantling of a structure; safety ensures protection of life, property, and environment.
- **Golden rule:** Demolition proceeds in the **reverse order of construction** (top-down).
- **Pre-demolition survey** is **mandatory** and must be done by a **competent person (structural engineer)**.
- **LOTO** (Lockout/Tagout) of all utilities **before** any structural work begins.
- **Hazardous material removal** (asbestos, lead, silica) is a **prerequisite** and must be done by **licensed contractors** with proper enclosures.
- **Exclusion zone formula:** $R = K \cdot H + B$, with $K = 1.5$ for mechanical, $K = 1.0$ for manual, $B$ = 5 m buffer.
- **Critical fall height trigger:** Working at height > 1.8 m (India Factories Act) requires fall protection.
- **Noise limit:** > 85 dB(A) TWA mandates hearing protection.
- **PPE is the last line of defense** — engineering controls (scaffolds, nets, LEV) come first per the **Hierarchy of Controls**.
- **Reverse-construction rule** applies to **member removal** as well — never remove a load-bearing member before relieving its load.
- **Vibration limits** for adjacent structures: PPV < 5 mm/s for fragile, < 25 mm/s for robust.
- **Key Indian standards to memorize for KTU:** IS 2925 (hard hat), IS 3521 (harness), IS 14746 (respirator), IS 6229 (hearing), IS 5983 (eye).
- **Stop-work conditions:** Wind > 30 km/h sustained, lightning within 8 km, PPV exceeding limit, fatigue hours exceeded, unsafe scaffold tag.
- **Documentation trio:** MSRA (Method Statement & Risk Assessment) + PTW (Permit to Work) + JSA/JHA (Job Safety Analysis / Hazard Analysis).
- **Five-Stage Framework:** Planning & Permits → Site Establishment → Hazard-Specific Controls → Sequence of Dismantling → Post-Demolition Closure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarization with Relevant Indian Standards and the National Building Code Provisions on Construction Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT526 - SAFETY IN CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization with Relevant Indian Standards and the National Building Code Provisions on Construction Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Familiarization with Indian Standards & NBC Provisions"?

In the context of KTU's **Safety in Construction** syllabus, this topic refers to the structured study of the **regulatory and codal framework** that governs occupational safety, structural safety, and constructional practices in India. It is *not* a single formula or design calculation — it is the **legal-cum-engineering compass** that every construction professional in India must follow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The Indian regulatory safety framework comprises three interlocking pillars:
> 1. **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** — the national standards body that publishes **IS Codes** (Indian Standards).
> 2. **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016)** — a *comprehensive* model code providing guidelines for building construction, administered by the **BIS** on behalf of the Government of India.
> 3. **Statutory Regulations** — Acts and Rules such as the **Building & Other Construction Workers (Regulation of Employment and Conditions of Service) Act, 1996**, the **Factories Act, 1948**, and the **Indian Electricity Rules**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Driving Licence" of Construction

Think of the regulatory framework like the rules that govern driving a car on Indian roads:

| Driving Analogy | Construction Safety Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Motor Vehicles Act** (the parent law) | **BOCW Act, 1996** & **Factories Act, 1948** |
| **Traffic Rules Handbook** | **National Building Code (NBC) 2016** |
| **Specific vehicle manuals** (e.g., for trucks, buses) | **IS Codes** (e.g., IS 3696 for scaffolds, IS 4130 for demolition) |
| **RTO enforcement** | **State PWD, Municipal Bodies, Inspector of Factories** |
| **Your driving licence** | **Engineer's professional responsibility** |

Just as a driver must know the *rules of the road* and the *specifics of their vehicle*, a construction engineer in India must be familiar with the **NBC** (the broad rulebook) and the **relevant IS Codes** (the specific technical guidelines for each activity).

### 1.2 Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) — The Custodian of IS Codes

The **Bureau of Indian Standards**, established under the **BIS Act, 2016** (replacing the original BIS Act, 1986), is the **National Standards Body of India**. It is headquartered in **New Delhi** and operates as a statutory body.

> [!NOTE]
> **BIS = India's ISO (International Organization for Standardization).**
> Where ISO publishes international standards (e.g., ISO 45001 for Occupational Health & Safety), BIS publishes **Indian Standards (IS)** with the prefix **"IS"** followed by the standard number and year of publication (e.g., **IS 3696 (Part 1) : 1987**).

**Functions of BIS relevant to construction safety:**
- Formulation of Indian Standards (IS) for materials, products, processes, and **safety codes of practice**.
- Certification through the **ISI Mark** (now called the **Standard Mark**) — mandatory for certain materials.
- **Hallmarking** of gold and silver.
- Acting as the custodian of the **National Building Code (NBC)**.

### 1.3 National Building Code of India (NBC) — The "Constitution of Construction"

The **National Building Code of India (NBC)** is a **comprehensive building code** that provides guidelines for regulating building construction activities across the country. The **current version is NBC 2016**, published by the **BIS**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NBC 2016 is a *model code* — it is NOT a federal law.**
> State governments and local municipal bodies *adopt* its provisions into their **building bylaws**, after which they become legally enforceable in that jurisdiction. For example, Kerala's **Kerala Municipal Building Rules (KMBR)** and **Kerala Panchayat Building Rules (KPBR)** are aligned with NBC.

**The NBC 2016 is organized into 12 Parts:**

| Part | Title | Relevance to Safety |
|---|---|---|
| Part 1 | Definitions | Terminology baseline |
| Part 2 | Administration | Statutory framework |
| Part 3 | Development Control Rules & General Building Requirements | Setbacks, heights, fire access |
| Part 4 | Fire and Life Safety | **Critical** — fire exits, alarms, sprinklers |
| Part 5 | Building Materials | Quality & safety of materials |
| Part 6 | Structural Design | Loads, foundations, seismic design |
| **Part 7** | **Constructional Practices and Safety** | **⭐ THE MOST IMPORTANT PART FOR THIS MODULE** |
| Part 8 | Building Services | Electrical, HVAC, lifts |
| Part 9 | Plumbing Services | Water supply, drainage |
| Part 10 | Landscape Development | Site safety, vegetation |
| Part 11 | Approach to Sustainability | Green construction |
| Part 12 | Asset and Facility Management | Maintenance safety |

### 1.4 Scope of This Module

For Module 2, the focus is on:
1. **NBC 2016, Part 7 — Constructional Practices and Safety** (the core).
2. **Key Indian Standards (IS codes)** that deal with specific construction activities (scaffolding, demolition, blasting, structural steel erection, etc.).
3. **Statutory acts** that provide the legal teeth to these technical codes.

This module forms the **"regulatory foundation"** upon which all practical safety measures (PPE, fall protection, hazard control) covered in other modules are legally anchored.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Summary Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Hierarchy of Safety Regulation in India

Indian construction safety regulation operates on **three hierarchical tiers**, each with increasing specificity and enforceability.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Tiered Framework (from broad to specific):**
> 1. **Tier 1 — Acts of Parliament** (Legislative authority)
> 2. **Tier 2 — National Building Code (NBC)** (Comprehensive technical code)
> 3. **Tier 3 — Indian Standards (IS Codes)** (Activity-specific technical specifications)

### Tier 1: Acts of Parliament (Statutory Authority)

These are the **primary laws** passed by the Indian Parliament. They carry full legal force.

| Act | Year | Key Provisions for Safety |
|---|---|---|
| **Factories Act** | **1948** | Safety of workers in factories; Section 6 (fencing of machinery), Section 21 (safety of building & machinery), Section 38 (fire protection) |
| **Building & Other Construction Workers (Regulation of Employment and Conditions of Service) Act (BOCW)** | **1996** | Registration of establishments, safety measures, welfare, hours of work, overtime |
| **BOCW Welfare Cess Act** | **1996** | Levy of cess (1% of construction cost) to fund worker welfare |
| **Contract Labour (Regulation & Abolition) Act** | **1970** | Registration of contractors, safety obligations of principal employer |
| **Indian Electricity Act** | **2003** | Electrical safety on construction sites |
| **Workmen's Compensation Act** | **1923** | Compensation for injury/death at work |
| **Public Liability Insurance Act** | **1991** | Mandatory insurance for hazardous industries |
| **Environment Protection Act** | **1986** | Environmental safety in construction |
| **Explosives Act** | **1884** | Storage and handling of explosives for blasting |
| **Minimum Wages Act** | **1948** | Welfare-linked safety considerations |
| **ESI Act** | **1948** | Employee State Insurance — medical benefit for injury |

### Tier 2: National Building Code of India (NBC 2016) — Part 7 Deep Dive

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NBC 2016, Part 7 — Constructional Practices and Safety** is divided into **6 sub-parts (Sections)**. Every KTU student must know these.

| Section | Title | Coverage |
|---|---|---|
| **7.1** | **General** | Scope, terminology, general safety obligations of the **Employer**, **Constructor**, and **Designer** |
| **7.2** | **Temporary Construction, Staging, Formwork & Scaffolding** | Design loads, inspection, dismantling, working platforms |
| **7.3** | **Construction Hazards & Their Prevention** | Falls, falling objects, electrical hazards, fire, mechanical equipment, confined spaces, hot work, dust & noise |
| **7.4** | **Stability of Structure During Construction** | Partial loading, bracing, sequencing, demolition stability |
| **7.5** | **Demolition** | Survey, sequence, protective measures, debris management |
| **7.6** | **Safety in Special Operations** | Blasting, piling, tunneling, work over water, work in compressed air, work at heights |

### Tier 3: Key Indian Standards (IS Codes) for Construction Safety

These are **activity-specific** codes. The table below is the **KTU high-yield cheat sheet** — these are the IS codes most frequently asked in KTU examinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to remember: "S-B-D-S-S" for the Big 5 Scaffold & Structure Codes:**
> - **S**caffolding
> - **B**lasting
> - **D**emolition
> - **S**teel erection
> - **S**pecial operations

| IS Code | Title | Key Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **IS 3696 (Part 1) : 1987** | Safety code for **scaffolds and ladders** | Material, design load (min. 600 N/m² on working platform), guard rails, ladder angles |
| **IS 3696 (Part 2) : 1991** | Safety code for scaffolds and ladders — **Ladders** | Wooden, metal ladders; inspection |
| **IS 4014 (Part 1 & 2) : 1967** | Code of practice for **steel tubular scaffolding** | Tube dimensions, couplers, ties |
| **IS 4081 : 1986** | Safety code for **blasting and related drilling operations** | Storage of explosives, blasting patterns, misfire handling |
| **IS 4130 : 1991** | Safety code for **demolition of buildings** | Pre-demolition survey, sequence (top-down), exclusion zone |
| **IS 4756 : 1978** | Safety code for **tunneling work** | Ventilation, gas testing, support, haulage |
| **IS 4912 : 1978** | Safety code for **piling and other deep foundation work** | Pile driver operations, cofferdams |
| **IS 5121 : 1969** | Safety code for **piling and other deep foundations** | Adjacent structure safety |
| **IS 7069 : 1973** | Safety code for **handling and storage of building materials** | Stacking heights, hazardous materials |
| **IS 7205 : 1974** | Safety code for **erection of structural steel work** | Lifting, alignment, bolting, welding at height |
| **IS 7293 : 1974** | Safety code for **working with construction machinery** | Hoists, cranes, excavators, conveyors |
| **IS 7969 : 1975** | Safety code for **handling and storage of dangerous goods** | Acids, gas cylinders, flammable liquids |
| **IS 8989 : 1978** | Safety code for **erection of concrete framed structures** | Formwork, shoring, pre-cast handling |
| **IS 13415 : 1992** | Safety code for **protective barriers in and around buildings** | Guard rails, toe boards, catch platforms |
| **IS 13416 (Parts 1–5) : 1992** | **Recommended preventive measures** for safety in construction (an *excellent* code) |
| **IS 13430 : 1992** | Code of practice for **safety during additional construction & alteration** | Live-structure work |
| **IS 14746 : 1999** | Safety code for **construction of roads** | Traffic management, work zone safety |
| **IS 15867 : 2008** | Construction project management — **Guidelines** | Project safety planning |
| **IS 15984 : 2013** | **Hazardous area classification** | Explosive atmospheres on site |
| **IS 16134 : 2014** | **Confined spaces** | Entry procedures, atmospheric testing |

### 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of NBC 2016 Part 7 — Section 7.3 (Construction Hazards)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Section 7.3 of NBC Part 7 is a *favourite KTU question*. The major hazards classified here are:

1. **Falls from height** — the **#1 cause of construction fatalities** in India.
   - Protection: guard rails, toe boards, safety nets, personal fall arrest systems (PFAS), **anchor strengths ≥ 22.2 kN (5000 lbf)** per worker.
2. **Falling objects** — materials, tools from above.
   - Protection: toe boards (≥ 100 mm high), overhead protection, barricades, hard hats.
3. **Electrical hazards** — overhead lines, cables, temporary wiring.
   - Protection: **min. clearance of 3 m** from 11 kV lines, lockout-tagout, ELCB/RCD.
4. **Fire hazards** — hot work, flammable liquids, temporary wiring.
   - Protection: fire extinguishers (Class A, B, C), fire watchers, hot work permits.
5. **Mechanical equipment** — cranes, hoists, excavators, conveyors.
   - Protection: trained operators, daily inspection, load charts, signal persons.
6. **Confined spaces** — manholes, trenches, pits, tanks.
   - Protection: permit-required confined space program, gas testing, attendant, rescue plan.
7. **Hot work** — welding, cutting, grinding.
   - Protection: hot work permits, fire watch for **at least 30 minutes** post-work.
8. **Dust, fumes, and noise** — silica dust, lead, asbestos, cement, noise-induced hearing loss.
   - Protection: wet cutting, ventilation, dust masks (FFP2/FFP3), ear protection (> 85 dB).
9. **Excavation and trenching** — cave-ins.
   - Protection: shoring, sloping (max 45° in Type C soil), benching, **daily inspection by competent person**.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of the NBC/IS Framework

**Why does a B.Tech student need to know this?**

1. **Statutory compliance** — Every KTU civil engineering graduate may become a *Builder*, *Engineer-in-charge*, or *Project Manager*. The **BOCW Act 1996** imposes **personal legal liability** on the employer and the in-charge engineer.
2. **Tender specifications** — Government PWD, CPWD, and KMC tenders *reference* specific IS codes (e.g., "All scaffolding shall conform to IS 3696 Part 1"). Non-compliance leads to disqualification or legal action.
3. **Insurance claims** — Insurance companies deny claims for injuries if the relevant IS code was not followed.
4. **Professional ethics** — The Institution of Engineers (India) and the National Building Code both bind engineers to *public safety first*.
5. **International practice alignment** — IS codes are increasingly **harmonized with ISO** and **EN standards**, making the Indian engineer globally competitive.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Application Matrix

## 3.1 The "Code-Compliance Walkthrough" — A Structured Procedure

This section provides an **analytical, step-by-step framework** that an engineer should follow to ensure a construction activity is *fully compliant* with the Indian regulatory framework. We will apply this to **three typical scenarios** that are KTU-favourites: **Scaffolding, Demolition, and Blasting**.

### SCENARIO A — Scaffolding Compliance Walkthrough

**Step 1: Identify the applicable IS codes.**
- **Primary:** IS 3696 (Part 1) : 1987 — Safety code for scaffolds and ladders.
- **Secondary:** IS 4014 (Part 1 & 2) : 1967 — Code of practice for steel tubular scaffolding.
- **NBC reference:** Part 7, Section 7.2 — Temporary Construction, Staging, Formwork & Scaffolding.

**Step 2: Determine the design loading class.**
IS 3696 classifies working platforms into load classes:

$$\text{Class} \; 1 \; = \; 0.75 \; \text{kN/m}^2 \; \text{(light duty, inspection)}$$

$$\text{Class} \; 2 \; = \; 1.50 \; \text{kN/m}^2 \; \text{(light maintenance)}$$

$$\text{Class} \; 3 \; = \; 2.00 \; \text{kN/m}^2 \; \text{(brickwork, plastering)}$$

$$\text{Class} \; 4 \; = \; 3.00 \; \text{kN/m}^2 \; \text{(heavy masonry, stone work)}$$

$$\text{Class} \; 5 \; = \; 4.50 \; \text{kN/m}^2 \; \text{(concrete work, storage of materials)}$$

$$\text{Class} \; 6 \; = \; 6.00 \; \text{kN/m}^2 \; \text{(very heavy duty, special operations)}$$

**Step 3: Verify structural design.**
- Standards require that the **scaffold structure** be designed by a *competent person* (a defined legal role under BOCW Rules).
- The **maximum platform height** for a *non-tied* scaffold is limited per IS 3696.
- **Foot ties** are required at vertical intervals of **every 4 bays** (typically every 8 m).
- **Guard rails** must be at **900 mm to 1000 mm** height with an **intermediate rail at ~450 mm**.
- **Toe boards** must be at least **100 mm high**.

**Step 4: Inspection regime.**
- Inspection by a competent person **at least once every 7 days**.
- Inspection **after any modification, exposure to bad weather, or after any incident**.
- Records maintained in a **Scaffold Inspection Register**.

**Step 5: Worker access and PPE.**
- **Ladders** must conform to IS 3696 Part 2 — slope **4:1 to 6:1** (75° to 65° from horizontal).
- Workers must wear **full body harness** with lanyard anchored to an independent point.
- **Hard hats, safety shoes, and high-visibility vests** are mandatory.

**Step 6: Documentation.**
- Scaff-tag system (Green = Safe, Yellow = Restricted, Red = Do Not Use).
- Method statement, risk assessment, and competency certificates for the erector.

### SCENARIO B — Demolition Compliance Walkthrough

**Step 1: Identify the applicable IS codes.**
- **Primary:** IS 4130 : 1991 — Safety code for demolition of buildings.
- **NBC reference:** Part 7, Section 7.5 — Demolition.

**Step 2: Conduct a pre-demolition survey.**
- **Structural survey** by a competent person to identify load-bearing elements, hazardous materials (asbestos, lead, chemicals), and adjacent structures.
- Document the **method of demolition**: manual, mechanical (excavator, breaker), implosion, or by hand tools.

**Step 3: Establish the exclusion zone.**
- An **exclusion zone** must be established **equal to at least 1.5 times the height of the structure** being demolished.
- The zone is barricaded and posted with **warning signs**.

**Step 4: Sequencing — the cardinal rule of demolition.**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 4130 cardinal rule:** *Demolition proceeds in the **reverse order** of construction — **top-down, storey-by-storey**.*
> - Roof services → Roof slab → Top floor columns → Top floor beams → Top floor slabs → ... → Ground floor.

**Step 5: Hazard-specific control measures.**

| Hazard | Control Measure (per IS 4130) |
|---|---|
| Falling debris | Catch platforms, debris nets, water sprays for dust suppression |
| Dust | Continuous water spray, wet demolition |
| Noise | Time restrictions, ear protection, silent breakers where possible |
| Falling from height | Independent scaffolds (NOT the structure being demolished), harnesses |
| Uncontrolled collapse | Pre-weakening prohibited; sequence strictly observed |
| Hazardous materials | Asbestos survey, licensed removal contractor |

**Step 6: Continuous monitoring.**
- A **competent person** must be present throughout the operation.
- **Daily safety briefing** to all workers.
- **Emergency response plan** must be in place (rescue, medical, fire).

### SCENARIO C — Blasting Compliance Walkthrough

**Step 1: Identify the applicable codes.**
- **Primary:** IS 4081 : 1986 — Safety code for blasting and related drilling operations.
- **Statutory:** **Explosives Act, 1884** and **Explosives Rules, 2008**.

**Step 2: Licensing and personnel.**
- **Blaster's certificate of competency** issued by the **Chief Controller of Explosives** is mandatory.
- The blaster must be at least **21 years old** and medically fit.

**Step 3: Storage of explosives.**
- A licensed **magazine** at a safe distance (per Explosives Rules) from any structure.
- Separate storage for **detonators and explosives** (the "two-magazine" rule).

**Step 4: Drilling and charging pattern.**
- Drilling depth, hole diameter, burden, and spacing determined by a *blasting engineer*.
- **Maximum charge per delay** limited to control **Peak Particle Velocity (PPV)** at adjacent structures.
- A common safe limit is **PPV ≤ 10 mm/s** for residential structures.

**Step 5: Firing and misfire handling.**
- **Warning siren** sequence: 3 short → 1 long → fire.
- **All personnel evacuated** to a safe shelter.
- **Misfire handling**: Wait **at least 15 minutes** before investigating; re-fire only by a competent blaster; never drill into a misfired hole.

**Step 6: Post-blast inspection.**
- Check for **unexploded charges** (misfires, fly-rock).
- Document blast details: **date, time, location, holes, charge, results, observations**.

### 3.2 Application Matrix — Cross-Reference of Hazard vs. IS Code

The following matrix is a high-yield KTU summary mapping **each construction hazard** to its **primary IS code, NBC reference, and statutory act**.

| Hazard Category | Primary IS Code | NBC Part 7 Reference | Statutory Act |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scaffolding & Ladders | IS 3696 (P1 & P2) | Sec 7.2 | BOCW Act 1996 |
| Formwork & Staging | IS 3696 / IS 8989 | Sec 7.2 | BOCW Act 1996 |
| Structural Steel Erection | IS 7205 | Sec 7.6 | Factories Act 1948 |
| Concrete Frame Erection | IS 8989 | Sec 7.2 | BOCW Act 1996 |
| Demolition | IS 4130 | Sec 7.5 | BOCW Act 1996 |
| Blasting & Drilling | IS 4081 | Sec 7.6 | Explosives Act 1884 |
| Piling & Deep Foundations | IS 4912 / IS 5121 | Sec 7.6 | Factories Act 1948 |
| Tunneling | IS 4756 | Sec 7.6 | Factories Act 1948 |
| Excavation & Trenching | (NBC provisions) | Sec 7.3 | BOCW Act 1996 |
| Handling Construction Machinery | IS 7293 | Sec 7.3 | Factories Act 1948 |
| Material Handling & Storage | IS 7069 | Sec 7.3 | Factories Act 1948 |
| Dangerous Goods Storage | IS 7969 | Sec 7.3 | Explosives Act / Public Liability Act |
| Protective Barriers | IS 13415 | Sec 7.3 | BOCW Act 1996 |
| Road Construction | IS 14746 | (Part 10) | Motor Vehicles Act / IRC |
| Confined Spaces | IS 16134 | Sec 7.3 | BOCW Act 1996 |
| Working at Heights | (NBC Sec 7.3) | Sec 7.3 | BOCW Act 1996 |
| Electrical Safety | (NBC Sec 7.3) | Sec 7.3 | Indian Electricity Act 2003 |
| Fire Safety | IS 14435 / NBC | Part 4 | NBC Part 4 / Fire Services Act |
| Hazardous Areas | IS 15984 | Sec 7.3 | Factories Act 1948 |
