# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Crane Load Moment and Tipping Condition

### Problem Statement
A mobile crane with a maximum rated capacity of **50 tonnes** at a **3 m** radius is lifting a load of **W = 28 tonnes** at a working radius of **R = 5 m**. The crane has four outriggers forming a support square of side **6 m**, and the crane's own weight of **40 tonnes** acts at the geometric center. Determine whether the crane will overturn.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the resisting moment (counter-torque).**

The crane's weight creates a stabilizing moment about the tipping edge (the outrigger farthest from the load).

$$ M_{stability} = W_{crane} \times \frac{L_{base}}{2} $$

where $L_{base} = 6$ m is the side of the outrigger square.

$$ M_{stability} = 40 \times \frac{6}{2} = 40 \times 3 = 120\ \text{tonne·m} $$

**Step 2 — Identify the tipping moment.**

$$ M_{load} = W_{load} \times R = 28 \times 5 = 140\ \text{tonne·m} $$

**Step 3 — Apply the stability criterion.**

The crane remains stable if:

$$ M_{load} < M_{stability} $$

$$ 140\ \text{tonne·m} < 120\ \text{tonne·m} \quad \text{(FALSE)} $$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

Since the tipping moment exceeds the stabilizing moment, **the crane will overturn**. The operator must either:
- Reduce the load: $W_{max} = M_{stability} / R = 120 / 5 = 24$ tonnes.
- Reduce the radius by repositioning the load closer to the cab.
- Extend the outriggers (increase $L_{base}$).

**Step 5 — Quantitative Safety Factor.**

$$ \text{Safety Factor (SF)} = \frac{M_{stability}}{M_{load}} = \frac{120}{140} = 0.857 $$

A safety factor of less than **1.0** confirms the lift is unsafe. KTU/IS standards require a minimum SF of **1.5** for normal lifts and **2.0** for lifts over personnel-occupied areas.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sling Angle Effect on Effective SWL

### Problem Statement
A wire-rope sling is rated at **SWL = 5 tonnes** in a vertical (straight) configuration. The sling is rigged at an angle of **θ = 60°** from the vertical (i.e., 30° from the horizontal). Determine the effective SWL and comment on safety.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the sling-angle correction formula.**

$$ SWL_{effective} = SWL_{rated} \times \cos\theta $$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$ SWL_{effective} = 5 \times \cos(60°) $$

$$ SWL_{effective} = 5 \times 0.5 = 2.5\ \text{tonnes} $$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**

The horizontal sling reduces the capacity by **50%**! This is a common cause of rigging failures. Always ensure the sling angle from horizontal is at least **60°** to maintain ≥ 87% of rated capacity.

**Step 4 — Tension in each sling leg (two-leg sling).**

The load is supported by two sling legs. The vertical reaction per leg is:

$$ T_{leg} = \frac{W}{2 \cos\theta} = \frac{W}{2 \times 0.5} = W $$

In this configuration, **each leg carries the full weight** of the load — a critical insight that explains why wide-span slings are preferred.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Body Current and Severity of Electric Shock

### Problem Statement
A construction electrician working with sweaty hands (skin resistance ≈ **1000 Ω**) accidentally contacts a **415 V** three-phase line. Determine:
1. The current through the body.
2. The severity using the **body-current severity chart**.
3. The required PPE rating.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply Ohm's Law.**

$$ I = \frac{V}{R_{body}} = \frac{415\ V}{1000\ \Omega} = 0.415\ A = 415\ mA $$

**Step 2 — Compare with severity thresholds.**

| Current Range | Physiological Effect |
|---|---|
| 0–1 mA | Imperceptible |
| 1–5 mA | Mild tingling |
| 5–15 mA | Painful, sustained muscle contraction |
| 15–50 mA | Severe pain, "let-go" failure |
| 50–100 mA | Respiratory paralysis |
| **100–200 mA** | **Ventricular fibrillation (often fatal)** |
| > 200 mA | Severe burns, cardiac arrest |

A current of **415 mA** falls in the **severe burns / cardiac arrest** category.

**Step 3 — Required PPE.**

The incident energy for a **415 V** system is relatively low, but for the **time duration** of contact (> 1 s), the **HRC (Hazard Risk Category) 2** arc-flash suit is required (rated to **8 cal/cm²**).

**Step 4 — Prevention.**

The correct control measure is a **GFCI (Ground Fault Circuit Interrupter)** that trips at **5 mA** within **25 ms**, which would have prevented this shock entirely. Note that even a fuse/circuit breaker at **30 mA** is too late — by the time it trips, the worker is already in the let-go failure range.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Earth Rod Resistance and Number of Rods Required

### Problem Statement
A construction site has soil resistivity **ρ_s = 200 Ω·m**. A single ground rod of length **L = 3 m** and radius **a = 0.0125 m** is installed. The required earth resistance is **R ≤ 5 Ω** per IS 3043. Determine the number of additional rods required to meet the specification.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate the resistance of a single rod.**

$$ R_{rod} = \frac{\rho_s}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{a}\right) - 1 \right] $$

$$ R_{rod} = \frac{200}{2\pi \times 3} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4 \times 3}{0.0125}\right) - 1 \right] $$

$$ R_{rod} = \frac{200}{18.85} \left[ \ln(960) - 1 \right] $$

$$ R_{rod} = 10.61 \times \left[ 6.867 - 1 \right] $$

$$ R_{rod} = 10.61 \times 5.867 = 62.25\ \Omega $$

**Step 2 — This exceeds 5 Ω; we need multiple rods.**

Using parallel-rod formula:

$$ R_{total} = \frac{R_{rod}}{n \cdot \eta} $$

Assuming a utilization factor **η = 0.75** (rods spaced ≥ rod length apart):

$$ n = \frac{R_{rod}}{R_{total} \cdot \eta} = \frac{62.25}{5 \times 0.75} = \frac{62.25}{3.75} = 16.6 $$

**Step 3 — Round up to the next whole number.**

$$ n = 17\ \text{rods} $$

**Step 4 — Verify with the actual configuration.**

With 17 rods, the actual total resistance is:

$$ R_{total} = \frac{62.25}{17 \times 0.75} = \frac{62.25}{12.75} = 4.88\ \Omega \quad \checkmark $$

This is below **5 Ω**, satisfying **IS 3043:1987**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Step Potential Calculation Near a Faulted Line

### Problem Statement
A **33 kV** transmission line falls to the ground, fault current is **I_ground = 5000 A**, soil resistivity is **ρ_s = 300 Ω·m**, and the worker's stride is **L_step = 1 m**. Determine the step potential at a horizontal distance of **d = 10 m** from the fault point.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the step-potential formula.**

$$ V_{step} = I_{ground} \times \rho_s \times \frac{L_{step}}{2\pi d} $$

$$ V_{step} = 5000 \times 300 \times \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10} $$

$$ V_{step} = 1{,}500{,}000 \times \frac{1}{62.83} $$

$$ V_{step} = 23{,}873\ V $$

**Step 2 — Interpretation.**

A step potential of **~24 kV** is **absolutely fatal** — orders of magnitude above the let-go threshold. The worker must:
- **Keep feet together** to reduce $L_{step}$ to near zero.
- **Hop (do not run)** out of the energized zone.
- **Call the utility** to de-energize the line.

**Step 3 — Safe distance calculation.**

Setting $V_{step} = 50$ V (a safe body-driving voltage):

$$ d_{safe} = \frac{I_{ground} \times \rho_s \times L_{step}}{2\pi \times V_{safe}} = \frac{5000 \times 300 \times 1}{2\pi \times 50} $$

$$ d_{safe} = \frac{1{,}500{,}000}{314.16} = 4774\ m \approx 4.8\ km $$

This extremely large distance shows why **all** personnel must be evacuated from a high-voltage fault zone.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Safety Assessment of Construction Machines

| Parameter | Excavator | Grader | Dozer | Mobile Crane | Tower Crane | Concrete Mixer |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Hazard** | Striking, electrocution | Rollover | Rollover, struck-by | Tipping, falling load | Collapse, collision | Entanglement, electric shock |
| **Operator License** | HMV + Operator Competency | HMV | HMV | Crane Operator License | Specialized | HMV / Plant Operator |
| **Daily Inspection** | Hydraulic, bucket, swing brake | Blade, scarifier, hydraulics | Track, blade, ripper | LMI, A2B, outriggers | Slew, hoist brakes, limit switches | Drum, gearbox, isolator |
| **LMI / Limiter** | Optional | Not required | Not required | **Mandatory** | **Mandatory** | Not required |
| **PPE for Operator** | Hi-vis, hard hat, ear | Same as excavator | Same + dust mask | Same + harness | Same + harness | Same + gloves |
| **Key Indian Standard** | IS 7293 | IS 7293 | IS 7293 | IS 4573 | IS 13674 | IS 1791 |
| **Fatal-Injury Rate (per million hours)** | 0.5 | 0.4 | 0.6 | 0.8 | 1.2 | 0.3 |
| **Most Dangerous Phase** | Slewing near workers | Working on slope | Track maintenance | Lifting at max radius | Climbing/Dismantling | Cleaning the drum |

---

## 3.7 Decision Matrix — Selecting Earthing Method Based on Soil Resistivity

| Soil Type | Typical ρ_s (Ω·m) | Recommended Earthing | Rod Length Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wet organic soil | 10–100 | Single rod, 2–3 m | Short |
| Loam / clay | 100–300 | 2–3 rods, 3 m | Medium |
| Sandy soil | 300–800 | 5–10 rods, 3 m + chemical treatment | Long |
| Rocky / gravel | 800–10,000 | Chemical earthing, bentonite backfill | Specialized |
| Concrete-encased | Variable | Ufer ground (concrete-encased electrode) | Continuous |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic for Electrical Hazard Controls:** **"E-SAFE"**
> - **E**ngineering controls (insulation, GFCI, guarding)
> - **S**ignage and barricading
> - **A**dministrative (LOTO, permits, training)
> - **F**ault-current calculation and PPE selection
> - **E**arthing and bonding verification

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Construction Site Safety Hierarchy — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Construction Site Safety Management System] --> B1[Elimination]
    A0 --> B2[Substitution]
    A0 --> B3[Engineering Controls]
    A0 --> B4[Administrative Controls]
    A0 --> B5[PPE Last Resort]

    B1 --> C1[De-energize circuits before work]
    B1 --> C2[Remove the hazard source]

    B2 --> D1[Battery tools vs mains tools]
    B2 --> D2[Low-voltage hand tools]

    B3 --> E1[Machine guards and interlocks]
    B3 --> E2[GFCI and ELCB protection]
```
